topics.title: QRS Detector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBL607 - BIO INSTRUMENTATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** QRS Detector ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **QRS Detector** is a signal-processing algorithm (or hardware subsystem) designed to identify the **QRS complex** — the most prominent deflection in an Electrocardiogram (ECG) signal corresponding to **ventricular depolarization**. The detector locates the temporal position of the dominant **R-peak** (and consequently the Q and S boundaries) within a continuous stream of noisy ECG samples sampled typically at **f_s = 250 Hz to 500 Hz**. Mathematically, given a discrete-time ECG sequence $x[n]$, the QRS detector produces a binary decision stream $d[n] \in \{0, 1\}$ and a series of fiducial markers $\tau_k$ corresponding to the R-peak instants: $$\tau_k = \{ n \;|\; d[n]=1 \;\text{and}\; x[n] \text{ is a local maximum} \}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEBL607 – Module 2):** > The QRS detector is the *backbone* of nearly every computer-aided ECG analysis pipeline — heart-rate computation, HRV (Heart Rate Variability) analysis, arrhythmia classification, and pacemaker rhythm evaluation all begin from correctly detected R-peaks. ### 1.2 The ECG Signal Anatomy (Recap) | Wave | Origin | Typical Duration | Amplitude Range | |------|--------|------------------|-----------------| | **P-wave** | Atrial depolarization | 60 – 100 ms | 0.1 – 0.25 mV | | **PR segment** | AV nodal conduction | 50 – 120 ms | Isoelectric | | **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | **80 – 120 ms** | **0.5 – 2.0 mV** | | **T-wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 100 – 250 ms | 0.1 – 0.7 mV | The QRS complex is the **largest and sharpest** feature in a normal ECG, occupying an effective spectral band of approximately **5 Hz – 15 Hz** (centered around 10 Hz). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Siren-In-Traffic Problem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy:** > Imagine standing on a busy street trying to hear a specific ambulance siren among honking cars, engine roars, and chatter. Your brain does the following: > 1. **Filters out low-frequency rumble** (engine noise) — like a **high-pass filter**. > 2. **Filters out high-frequency hiss** (wind, hiss of tires) — like a **low-pass filter**. > 3. **Listens for sudden rising and falling pitch** (the *wail*) — like a **differentiator**. > 4. **Amplifies the loudness** (squared) so weak signals become strong — like a **squaring block**. > 5. **Integrates over a short window** to confirm the siren is sustained, not a one-off honk — like a **moving average integrator**. > 6. **Sets an adaptive threshold** that adjusts to the noise floor. > > A QRS detector does **exactly this** on the ECG signal — it hunts for the *siren-like* QRS wail buried in muscle noise, baseline wander, and mains interference. --- ### 1.4 Why QRS Detection is Non-Trivial The QRS detector must reliably cope with: - **Baseline wander** (0.1 – 0.5 Hz respiration & electrode movement) - **Powerline interference** (50 Hz India / 60 Hz elsewhere) - **Muscle artifact / EMG** (wideband 20 – 500 Hz) - **Motion artifact** (low-frequency transients) - **Tall T-waves** that can be falsely detected as R-peaks - **Abnormal morphologies** (PVC, bundle branch blocks, paced beats) The benchmark solution in nearly all biomedical textbooks is the **Pan–Tompkins Algorithm (1985)**, which achieves a sensitivity of > 99% on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Pan–Tompkins Algorithm — Six Logical Stages The Pan–Tompkins QRS detector is a cascade of linear and non-linear stages. Each stage has a deliberate purpose. | Stage | Block | Purpose | Key Parameter | |-------|-------|---------|---------------| | 1 | **Bandpass Filter (BPF)** | Isolate 5–15 Hz QRS energy, reject baseline & mains | LP: 11 Hz cut, HP: 5 Hz cut | | 2 | **Differentiator** | Highlight the *slope* of QRS (steep R-rise) | 5-tap FIR | | 3 | **Squaring** | Make all values positive; emphasize large slopes (non-linear) | $y[n]=x^2[n]$ | | 4 | **Moving-Window Integrator (MWI)** | Produce a smooth envelope that peaks at the R-wave | N = 30 samples (~150 ms) | | 5 | **Adaptive Thresholding** | Decide QRS present / absent | Dual threshold (signal & noise levels) | | 6 | **Search-back & T-wave discrimination** | Recover missed beats; reject tall T-waves | 200 ms refractory window | ### 2.2 Stage 1 — Bandpass Filter (Cascade of LP + HP) The Pan–Tompkins bandpass is implemented as a **cascaded low-pass and high-pass** filter pair, both designed via the **bilinear transform** of analog prototypes. **Low-pass filter** (cutoff ≈ 11 Hz, 3 dB): $$H_{LP}(z) = \frac{\left(1 - z^{-6}\right)^2}{\left(1 - z^{-1}\right)^2}$$ **High-pass filter** (cutoff ≈ 5 Hz, 3 dB): $$H_{HP}(z) = \frac{-1 + 2z^{-16} - z^{-32}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$ **Net bandpass**: $H_{BP}(z) = H_{LP}(z) \cdot H_{HP}(z)$ — passes roughly **5 – 11 Hz** with a gentle roll-off that intentionally preserves enough QRS slope to drive the differentiator. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this band?** > The QRS complex has 99% of its power spectral density between 3 Hz and 40 Hz, with the dominant energy between **8 and 12 Hz**. A 5–11 Hz band maximizes the **SNR for QRS** against baseline wander (< 0.5 Hz), 50 Hz mains interference, and EMG noise. ### 2.3 Stage 2 — Five-Point Derivative The differentiator approximates the first derivative $\frac{dx}{dt}$ using a 5-tap FIR filter: $$y[n] = \frac{1}{8}\Big( -x[n-2] - 2x[n-1] + 2x[n+1] + x[n+2] \Big)$$ - Output is divided by **8** (the sum $\mid -1-2+0+2+1 \mid = 8$) for **unity DC normalization**. - Suppresses low-frequency P and T waves (low slope). - Emphasizes the **steep rising and falling edges of R**. ### 2.4 Stage 3 — Squaring $$y[n] = \big( x[n] \big)^2$$ - **Non-linear point operation**. - All samples become **strictly non-negative** (rectified). - **Large slopes are amplified disproportionately** (squaring $\to$ super-linear). - Output is essentially the **QRS energy envelope** estimate. ### 2.5 Stage 4 — Moving-Window Integrator (MWI) $$y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$$ - Window length **N = 30 samples** for $f_s = 200$ Hz (≈ 150 ms). - Smooths the squared signal into a single **blob per QRS**. - Window width must be **roughly equal to the widest expected QRS** (≈ 150 ms) to avoid merging adjacent QRS in fast heart rates. ### 2.6 Stage 5 — Adaptive Dual Threshold Two thresholds are maintained: **signal level** $SPK$ and **noise level** $NPK$. **During a QRS detection (peak above $THR1$):** $$SPK = 0.125 \cdot PEAK + 0.875 \cdot SPK$$ **When no QRS detected (peak below $THR1$):** $$NPK = 0.125 \cdot PEAK + 0.875 \cdot NPK$$ **Threshold update:** $$THR_1 = NPK + 0.25\,(SPK - NPK)$$ $$THR_2 = 0.5 \cdot THR_1$$ - $THR_1$: **first-pass detection**. - $THR_2$: **search-back** threshold (if no QRS detected for > 1.66 × current RR interval, the algorithm searches back to the highest peak exceeding $THR_2$). ### 2.7 Stage 6 — Refractory Blanking & T-Wave Rejection - After a true QRS detection, the detector is **blanked for 200 ms** to ignore the T-wave that follows. - If a candidate peak occurs **within 360 ms** of the previous QRS and its amplitude is **less than half** the previous QRS amplitude, it is **rejected as T-wave**. ### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Equation | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units | |----------|---------|------------------|-------| | QRS frequency band | $f \in [5, 15]\;\text{Hz}$ | Spectral locus of ventricular depolarization | Hz | | Sampling frequency | $f_s = 250\text{–}500$ Hz | Nyquist satisfied up to 250 Hz | Hz | | Five-point derivative | $y[n] = \frac{1}{8}\big(-x[n-2]-2x[n-1]+2x[n+1]+x[n+2]\big)$ | Slope of ECG | mV/sample | | Squaring | $y[n]=(x[n])^2$ | Energy-like envelope | mV² | | MWI | $y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$ | Smoothed QRS envelope | mV² | | MWI window | $N \approx 0.15 \cdot f_s$ | ≈ 150 ms QRS width | samples | | Adaptive thr. | $THR_1 = NPK + 0.25(SPK-NPK)$ | Dual-level decision | mV² | | Refractory period | $T_{ref} = 200$ ms | T-wave blanking | ms | | Search-back | $T_{sb} = 1.66 \cdot RR_{avg}$ | Missed-beat recovery | ms | | Heart rate (instantaneous) | $HR = \frac{60}{RR\;\text{(s)}}$ | Beats per minute | bpm | | Sensitivity | $Se = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Beat-detection yield | dimensionless | | Positive predictivity | $PPV = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Detection reliability | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Pan–Tompkins is the *de facto* front-end in commercial Holter monitors, ICU patient monitors, defibrillators, and wearable ECG patches (Apple Watch, Fitbit ECG, KardiaMobile). Variants of the same algorithm also drive real-time R-peak detection in **BCI (Brain–Computer Interface)** systems using cardiac-synchronous EEG triggering. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Why the Squaring Stage is Non-Negotiable **Setup:** Let the bandpass-filtered ECG be modelled as a QRS component $s[n]$ buried in additive noise $w[n]$: $$x[n] = s[n] + w[n]$$ Assume the noise is **zero-mean Gaussian**: $w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2)$, and that $s[n]$ is **non-zero only near R-peaks** with peak amplitude $A$. After squaring: $$y[n] = x^2[n] = s^2[n] + 2s[n]w[n] + w^2[n]$$ Taking the **expectation** (mean of $y$): $$E\{y[n]\} = E\{s^2[n]\} + 2E\{s[n]\}E\{w[n]\} + E\{w^2[n]\}$$ Since $E\{w[n]\}=0$ and $E\{w^2[n]\}=\sigma_w^2$: $$E\{y[n]\} = s^2[n] + \sigma_w^2$$ The mean has gained an **additive constant** $\sigma_w^2$ — the noise floor becomes flat and predictable. This is the foundation for **constant false-alarm-rate (CFAR) adaptive thresholding**. The **variance** of $y$ is: $$\text{Var}\{y[n]\} = 2\sigma_w^4 + 4\sigma_w^2 s^2[n]$$ - At noise-only points: $\text{Var} = 2\sigma_w^4$ (small). - At R-peak: $\text{Var} = 2\sigma_w^4 + 4\sigma_w^2 A^2$ (very large, scaled by $A^2$). Thus **the SNR at the threshold input scales linearly with peak amplitude squared**, making the R-peak stand out far more than in the raw ECG. This is *why* squaring is the critical non-linearity in Pan–Tompkins. ### 3.2 Derivation — MWI Window Length $N$ Selection We want a window that: - Spans the **widest QRS** (≈ 150 ms for normal beats, up to 200 ms for BBB). - Does **not merge** two adjacent QRS at the **fastest plausible HR**. Maximum plausible HR ≈ **220 bpm** ⇒ minimum RR interval: $$RR_{min} = \frac{60}{220} \approx 0.273 \text{ s} = 273 \text{ ms}$$ To avoid merging, the MWI width must satisfy: $$N \cdot T_s < RR_{min} \Rightarrow N < \frac{273\,\text{ms}}{T_s}$$ For $f_s = 200$ Hz ($T_s = 5$ ms): $$N < \frac{273}{5} = 54.6 \Rightarrow N \le 30 \text{ (Pan–Tompkins choice, conservative)}$$ For $f_s = 500$ Hz ($T_s = 2$ ms): $$N < \frac{273}{2} = 136.5 \Rightarrow N \le 75$$ **Conclusion:** $N$ must be **scaled with sampling frequency**: $N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$ is the rule of thumb. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Adaptive Threshold Update **Given:** A patient has just produced a sequence of MWI peaks. Initial estimates: $SPK_0 = 0.50$ mV², $NPK_0 = 0.05$ mV². Current observed peak = $PEAK = 0.80$ mV². The peak **exceeds** $THR_1$ (so it is a signal, not noise). **Step 1 — Compute initial threshold:** $$THR_1^{(0)} = 0.05 + 0.25(0.50 - 0.05) = 0.05 + 0.1125 = 0.1625 \text{ mV}^2$$ **Step 2 — Verify detection:** $PEAK = 0.80 > 0.1625$ ✓ — QRS confirmed. **Step 3 — Update signal level:** $$SPK_1 = 0.125 \cdot 0.80 + 0.875 \cdot 0.50 = 0.100 + 0.4375 = 0.5375 \text{ mV}^2$$ **Step 4 — Noise level unchanged (no noise-only peak observed):** $$NPK_1 = 0.05 \text{ mV}^2$$ **Step 5 — New threshold for next beat:** $$THR_1^{(1)} = 0.05 + 0.25(0.5375 - 0.05) = 0.05 + 0.1219 = 0.1719 \text{ mV}^2$$ **Step 6 — Second threshold:** $$THR_2^{(1)} = 0.5 \times 0.1719 = 0.0859 \text{ mV}^2$$ **Observation:** The threshold tracks the **true QRS amplitude envelope** with a **forgetting factor of 0.875** (slow adaptation, weighted toward the history). This makes the detector robust to gradual changes in skin-electrode impedance or posture shifts. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — QRS Detection Performance Benchmarks | Detector Method | Sensitivity (%) | PPV (%) | MIT-BIH Reference | Computational Cost | |-----------------|-----------------|---------|-------------------|---------------------| | **Pan–Tompkins (1985)** | 99.76 | 99.56 | Yes | Low (real-time) | | Hamilton–Tompkins (1986) | 99.74 | 99.59 | Yes | Low | | **Wavelet Transform (Li et al.)** | 99.89 | 99.94 | Yes | Medium | | Hilbert Transform (Benitez et al.) | 99.55 | 99.49 | Yes | Low | | Neural Network (deep CNN) | 99.30 | 99.60 | Yes | High (GPU) | | Matched Filter (template) | 98.10 | 97.40 | Yes | Medium | | Simple Threshold (amplitude only) | 75 – 85 | 60 – 75 | No | Trivial | > [!NOTE] > **Why Pan–Tompkins remains the lab standard:** It is **causal** (uses only past + current sample, suitable for real-time hardware), **derivative-free** in software (only 5-tap FIR), **noise-adaptive**, and **MIT-BIH validated** with > 99.5% accuracy — all without requiring any training data. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Pan–Tompkins Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw ECG Input
xn sampled at fs Hz] --> B[Bandpass Filter
5 to 15 Hz] B --> C[Differentiator
5-tap FIR] C --> D[Squaring Block
yn equals xn squared] D --> E[Moving Window Integrator
N samples wide] E --> F[Adaptive Thresholding
Dual THR1 and THR2] F --> G{Refractory Check
200 ms blanking} G -->|Within 200 ms| H[Reject as T-wave] G -->|Beyond 200 ms| I[Confirm QRS Detected] I --> J[Update SPK Level
SPK = 0.125 PEAK + 0.875 SPK] F --> K{Peak less than THR1} K -->|Yes| L[Update NPK Level
NPK = 0.125 PEAK + 0.875 NPK] L --> M{No QRS for
1.66 times RR average} M -->|Yes| N[Search-back using THR2] N --> I M -->|No| O[Continue Scanning] J --> O I --> P[R-peak Fiducial Output
tau k] O --> P ``` ### 4.2 Signal-Waveform Stage-by-Stage Transformation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1 [Stage 1 Raw ECG] A1[Noisy ECG
P QRS T baseline wander] end subgraph S2 [Stage 2 After BPF] A2[Cleaned ECG
QRS dominates] end subgraph S3 [Stage 3 After Differentiator] A3[Bipolar Spikes
at R edges] end subgraph S4 [Stage 4 After Squaring] A4[All Positive
sharp peaks] end subgraph S5 [Stage 5 After MWI] A5[Smooth Blobs
one per QRS] end A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 ``` ### 4.3 Threshold Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[New MWI Peak Available] --> Q1{Peak greater than THR1} Q1 -->|Yes| Sig[Classify as QRS
Update SPK] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Peak greater than THR2} Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Refractory elapsed
and amplitude test} Q3 -->|Yes| Sig2[QRS via Search-back] Q3 -->|No| Noise[Classify as Noise
Update NPK] Q2 -->|No| Noise Sig --> Out[Output R-peak marker] Sig2 --> Out Noise --> Out ``` ### 4.4 Algorithm Pseudocode (Lab-Ready) ``` ALGORITHM : Pan_Tompkins_QRS_Detector INPUT : ecg[N] # array of N samples at fs Hz OUTPUT : R_peaks # list of R-peak sample indices CONSTANTS : fs = 250 # sampling rate N_int = round(0.150*fs) # MWI width (~38 samples) refractory = round(0.200*fs) # 200 ms blanking thr1_alpha = 0.125 # signal level update thr2_alpha = 0.25 # threshold level offset spk = 0.0 npk = 0.0 last_qrs = -refractory missed_beats = 0 PROCEDURE : 1. y1 = BANDPASS(ecg, 5, 15, fs) # Stage 1 2. y2 = DIFFERENTIATE(y1) # Stage 2 3. y3 = SQUARE(y2) # Stage 3 4. y4 = MOVING_INTEGRATE(y3, N_int) # Stage 4 5. FOR each local peak p in y4: IF p > spk : spk = thr1_alpha*p + (1-thr1_alpha)*spk IF p > thr1 AND (i - last_qrs) > refractory: R_peaks.append(i) last_qrs = i missed_beats = 0 ELSE: npk = thr1_alpha*p + (1-thr1_alpha)*npk missed_beats += 1 thr1 = npk + thr2_alpha*(spk - npk) thr2 = 0.5 * thr1 IF missed_beats*fs > 1.66*RR_average: # search-back using thr2 ... 6. RETURN R_peaks ``` > [!NOTE] > **MATLAB Note for KTU Lab:** The above pseudocode can be implemented using MATLAB's `filter`, `diff`, and `movmean` primitives. The standard `pan_tompkin` MATLAB function on the File Exchange repository (by Reza Sameni) is approved for direct use in the PCEBL607 lab record. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define a QRS detector. Why is the QRS complex the most reliable fiducial marker in an ECG?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A QRS detector is a signal-processing algorithm that locates the temporal occurrence of the **QRS complex**, the ventricular depolarization waveform, in a continuous ECG signal. The QRS complex is the preferred fiducial marker because: 1. It is the **largest-amplitude feature** (0.5 – 2.0 mV) in the ECG, dominated by ventricular muscle mass. 2. It has the **steepest slope** (highest $dx/dt$) of any ECG wave, making it robustly distinguishable via derivative operations. 3. It is **spectrally localized** to a narrow band (5 – 15 Hz, centered at 10 Hz), enabling frequency-domain isolation. 4. It occurs **once per cardiac cycle** in normal sinus rhythm, making R-peaks a unique, monotonic timing reference. *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the four-point justification.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Expected]** *What is the role of the squaring block in the Pan–Tompkins algorithm?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The squaring block performs the non-linear operation $y[n] = (x[n])^2$, which serves **three critical roles**: 1. **Rectification:** All signal values become non-negative, so the negative-going S-wave combines constructively with the positive-going R-wave rather than cancelling it. 2. **Non-linear amplification:** Large slopes (QRS) are amplified by the square of their magnitude, while small slopes (P, T) are suppressed, dramatically **improving the SNR**. 3. **Energy estimation:** The output approximates a **short-term energy envelope** of the QRS, suitable for peak-based thresholding and CFAR detection. Mathematically, if $x = s + w$ with $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2)$, then $E\{x^2\} = s^2 + \sigma_w^2$ — i.e., the noise floor becomes a *flat, predictable* constant. *(1 mark per role.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of the **Pan–Tompkins QRS detection algorithm**. State the purpose of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 30-second ECG is sampled at $f_s = 200$ Hz. The Pan–Tompkins moving-window integrator is to be designed with a window covering the widest expected QRS (150 ms). Compute the window length $N$ and explain how you would choose $N$ for a patient with maximum heart rate of 200 bpm. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Block Diagram & Purpose (7 marks)** The Pan–Tompkins QRS detector is a six-stage cascade: **Stage 1 – Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz):** Implemented as a cascade of low-pass and high-pass filters. The low-pass section (cutoff ≈ 11 Hz) suppresses high-frequency EMG noise and 50 Hz mains interference, while the high-pass section (cutoff ≈ 5 Hz) removes baseline wander and DC offset. Together, they isolate the QRS spectral band. *(1.5 marks)* **Stage 2 – Five-Point Differentiator:** Approximates $\frac{dx}{dt}$ using the filter $$y[n] = \tfrac{1}{8}\big(-x[n-2]-2x[n-1]+2x[n+1]+x[n+2]\big)$$ This emphasizes the **steep slopes of the QRS** while suppressing the gentler P and T waves. *(1 mark)* **Stage 3 – Squaring:** $y[n] = x^2[n]$. Rectifies the differentiated signal and amplifies large slopes non-linearly, producing a positive envelope dominated by QRS energy. *(1 mark)* **Stage 4 – Moving-Window Integrator:** $y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}x[n-k]$. Smooths the squared signal into a single smooth pulse per QRS, with width ≈ 150 ms. *(1 mark)* **Stage 5 – Adaptive Thresholding:** Maintains two running estimates — signal level $SPK$ and noise level $NPK$ — updated as $$SPK = 0.125\cdot PEAK + 0.875\cdot SPK,\quad NPK = 0.125\cdot PEAK + 0.875\cdot NPK$$ with thresholds $THR_1 = NPK + 0.25(SPK - NPK)$ and $THR_2 = 0.5\,THR_1$. *(1.5 marks)* **Stage 6 – Refractory Blanking & T-Wave Rejection:** A 200 ms blanking window prevents double-counting; peaks within 360 ms of the previous QRS and < half its amplitude are rejected as T-waves. *(1 mark)* **Part (b) — MWI Window Length Computation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Window from QRS width:** $$N = \frac{W_{QRS}}{T_s} = \frac{150\,\text{ms}}{1/200\,\text{Hz}^{-1}} = \frac{0.150}{0.005} = 30 \text{ samples}$$ So the MWI uses **N = 30 samples** for $f_s = 200$ Hz. *(2 marks)* **Step 2 — Constraint from maximum heart rate:** Maximum HR = 200 bpm ⇒ minimum RR interval: $$RR_{min} = \frac{60}{200} = 0.300 \text{ s} = 300 \text{ ms}$$ To prevent two adjacent QRS from merging in the MWI output, the window width must be **strictly less than** $RR_{min}$: $$N \cdot T_s < RR_{min} \Rightarrow N < \frac{300\,\text{ms}}{5\,\text{ms}} = 60 \text{ samples}$$ The Pan–Tompkins choice of $N = 30$ satisfies this comfortably. *(2 marks)* **Step 3 — Sensitivity to $f_s$:** For a different sampling rate, e.g., $f_s = 500$ Hz, $N$ must be re-scaled: $$N = 0.150 \times 500 = 75 \text{ samples}$$ In general, $N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$ is the design rule. *(2 marks)* **Step 4 — Trade-off note:** A larger $N$ yields a smoother envelope but increases the risk of merging fast beats; a smaller $N$ resolves fast beats but may fragment wide QRS (e.g., in bundle branch block). The 150 ms value is a **clinical compromise**. *(1 mark)* --- **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023, Restated for 2024 Scheme] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** **(a)** Explain the concept of **adaptive thresholding** in QRS detection. Derive the update equations for signal and noise levels and explain the role of $THR_2$ and **search-back**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An ECG is sampled at 250 Hz. The integrator output yields a sequence of peaks $\{0.04, 0.06, 0.05, 0.45, 0.04, 0.50, 0.05\}$ mV². The initial signal level is $SPK = 0.10$ mV² and initial noise level is $NPK = 0.02$ mV². Assuming the threshold is recomputed after every peak, **trace the algorithm** to identify which peaks are detected as QRS. Use forgetting factor $\alpha = 0.125$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Adaptive Thresholding Theory (7 marks)** **Concept (2 marks):** In real-world ECG monitoring, the amplitude of QRS complexes varies continuously with electrode impedance, patient posture, respiration depth, and pathological state (e.g., pericardial effusion, MI). A **fixed threshold** would either miss small QRS or false-trigger on large T-waves. The Pan–Tompkins solution is a **dual-level adaptive threshold** that tracks the *running statistics* of the signal and noise envelopes. **Update equations (3 marks):** Two exponentially-weighted running averages are maintained. For a peak $P$ observed at iteration $k$: - If $P$ is classified as **signal** ($P > THR_1$): $$SPK_{k+1} = \alpha P + (1-\alpha) SPK_k$$ - If $P$ is classified as **noise** ($P \le THR_1$): $$NPK_{k+1} = \alpha P + (1-\alpha) NPK_k$$ The two thresholds are: $$THR_1 = NPK + 0.25\,(SPK - NPK)$$ $$THR_2 = 0.5 \cdot THR_1$$ With $\alpha = 0.125$, the **effective memory** is $\frac{1}{\alpha} = 8$ peaks — the running average reflects the last ~8 beats with exponentially decaying weight. **$THR_2$ and search-back (2 marks):** $THR_2$ is half the primary threshold. If the algorithm fails to detect a QRS for a duration exceeding **1.66 × current mean RR interval** (corresponding to a missed beat at HR < 36 bpm — a bradycardic event), it enters **search-back mode**: the algorithm scans backward in the MWI waveform for the highest peak that exceeds $THR_2$ (even if it didn't reach $THR_1$) and accepts it as a QRS detection. This recovers **missed beats** due to sudden amplitude drops. **Part (b) — Numerical Trace (7 marks)** **Initialize:** $SPK_0 = 0.10$, $NPK_0 = 0.02$. | Peak $k$ | $P_k$ (mV²) | $THR_1$ computed | $P_k > THR_1$? | Class | $SPK_{k+1}$ | $NPK_{k+1}$ | |----------|-------------|------------------|------------------|-------|--------------|--------------| | 1 | 0.04 | $0.02 + 0.25(0.10-0.02) = 0.040$ | = (not strictly >) | Noise | 0.100 | $0.125(0.04)+0.875(0.02)=0.0225$ | | 2 | 0.06 | $0.0225 + 0.25(0.10-0.0225)=0.0419$ | Yes | **QRS** | $0.125(0.06)+0.875(0.10)=0.0950$ | 0.0225 | | 3 | 0.05 | $0.0225 + 0.25(0.095-0.0225)=0.0406$ | Yes | **QRS** | $0.125(0.05)+0.875(0.095)=0.0894$ | 0.0225 | | 4 | 0.45 | $0.0225 + 0.25(0.0894-0.0225)=0.0392$ | Yes | **QRS** | $0.125(0.45)+0.875(0.0894)=0.1345$ | 0.0225 | | 5 | 0.04 | $0.0225 + 0.25(0.1345-0.0225)=0.0505$ | No | Noise | 0.1345 | $0.125(0.04)+0.875(0.0225)=0.0247$ | | 6 | 0.50 | $0.0247 + 0.25(0.1345-0.0247)=0.0546$ | Yes | **QRS** | $0.125(0.50)+0.875(0.1345)=0.1802$ | 0.0247 | | 7 | 0.05 | $0.0247 + 0.25(0.1802-0.0247)=0.0636$ | No | Noise | 0.1802 | $0.125(0.05)+0.875(0.0247)=0.0278$ | *(Each row: 1 mark; total 7 marks for complete table.)* **Detected QRS positions: Peaks #2, #3, #4, #6.** **Key observation:** Peak #1 (equal to $THR_1$) is **not detected** — the algorithm uses strict `>`, ensuring a safety margin. Peak #4 (0.45) and Peak #6 (0.50) cause large jumps in $SPK$, demonstrating the **fast adaptation to genuine QRS amplitude**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the cascade structure of the bandpass filter:** It is a *product* of an LP and an HP filter, not a single transfer function. Writing only $H(z) = (1-z^{-6})^2/(1-z^{-1})^2$ (the LP part) loses 2–3 marks. > 2. **Confusing the integrator with a low-pass filter:** The MWI is a *moving average*, not a Butterworth LPF. Many students write the wrong block. > 3. **Omitting the refractory period and search-back:** These are **Stage 5b/6** elements. A complete answer must mention them; otherwise full marks on "adaptive thresholding" are not awarded. > 4. **Using a fixed threshold in numerical examples:** Examiners explicitly test whether the student *recomputes* $THR_1$ after every peak. Static computation is a **common, fatal error**. > 5. **Units in MWI:** Forgetting that the MWI output has units of (mV)² — it is **energy**, not voltage. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — QRS Detector (PCEBL607 / Module 2)** > - **QRS** = ventricular depolarization; 80–120 ms; 0.5–2.0 mV; spectrum 5–15 Hz centered at 10 Hz. > - **Pan–Tompkins (1985)** is the **gold-standard** lab algorithm; 6 stages: BPF → Differentiator → Square → MWI → Adaptive Threshold → Refractory/Search-back. > - **Bandpass filter** is a cascade of LP (cutoff 11 Hz, transfer $(1-z^{-6})^2/(1-z^{-1})^2$) and HP (cutoff 5 Hz, transfer $(-1+2z^{-16}-z^{-32})/(1-z^{-1})$). > - **Five-point differentiator:** $y[n] = \frac{1}{8}(-x[n-2]-2x[n-1]+2x[n+1]+x[n+2])$. > - **Squaring** rectifies and amplifies QRS non-linearly; produces a positive energy envelope. > - **MWI window** $N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$ (≈ 150 ms QRS width). Upper bound: $N \le RR_{min}/T_s$ to avoid merging fast beats. > - **Adaptive thresholds:** $THR_1 = NPK + 0.25(SPK - NPK)$, $THR_2 = 0.5\,THR_1$. Update with $\alpha = 0.125$. > - **Refractory period** = **200 ms** (T-wave blanking). > - **Search-back** triggered when no QRS for **1.66 × RR_avg**, scanning back with $THR_2$. > - **T-wave rejection rule:** peak within 360 ms of previous QRS, amplitude < 0.5 × previous QRS amplitude → reject. > - **Performance:** Sensitivity > 99.5%, PPV > 99.5% on MIT-BIH. > - **Lab deliverables (PCEBL607):** implement in MATLAB using `pan_tompkin` File Exchange function; plot raw ECG, after BPF, after MWI, and overlay R-peak markers; compute instantaneous HR; report sensitivity against annotated beats. > - **Alternative methods to mention in viva:** Hilbert transform, wavelet (modulus maxima), matched filter, deep learning (1-D CNN) — Pan–Tompkins remains the most cited baseline. > - **Common mistakes:** (i) applying a *fixed* threshold, (ii) using a Butterworth LPF in place of the MWI, (iii) forgetting the squaring stage, (iv) setting $N$ independent of $f_s$, (v) missing the search-back logic.

topics.title: Patient Isolation Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL607 - BIO INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Patient Isolation Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Patient Isolation Circuit (PIC)** is an electrical safety subsystem employed in biomedical instrumentation (especially ECG, EEG, EMG, and patient monitoring systems) that provides **galvanic isolation** between the patient and the line-powered (mains) circuitry of the instrument. Its primary engineering objective is to prevent any hazardous leakage current — particularly **microshock currents (10 µA to 100 µA)** — from flowing through the patient to ground, in compliance with the **IEC 60601-1** medical electrical equipment safety standard.

The isolation is typically achieved through one (or a combination) of the following techniques:

1. **Transformer-based isolation** (mains isolation transformer at the front end)
2. **Optical isolation** (optocouplers/phototransistors across the signal path)
3. **Battery-powered floating operation** (complete galvanic isolation from mains)
4. **Capacitor-coupled / Hall-effect isolation** for signal transfer without a conductive path

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight — Patient isolation is a *mandatory* design requirement in any equipment that makes direct electrical contact with the patient (Type BF — Body Floating, and Type CF — Cardiac Floating) as per IEC 60601-1.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a bathtub (wet environment, very low body resistance ≈ **1,000 Ω**). The water pipe is connected to the AC mains. If the water pipe develops even a tiny fault, the entire current from the mains can pass through you to the ground. That is the situation a patient is in — they are physically connected to multiple electrodes and sensors, and any fault current can flow straight through the heart.

A **Patient Isolation Circuit is like a thick, non-conductive rubber raft placed between the patient and the power grid.** The raft physically separates the patient from the mains, yet still allows information (signals like ECG) to pass across — but only via "non-conductive messengers" (light, magnetism, or radio waves). Even if a massive fault occurs on the instrument side, the rubber raft ensures **zero conductive path** exists to the patient.

### Key Safety Thresholds (Bolded Standard Metrics)

| Current Level | Physiological Effect | Safety Standard |
|---------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **1 mA** | Threshold of perception (macroshock) | IEC 60601-1 limit (enclosure) |
| **10 mA** | "Let-go" current — muscle tetanus | — |
| **100 mA** | Ventricular fibrillation (macroshock, > 1 s) | — |
| **10 µA** | **Microshock fibrillation threshold** (direct to heart) | IEC 60601-1 patient leakage limit |

> [!NOTE]
> The **10 µA microshock limit** is **100 times smaller** than the perception threshold because it is applied *directly* to the myocardium via catheters or pacemaker leads, bypassing the skin's natural resistance.

## 1.3 Why Patient Isolation is Essential

A patient in a clinical environment is uniquely vulnerable because:

- **Skin resistance is bypassed** by electrodes, catheters, or IV lines
- **Multiple grounded devices** (monitors, ventilators, pumps) may connect to the same patient simultaneously
- A single **ground fault** in any one device can drive current through the patient's heart
- The patient may be **unconscious or sedated** and unable to react to current

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — How Patient Isolation Works

The fundamental engineering principle is **Galvanic Isolation**: breaking the direct conductive (galvanic) path between two electrical circuits while still permitting useful signal/power transfer through a non-conductive medium.

### Logic Steps of Operation

1. **Stage 1 — Mains Isolation Transformer**
   - The AC mains (230 V, 50 Hz in India) is stepped down by an isolation transformer with a **1:1 turns ratio** but with **galvanic isolation** between primary and secondary windings.
   - The transformer's dielectric barrier (insulation between windings) withstands **≥ 4000 V AC** (Hi-Pot test voltage per IEC 60601-1).
   - This physically separates the "mains side" (earth-referenced) from the "patient side" (floating).

2. **Stage 2 — Rectification and Regulation**
   - The isolated AC is rectified, filtered, and regulated to produce the DC rails (e.g., ±5 V, ±12 V) that power the analog front-end on the patient side.
   - These DC rails are **floating with respect to earth ground**.

3. **Stage 3 — Signal Acquisition**
   - ECG/EEG electrodes (Type CF applied parts) acquire biopotential signals from the patient.
   - An **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., AD620, INA128) amplifies the differential signal with high CMRR (> 100 dB).

4. **Stage 4 — Optical/Electrical Isolation Barrier**
   - The amplified signal is passed through an **optocoupler** (e.g., ILCT6, 4N35, HCPL-7800) to cross the isolation barrier.
   - The signal modulates an LED on the patient side; a phototransistor on the mains side reconstructs it — there is **no wire** between the two sides.

5. **Stage 5 — Driven Right Leg (DRL) Circuit** (related safety feature)
   - The common-mode voltage is sensed and actively driven back to the patient's right leg through a feedback amplifier, reducing common-mode interference and improving safety.

6. **Stage 6 — Output Stage**
   - The isolated, amplified signal is processed, displayed, and recorded — all on the mains side, completely decoupled from the patient.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities are in standard SI units unless otherwise stated. Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables to avoid markdown breakage.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical / Standard Value |
|---|-------------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $\text{Ohm's Law (Body)}$ $I = \dfrac{V}{R_{\text{body}}}$ | Current through the body from applied voltage | — |
| 2 | $R_{\text{body, dry skin}}$ | Skin resistance (dry, intact) | $\approx 100{,}000\ \Omega$ |
| 3 | $R_{\text{body, wet/broken skin}}$ | Skin resistance (wet, ECG electrodes) | $\approx 1{,}000\ \Omega$ |
| 4 | $R_{\text{internal}}$ | Resistance of internal body tissues | $\approx 300\text{–}500\ \Omega$ |
| 5 | $I_{\text{perception}}$ | Threshold of perception (macroshock) | $\approx 1\ \text{mA}$ |
| 6 | $I_{\text{let-go}}$ | "Let-go" current threshold | $\approx 10\ \text{mA}$ |
| 7 | $I_{\text{fib, macro}}$ | Macroshock ventricular fibrillation | $\approx 100\ \text{mA}$ (for $t > 1\ \text{s}$) |
| 8 | $I_{\text{fib, micro}}$ | Microshock fibrillation threshold (direct to heart) | $\approx 10\ \mu\text{A}$ |
| 9 | $I_{\text{leak, enclosure}}$ | Enclosure leakage current limit (IEC 60601-1) | $\leq 0.1\ \text{mA}$ |
| 10 | $I_{\text{leak, patient}}$ | Patient leakage current limit (Type CF) | $\leq 10\ \mu\text{A}$ |
| 11 | $I_{\text{leak, earth}}$ | Earth leakage current limit | $\leq 0.5\ \texttext{mA}$ |
| 12 | $V_{\text{Hi-Pot, isolation}}$ | Dielectric withstand (isolation barrier) | $\geq 4000\ \text{V AC}$ |
| 13 | $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $> 100\ \text{dB}$ |
| 14 | $I_{\text{iso}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{fault}}}{Z_{\text{isolation}}}$ | Fault current limited by isolation impedance | $Z_{\text{isolation}} \to \infty$ (ideal) |
| 15 | $P_{\text{iso, creepage}} \geq k \cdot V_{\text{working}}$ | Creepage distance for PCB layout | per IEC 60601-1 Table III |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **ECG Machines, EEG Machines, EMG Systems** — patient-side analog front-end must be isolated
- **Defibrillators** — require isolation to protect the patient during high-voltage discharge
- **Patient Monitors** (SpO₂, NIBP, multi-parameter) — IEC 60601-1 Type CF compliance
- **Implantable Devices** (pacemakers, ICDs) — extreme isolation to prevent microshock
- **Surgical Equipment** (electrosurgical units, diathermy) — isolation between RF generator and patient
- **Telemedicine and Wireless Patient Monitors** — optical/galvanic isolation in the front-end

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Allowable Leakage Current and Required Isolation Impedance

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A patient is connected to a Type CF (Cardiac Floating) medical device. The maximum allowable patient leakage current per IEC 60601-1 is **10 µA**. If a single fault occurs (e.g., the patient comes into contact with a mains-live conductor via a faulty electrode), determine the **minimum isolation impedance** that must exist between the patient and earth ground to limit the leakage current to the safe threshold.

### 3.1.2 Given Data

- Maximum permissible patient leakage current: $I_{\text{leak,max}} = 10\ \mu\text{A} = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}$
- Mains voltage (worst case in India, single fault): $V_{\text{mains}} = 230\ \text{V AC}$ (RMS)
- We assume the worst-case fault where full mains voltage appears across the isolation barrier.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

We model the isolation barrier as a single impedance $Z_{\text{iso}}$ in series between the patient and earth ground. Applying Ohm's Law for AC circuits:

$$I_{\text{leak}} = \frac{V_{\text{mains}}}{Z_{\text{isolation}}}$$

Solving for the required isolation impedance:

$$Z_{\text{isolation}} = \frac{V_{\text{mains}}}{I_{\text{leak,max}}}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{isolation}} &= \frac{230\ \text{V}}{10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}} \\
&= \frac{230}{10^{-5}}\ \Omega \\
&= 230 \times 10^{5}\ \Omega \\
&= 2.3 \times 10^{7}\ \Omega \\
&= 23\ \text{M}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Therefore, the isolation barrier between the patient side and the mains side must present an impedance of **at least 23 MΩ** to ensure that even a single fault cannot drive more than 10 µA through the patient.

### 3.1.4 Safety Margin Calculation

In practice, designers aim for a safety factor of **10× to 100×**, so the actual isolation impedance target is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{isolation, design}} &\geq 10 \times Z_{\text{isolation, min}} \\
&\geq 10 \times 23\ \text{M}\Omega \\
&\geq 230\ \text{M}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

This is achieved by combining transformer dielectric insulation, optocoupler isolation, and PCB creepage/clearance distances per IEC 60601-1.

### 3.1.5 Creepage and Clearance Distance

For a working voltage of 250 V and a basic insulation (per IEC 60601-1 Table III), the minimum creepage distance is:

$$d_{\text{creepage}} \geq k_{\text{material}} \cdot V_{\text{working}}$$

where $k_{\text{material}} = 3.0\ \text{mm/kV}$ for a Material Group II PCB (CTI 400–599):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{creepage}} &\geq 3.0\ \frac{\text{mm}}{\text{kV}} \times 0.25\ \text{kV} \\
&\geq 0.75\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU students must remember that **creepage = surface distance** and **clearance = air distance** between conductive parts. For reinforced insulation (patient isolation), **double** these values.

## 3.2 Derivation: Macroshock vs Microshock Current Estimation

### 3.2.1 Macroshock Case

In macroshock, current enters through the **skin** and passes through the body. Total body resistance (skin + internal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{body,total, macro}} &= 2 \cdot R_{\text{skin}} + R_{\text{internal}} \\
&= 2 \times 100{,}000\ \Omega + 500\ \Omega \\
&\approx 200{,}500\ \Omega \approx 200\ \text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

For 230 V mains:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{macro}} &= \frac{V}{R_{\text{body,total}}} = \frac{230}{200{,}000} \\
&= 1.15\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is just above the perception threshold — usually harmless unless prolonged.

### 3.2.2 Microshock Case

In microshock, current is delivered **directly to the heart muscle** via a catheter or pacemaker lead. The skin resistance is **bypassed**, so only $R_{\text{internal}}$ matters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{micro}} &= \frac{V}{R_{\text{internal}}} = \frac{230}{500} \\
&= 460\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Even a few millivolts across a cardiac catheter can drive microamp-level currents through the myocardium. A leakage of just **10 µA** can cause ventricular fibrillation. This is why patient isolation is non-negotiable in cardiac equipment (Type CF).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Isolation Techniques Comparison

| Isolation Method | Isolation Voltage | Bandwidth | Linearity | Cost | Typical Application |
|------------------|-------------------|-----------|-----------|------|---------------------|
| Isolation Transformer | 4000–5000 V AC | DC to 100 kHz | Excellent | Medium | Mains power isolation, line-frequency EEG |
| Optocoupler (e.g., HCPL-7800) | 5000 V RMS | DC to 100 kHz | Good (0.1%) | Low | ECG signal isolation, digital signal isolation |
| Capacitive Coupling (digital isolator, e.g., ADuM1411) | 2500–5000 V RMS | DC to 25 Mbps (digital) | Excellent | Medium | Digital data isolation, USB isolation |
| Hall Effect Sensor | 2500 V RMS | DC to 100 kHz | Moderate | High | Current sensing without shunt |
| Battery Operation (Floating) | Infinite (no mains) | Full analog | Excellent | High (battery) | Portable monitors, Holter, ambulatory |
| Fiber Optic Link | > 50 kV (inherent) | MHz–GHz | Excellent | Very High | X-ray systems, MRI, high-voltage cath lab |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "design" or "select" an isolation method, the answer must always consider **(a) isolation voltage rating, (b) signal bandwidth, (c) leakage capacitance, and (d) cost.** A common mistake is choosing a digital isolator for a low-frequency analog ECG signal — which requires either an **analog linear optocoupler** (e.g., IL300) or a **modulator + digital isolator + demodulator** scheme (e.g., HCPL-7800 isolated amplifier).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Patient Isolation in an ECG System

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the functional signal and power flow between the patient and the mains-powered monitor. All nodes are alphanumeric and follow the Mermaid safe-identifier rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> B[Isolation Transformer 1:1]
    B --> C[Rectifier and Filter]
    C --> D[DC Regulator Floating Rails plus minus 12V plus minus 5V]
    D --> E[Patient Side Analog Front End]
    E --> F[ECG Electrodes on Patient]
    F --> G[Instrumentation Amplifier AD620]
    G --> H[Isolation Barrier Optocoupler HCPL7800]
    H --> I[Mains Side Signal Processing]
    I --> J[Display and Recorder]
    D -.No conductive path.- H
    H -.Light coupling only.- I
    K[Driven Right Leg DRL Circuit] --> F
    G --> K
    L[Earth Ground] -.Blocked by isolation.-> F
```

### Reading the Diagram

- The **dashed line** from `Earth Ground` to the patient is intentionally shown as a **blocked path** — illustrating that the isolation barrier prevents any conductive return to earth.
- The **isolation barrier** (node H) uses light as the "messenger" — the patient side and mains side share **no wire**.
- The **DRL circuit** actively drives the common-mode voltage back to the patient's right leg for noise cancellation, but it remains on the patient side of the barrier.

## 4.2 Schematic — Optocoupler-Based Analog Isolation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient ECG Signal Differential plus minus 1mV] --> B[INA128 Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 100]
    B --> C[ILCT6 Optocoupler LED Driver]
    C -- Light --> D[Phototransistor on Mains Side]
    D --> E[Op-Amp Buffer and Filter]
    E --> F[ADC and Microcontroller]
    F --> G[Display]
    H[Floating Power Supply plus minus 5V] --> B
    H --> C
    I[Mains Power 230V] --> J[Isolation Transformer] --> H
```

### Notes on the Schematic

- The **ILCT6** is a linear optocoupler that preserves analog fidelity.
- The **INA128** is on the patient side and is powered by the **floating DC rails** derived from the isolation transformer.
- The **microcontroller and display** are on the mains side, fully isolated from the patient.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Complete Patient Safety Subsystem (with DRL and Isolation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Patient] --> A2[ECG Electrodes RA LA LL]
    A2 --> A3[Defibrillation Protection Resistors and Gas Discharge Tubes]
    A3 --> A4[ESD Protection and Input Filtering]
    A4 --> A5[Right Leg Drive Amplifier and Common Mode Feedback]
    A5 --> A6[Instrumentation Amplifier Stage 1]
    A6 --> A7[Optically Isolated Amplifier Barrier 5kV]
    A7 --> A8[Mains Side Amplifier and Filter]
    A8 --> A9[ADC 16 bit]
    A9 --> A10[Digital Signal Processing]
    A10 --> A11[Display and Network Output]
    A5 -- Right Leg Electrode --> A1
    A12[230V AC Mains] --> A13[Medical Grade Isolation Transformer]
    A13 --> A14[Floating Linear Power Supply]
    A14 --> A5
    A14 --> A6
    A14 --> A7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is a Patient Isolation Circuit? Why is it necessary in biomedical equipment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Patient Isolation Circuit (PIC) is a safety subsystem that provides **galvanic isolation** between the patient and the mains-powered circuitry of a medical device. It is necessary because:

1. **Microshock prevention** — A leakage current as low as **10 µA** delivered directly to the heart (via catheters or pacemaker leads) can cause ventricular fibrillation.
2. **Ground fault protection** — In hospitals, multiple devices connect to one patient. A single ground fault in any one device could otherwise drive current through the patient.
3. **Compliance with IEC 60601-1** — The international safety standard mandates patient leakage current limits of **≤ 10 µA (Type CF)** and **≤ 100 µA (Type BF)**.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Macroshock and Microshock. Why is microshock more dangerous?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Macroshock | Microshock |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| Current path | Through intact skin | Directly to heart (via catheter) |
| Skin resistance | Present (~100 kΩ dry) | Bypassed (~500 Ω internal only) |
| Fibrillation threshold | ~100 mA | ~10 µA |
| Frequency | Skin contact scenarios | Surgical/cardiac catheter scenarios |

Microshock is more dangerous because the **skin resistance is bypassed**, allowing even tiny currents (~10 µA) to reach the myocardium and trigger ventricular fibrillation. The patient is often **sedated or unconscious** and cannot react.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Patient Isolation Circuit** used in an ECG machine. Identify the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ECG monitor is connected to a patient via three electrodes. The patient has a catheter inserted into the heart. If a single fault occurs such that 230 V AC appears on the patient-side input, calculate the **minimum isolation impedance** required to limit the leakage current through the catheter to **10 µA**. Comment on the design implications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks)

The architecture consists of the following blocks (refer to Section 4 diagrams):

1. **Mains Isolation Transformer (1:1, 4 kV Hi-Pot)** — provides galvanic isolation between mains and the floating patient-side supply. (1 mark)

2. **Floating DC Power Supply** — Rectifier + filter + regulator producing ±12 V and ±5 V referenced to a floating ground (not earth). (1 mark)

3. **Defibrillation Protection Network** — Series resistors (10 kΩ) and gas discharge tubes (or Zener diodes) at each electrode input to protect the amplifier from defibrillator shocks. (1 mark)

4. **Instrumentation Amplifier (e.g., AD620)** — High CMRR (> 100 dB), differential ECG amplification with gain 10–100. (1 mark)

5. **Driven Right Leg (DRL) Circuit** — Senses common-mode voltage and drives it back to the right-leg electrode via a feedback amplifier, improving CMRR and patient safety. (1 mark)

6. **Optical Isolation Barrier (Optocoupler / Isolated Amplifier)** — e.g., HCPL-7800 with 5 kV isolation. Transfers the amplified analog signal across the isolation barrier using light. (1 mark)

7. **Mains-Side Signal Processing and Display** — ADC, DSP, microcontroller, LCD/CRT display. Fully isolated from the patient. (1 mark)

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{mains}} = 230\ \text{V (RMS)}$
- $I_{\text{leak, max}} = 10\ \mu\text{A} = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}$
- Catheter to heart — direct path, microshock scenario.

**Step 1:** Apply Ohm's Law to the isolation barrier (series impedance between patient and earth):

$$Z_{\text{isolation}} = \frac{V_{\text{mains}}}{I_{\text{leak, max}}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{isolation}} &= \frac{230\ \text{V}}{10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}} \\
&= \frac{230}{10^{-5}}\ \Omega \\
&= 23 \times 10^{6}\ \Omega \\
&= 23\ \text{M}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

(3 marks for setup, 2 marks for numerical computation)

**Step 3: Design implications** (2 marks)

- The isolation barrier must present **at least 23 MΩ** under single-fault conditions.
- In practice, a **safety factor of 10× to 100×** is used, giving a design target of **230 MΩ to 2.3 GΩ**.
- This is achieved by combining: **isolation transformer dielectric**, **optocoupler insulation**, and **PCB creepage/clearance distances** per IEC 60601-1.
- For Type CF (cardiac) applications, **reinforced insulation** is mandatory — double the creepage distance of basic insulation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the different **methods of patient isolation** in biomedical instruments. Compare transformer isolation, optical isolation, and battery-powered (floating) operation in terms of isolation voltage, bandwidth, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient is connected to a Type CF defibrillator-protected ECG monitor. The enclosure leakage current was measured to be **75 µA** and the patient leakage current was **8 µA**. State whether the device **passes** the IEC 60601-1 safety test. Justify with reference to the standard limits. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Methods of Patient Isolation (7 marks)

**Method 1: Transformer Isolation** (2 marks)
- Uses a 1:1 mains isolation transformer (4 kV dielectric withstand).
- Provides isolation only for the power path; the signal path still needs optocouplers.
- Bandwidth: DC to 100 kHz.
- Application: Mains power front-end of all line-powered medical devices.

**Method 2: Optical Isolation** (2 marks)
- Uses optocouplers (e.g., IL300, HCPL-7800) or digital isolators (e.g., ADuM1411).
- Transfers analog or digital signals across the barrier using light — no conductive path.
- Isolation voltage: 5 kV RMS typical; bandwidth depends on type (linear vs digital).
- Application: ECG/EEG signal path isolation, USB isolation, data acquisition.

**Method 3: Battery-Powered Floating Operation** (2 marks)
- The device is powered entirely by internal batteries; no mains connection on the patient side.
- Provides **infinite isolation impedance** between the device and earth.
- Limitation: Limited operating time, must be recharged.
- Application: Portable/ambulatory monitors, Holter, implantable devices.

**Comparison summary** (1 mark):

| Method | Isolation | Bandwidth | Application |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Transformer | 4 kV | DC–100 kHz | Power front-end |
| Optocoupler | 5 kV | DC–100 kHz (analog) / Mbps (digital) | Signal path |
| Battery | Infinite | Full analog | Portable monitors |

#### Part (b) — Safety Compliance (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Enclosure leakage current: $I_{\text{enc}} = 75\ \mu\text{A} = 0.075\ \text{mA}$
- Patient leakage current: $I_{\text{pat}} = 8\ \mu\text{A}$

**IEC 60601-1 Limits (Type CF, normal condition):**
- Enclosure leakage current: $\leq 0.1\ \text{mA}$ (100 µA) — (2 marks)
- Patient leakage current (Type CF): $\leq 10\ \mu\text{A}$ — (2 marks)

**Comparison and Conclusion:** (3 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{enc, measured}} = 0.075\ \text{mA} &< 0.1\ \text{mA} \quad \text{(PASSES)} \\
I_{\text{pat, measured}} = 8\ \mu\text{A} &< 10\ \mu\text{A} \quad \text{(PASSES)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The device **PASSES** the IEC 60601-1 safety test in normal condition for Type CF. However, the values are close to the limit; the design must also be tested under **single-fault condition** (e.g., one protective earth broken), where the limits are typically **5× to 10× stricter**. The margin is acceptable but should be documented in the design history file.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "patient leakage" with "earth leakage"** — earth leakage is up to 0.5 mA; patient leakage is only 10 µA (Type CF). Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor** — the calculated 23 MΩ is the *minimum*. Real designs use 10× to 100× margin. Examiners expect to see this mentioned.
> 3. **Skipping the unit analysis in calculations** — always write units at every step. Many students lose 1 mark for "75 µA vs 0.075 mA" confusion.
> 4. **Ignoring Type BF vs Type CF distinction** — Type BF (body floating) allows 100 µA patient leakage; Type CF (cardiac floating) is stricter at 10 µA. Examiners often ask to identify the class.
> 5. **Forgetting defibrillation protection** — a real patient isolation design must include **resistors + gas discharge tubes** at the electrode inputs to survive defibrillator shocks. This is a frequent 2-mark item.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Patient Isolation Circuit**

- ☐ **Definition**: A safety circuit that provides **galvanic isolation** between the patient and mains-powered circuitry.
- ☐ **Primary Goal**: Prevent **microshock (≥ 10 µA)** from reaching the heart via catheters/leads.
- ☐ **Key Standard**: **IEC 60601-1** — international medical electrical equipment safety standard.
- ☐ **Three isolation methods**:
  - **Transformer** — for power path (4 kV dielectric)
  - **Optocoupler** — for signal path (5 kV isolation, light coupling)
  - **Battery** — for portable/mobile (infinite isolation)
- ☐ **Creepage vs Clearance**: surface distance vs air distance between conductors; double for reinforced insulation.
- ☐ **Key leakage current limits** (memorize these!):
  - Enclosure: **≤ 0.1 mA (100 µA)**
  - Patient (Type BF): **≤ 100 µA**
  - Patient (Type CF): **≤ 10 µA**
  - Earth: **≤ 0.5 mA**
- ☐ **Macroshock vs Microshock**:
  - Macroshock: through skin, perception at 1 mA, fibrillation at 100 mA.
  - Microshock: direct to heart, fibrillation at **10 µA** — 10,000× more dangerous.
- ☐ **Skin resistance** (dry): **100 kΩ**; (wet/ECG): **1 kΩ**; (internal): **300–500 Ω**.
- ☐ **Defibrillation protection**: 10 kΩ series resistors + gas discharge tubes at every electrode input.
- ☐ **DRL circuit**: actively drives common-mode voltage to right-leg electrode to improve CMRR and safety.
- ☐ **CMRR of instrumentation amplifier**: must be **> 100 dB** (preferably > 110 dB).
- ☐ **Isolation impedance formula**: $Z_{\text{iso, min}} = V_{\text{mains}} / I_{\text{leak, max}}$ — at single fault, 230 V / 10 µA = **23 MΩ minimum**.
- ☐ **Safety factor**: always design for **10× to 100× margin** in actual hardware.
- ☐ **Common exam keywords**: "galvanic isolation," "creepage," "Hi-Pot test," "Type CF," "microshock," "driven right leg," "floating ground," "optocoupler."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash ADC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL607 - BIO INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Flash ADC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Flash Analog-to-Digital Converter (Flash ADC)**, also called a *parallel ADC*, is a high-speed data conversion architecture that simultaneously compares an unknown analog input voltage against a set of finely spaced reference voltages using a parallel bank of latched comparators. The combined output of these comparators forms a *thermometer code*, which is then asynchronously translated by a **priority encoder** into a standard N-bit binary word in essentially one comparator-propagation-delay plus one encoder-delay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A Flash ADC is an N-bit data converter that uses $2^N - 1$ identical high-speed comparators connected to a precision resistor reference ladder. It performs quantization in a single parallel step, making it the **fastest** ADC architecture available, with typical conversion times in the **nanosecond** range and sampling rates exceeding **1 Gs/sec** in modern CMOS implementations.

In the context of **Bio-Instrumentation**, the Flash ADC is critical in front-end digitizers for high-bandwidth physiological signals captured by devices such as **digital storage oscilloscopes** used in electrophysiology, **ultrasound beam-formers**, **CT/MRI front-end digitizers**, and **wide-bandwidth neural recording head-stages**, where biosignals demand sub-microsecond quantization.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Measuring Stick" Model

Imagine you are given an unknown length of wooden plank and asked to measure it to the nearest centimeter.

| Method | Approach | Time Taken |
|---|---|---|
| **Successive Approximation (SAR)** | Binary-search the plank: is it longer than 1m? then 2m? then 1.5m? … | Slow, multiple cuts |
| **Flash ADC** | Lay 99 pre-cut measuring sticks (1 cm, 2 cm … 99 cm) side by side and find the tallest one the plank *cannot* exceed. | **Single glance** |

The Flash ADC is exactly this "single glance" method — instead of sequentially trying each reference, it uses **every comparator at once** and reports the answer in a single clock tick.

## 1.3 Architectural Pillars (Three Building Blocks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Architectural Triad of a Flash ADC**
> 1. **Precision Resistor Reference Ladder** — generates $2^N$ equally spaced reference voltages.
> 2. **Bank of Latched Comparators** — $2^N - 1$ comparators running in parallel.
> 3. **Priority (Thermometer-to-Binary) Encoder** — collapses the $2^N - 1$ bit "thermometer code" into N output bits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)**
> - *Resolution (LSB):* $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N}$
> - *Quantization Error (peak):* $\pm \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{LSB}$
> - *Sampling Rate ($f_s$):* typically **100 MSPS – 10 GSPS** for CMOS Flash
> - *Hardware Cost:* $\mathcal{O}(2^N)$ components → doubles per extra bit of resolution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Walk-Through (Step-by-Step Logic)

The Flash ADC pipeline executes in **three time-stages** for every analog sample $V_{in}(t)$:

**Stage 1 — Reference Ladder Settling (analog, DC)**
- A matched resistor chain of $2^N$ equal resistors, $R$, is tied between a top reference $V_{REF+}$ and a bottom reference $V_{REF-}$.
- The ladder produces reference taps at:
  $$V_{REF,k} = V_{REF-} + k \cdot \frac{V_{REF+} - V_{REF-}}{2^N}, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, 2^N - 1$$

**Stage 2 — Parallel Comparison (single clock edge)**
- Comparator $k$ evaluates the boolean function:
  $$\text{OUT}_k = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } V_{in} > V_{REF,k} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
- All $2^N - 1$ comparators latch simultaneously on the rising edge of the sample clock.

**Stage 3 — Priority Encoding (combinational logic)**
- The resulting pattern of 1s followed by 0s (the "thermometer code") is fed to a priority encoder.
- The encoder outputs the **index of the highest-order 1**, which becomes the binary code word $D_{\text{out}}[N\!-\!1\!:\!0]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the name "Flash"?** Because the conversion happens in a *flash* — one clock cycle — with no iterative feedback or successive approximation. The data is "frozen" in a single instant.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of literal `\|` in any table cell to preserve markdown syntax.**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resolution (LSB)** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N}$ | Volts | Smallest distinguishable voltage step |
| 2 | Number of Comparators | $N_c = 2^N - 1$ | count | Hardware complexity ceiling |
| 3 | Number of Resistors | $N_R = 2^N$ | count | Ladder area / power |
| 4 | Total Ladder Resistance | $R_{\text{tot}} = 2^N \cdot R$ | $\Omega$ | Bias current = $V_{REF}/R_{\text{tot}}$ |
| 5 | Ladder Bias Current | $I_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N R}$ | A | Power dissipation driver |
| 6 | Conversion Time | $T_c = t_{\text{comp}} + t_{\text{enc}}$ | s | Sets max sampling rate |
| 7 | Maximum Sampling Rate | $f_s^{\max} = \dfrac{1}{T_c}$ | Samples/s | Throughput limit |
| 8 | Quantization SNR (ideal) | $\text{SNR}_q = 6.02 N + 1.76$ | dB | Theoretical noise floor |
| 9 | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | $\text{SQNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2^{2N}}{12}\right)$ | dB | Real dynamic range |
| 10 | Power Dissipation (ladder) | $P_{\text{ladder}} = \dfrac{V_{REF}^{2}}{2^N R}$ | W | Dominates static power |
| 11 | Total Static Power | $P_{\text{tot}} \approx N_c \cdot P_{\text{comp}} + P_{\text{ladder}}$ | W | Thermal budget for IC |
| 12 | Input Capacitance | $C_{in} \approx N_c \cdot C_{\text{comp}}$ | F | Limits analog BW |
| 13 | Analog Bandwidth | $f_{-3dB} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_s C_{in}}$ | Hz | Input tracking ability |
| 14 | DNL (ideal) | $0$ LSB | LSB | No missing code |
| 15 | INL (ideal) | $0$ LSB | LSB | No integral non-linearity |

## 2.3 Why Flash ADC Matters in Bio-Instrumentation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Use-Cases in Biomedical Engineering (KTU Hot Topic)**

1. **Wide-bandwidth neural recording** — Action potentials have rise-times of 100–300 µs; high $f_s$ preserves morphology.
2. **Ultrasound A/D front-end** — Center frequencies of 2–15 MHz require sampling at $\geq 40$ MSPS per channel; Flash ADCs sit in the beam-former ASICs.
3. **Digital oscilloscopes in cardiac catheterization labs** — capture transient bioelectric events.
4. **Mass spectrometry detectors** — digitize microsecond ion current pulses.
5. **Implantable closed-loop neurostimulators** — low-latency feedback demands Flash-class latency.

The **trade-off** that limits Flash ADCs in portable/wearable bio-devices is the exponential hardware growth — an 8-bit Flash needs **255 comparators**; a 10-bit Flash needs **1023**; a 12-bit Flash needs **4095** comparators. Hence, in KTU lab experiments, Flash ADCs are typically demonstrated at **N = 3 or N = 4 bits** for didactic clarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reference Voltage of the k-th Ladder Tap

We begin with a uniform resistor ladder of $2^N$ identical resistors $R$, spanning $V_{REF+}$ (top) and $V_{REF-}$ (bottom). The total ladder current is constant:

$$I_{\text{ladder}} = \frac{V_{REF+} - V_{REF-}}{2^N \cdot R}$$

Each resistor drops a fixed voltage:

$$V_{\text{drop}} = I_{\text{ladder}} \cdot R = \frac{V_{REF+} - V_{REF-}}{2^N} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^N} \quad (\text{assuming } V_{REF-} = 0)$$

The k-th tap, measured from the bottom, sits $k$ resistors above $V_{REF-}$:

$$V_{REF,k} = V_{REF-} + k \cdot \frac{V_{REF+} - V_{REF-}}{2^N}$$

**Textual Logic Row 1:** The current through every resistor is identical (Kirchhoff's Current Law) because the comparator inputs are ideally infinite impedance.

**Textual Logic Row 2:** Hence each resistor drops a constant voltage equal to the LSB $\Delta V$.

**Textual Logic Row 3:** The k-th tap voltage is a *linear interpolation* between the two reference rails.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermometer Code Length to Binary Index

The number of comparators whose output is logic 1 equals the integer part of $\dfrac{V_{in}}{\Delta V}$:

$$M = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor, \quad 0 \le M \le 2^N - 1$$

The encoder must produce an N-bit word equal to $M$ in binary. Using Gray-encoded priority logic (preferred to suppress bubble errors):

$$D_{\text{out}}[i] = \bigoplus_{k=0}^{2^{i+1}-1} \text{OUT}_{k+1}, \quad i = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

where $\oplus$ denotes XOR. This converts a thermometer bit-stream into a **binary** index in one logic level of XOR gates.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio

Assume the input is a full-scale sinusoid: $v(t) = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2}\sin(2\pi f t)$.

The signal RMS power is:

$$P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt = \frac{V_{REF}^{2}}{8}$$

The quantization noise is modeled as a uniform random variable over $[-\Delta V/2,\,+\Delta V/2]$:

$$P_{\text{noise}} = \frac{1}{\Delta V}\int_{-\Delta V/2}^{+\Delta V/2} e^{2}\,de = \frac{\Delta V^{2}}{12}$$

Substituting $\Delta V = V_{REF}/2^N$:

$$P_{\text{noise}} = \frac{1}{12}\left(\frac{V_{REF}}{2^N}\right)^{2} = \frac{V_{REF}^{2}}{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}$$

Hence:

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{V_{REF}^{2}/8}{V_{REF}^{2}/(12\cdot 2^{2N})} = \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}{8} = \frac{3}{2}\cdot 2^{2N}$$

In decibels:

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 20 N \log_{10} 2 \approx 1.76 + 6.02\,N$$

**Textual Logic Row 1:** Signal power uses the RMS of a sine wave ($1/\sqrt{2}$ of amplitude).

**Textual Logic Row 2:** Quantization noise power uses the variance of a uniform distribution ($L^{2}/12$).

**Textual Logic Row 3:** The ratio simplifies to a clean expression showing that every additional bit improves SNR by exactly $\mathbf{6.02}$ **dB**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Static Power Budget of a 4-bit Flash ADC

Let $V_{REF} = 1\,\text{V}$, $N = 4$, $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$.

$$N_c = 2^4 - 1 = 15 \text{ comparators}$$
$$N_R = 2^4 = 16 \text{ resistors}$$
$$R_{\text{tot}} = 16 \cdot 1\,\text{k}\Omega = 16\,\text{k}\Omega$$
$$I_{\text{ladder}} = \frac{1\,\text{V}}{16\,\text{k}\Omega} = 62.5\,\mu\text{A}$$
$$P_{\text{ladder}} = V_{REF} \cdot I_{\text{ladder}} = 1\,\text{V} \cdot 62.5\,\mu\text{A} = 62.5\,\mu\text{W}$$

Assuming each comparator draws $I_{\text{comp}} = 50\,\mu\text{A}$ from a 3.3 V supply:

$$P_{\text{comp,total}} = 15 \cdot 50\,\mu\text{A} \cdot 3.3\,\text{V} = 2.475\,\text{mW}$$
$$P_{\text{tot}} \approx 2.475\,\text{mW} + 0.0625\,\text{mW} = 2.538\,\text{mW}$$

**Textual Logic Row 1:** Resistor ladder contributes negligible power because of high impedance.

**Textual Logic Row 2:** Comparator array dominates static dissipation — a key reason Flash ADCs scale poorly with $N$.

**Textual Logic Row 3:** This is why **interleaved** or **folding** ADC architectures are used in practice for $N > 8$ bits.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Trade-off Table

| ADC Architecture | Speed (MSPS) | Resolution (bits) | Power (mW) | Hardware Scaling | Typical Bio-App |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash** | 100 – 10 000 | 4 – 8 | 5 – 500 | $2^N$ | Oscilloscope digitizer |
| SAR | 0.1 – 50 | 8 – 18 | 0.001 – 5 | $N$ | Wearable ECG/PPG |
| Sigma-Delta | 0.01 – 1 | 16 – 24 | 1 – 10 | Moderate | EEG, EMG precision |
| Pipelined | 10 – 500 | 8 – 16 | 10 – 100 | $N$ stages | Ultrasound beam-former |
| Dual-Slope | 0.001 – 0.1 | 16 – 22 | < 0.1 | Low | Bench-top pH meters |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Vin] --> B[Input Buffer]
    B --> C1[Comparator Bank 2N minus 1]
    R1[VREF+ Top Rail] --> C1
    R2[Resistor Ladder 2N] --> C1
    R2 --> R3[VREF- Bottom Rail]
    C1 --> D[Thermometer Code Bus]
    D --> E[Priority Encoder]
    CLK[Sample Clock] --> C1
    CLK --> E
    E --> F[N-bit Binary Output D N minus 1 to 0]
    F --> G[Bio-Signal Processor DSP]
    G --> H[Display / Storage]

    classDef analog fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef digital fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    class A,B,R1,R2,R3 analog
    class C1,D,E,F,CLK digital
    class G,H analog
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read-out Logic:** The comparator bank is the *only* analog block; everything from the thermometer code bus onward is purely digital, which is why Flash ADCs scale gracefully with CMOS process shrinks.

## 4.2 Internal Schematic — Resistor Ladder + Comparator Tap

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Ladder["Precision Resistor Ladder 2 to the N"]
        VRP[VREF+ Top] --- R1a[2R unit]
        R1a --- TAP1[Tap 1]
        TAP1 --- R1b[2R unit]
        R1b --- TAP2[Tap 2]
        TAP2 --- R1c[2R unit]
        R1c --- TAP3[Tap 3 dots]
        TAP3 --- R1d[2R unit]
        R1d --- TAPN[Tap 2N minus 1]
        TAPN --- R1e[2R unit]
        R1e --- VRN[VREF- Ground]
    end

    VIN[Vin] --> CMP1[Comparator 1]
    TAP1 --> CMP1
    VIN --> CMP2[Comparator 2]
    TAP2 --> CMP2
    VIN --> CMPN[Comparator 2N minus 1]
    TAPN --> CMPN
    CMP1 --> TH1[Therm Bit 1]
    CMP2 --> TH2[Therm Bit 2]
    CMPN --> THN[Therm Bit 2N minus 1]

    classDef ladderNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef cmpNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef sigNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    class VRP,VRN,TAP1,TAP2,TAP3,TAPN,R1a,R1b,R1c,R1d,R1e ladderNode
    class CMP1,CMP2,CMPN,TH1,TH2,THN cmpNode
    class VIN sigNode
```

## 4.3 Thermometer-Code to Binary Conversion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TH[Thermometer Code 2N minus 1 bits] --> XOR[XOR Reduction Network]
    XOR --> BIN[N-bit Binary Word]
    BIN --> OUT[Parallel Output Bus]
    STB[Strobe / Latch Signal] --> XOR
    STB --> OUT

    classDef enc fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    class TH,XOR,BIN,OUT enc
    class STB ctrl
```

## 4.4 Functional Pipeline — Flash ADC Inside a Bio-Instrumentation Acquisition Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BIO[Biosignal Source ECG EMG EEG] --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> FIL[Anti-Alias Filter]
    FIL --> S_H[Sample and Hold]
    S_H --> FLASH[Flash ADC N bit]
    FLASH --> MEM[Memory FIFO]
    MEM --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    DSP --> DISP[Clinician Display]
    DSP --> TX[Wireless Telemetry]

    classDef bio fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    classDef ana fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef dig fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    class BIO bio
    class AMP,FIL,S_H,FLASH ana
    class MEM,DSP dig
    class DISP,TX out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Didactic Note (KTU Lab Context):** In Module Experiment 2, the Flash ADC is typically implemented on a trainer kit as a 3-bit or 4-bit converter. The student is expected to *physically probe* each comparator output on an oscilloscope to observe the thermometer-code rising/falling pattern as the analog input is swept from 0 V to $V_{REF}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** — *Define a Flash ADC. State the number of comparators required for an 8-bit Flash converter and justify the formula.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Flash ADC** is a parallel analog-to-digital converter that uses a resistor reference ladder and a bank of high-speed comparators to convert an analog voltage to a digital code in a *single clock cycle*.

For an N-bit converter, the resistor ladder generates $2^N$ reference levels. Each adjacent pair defines one LSB step, requiring **one comparator per transition**. Hence, the number of comparators is:

$$N_c = 2^N - 1$$

For $N = 8$:

$$N_c = 2^8 - 1 = 256 - 1 = 255 \text{ comparators}$$

> *(Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for numerical substitution.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — *Why is Flash ADC called the fastest ADC? List two limitations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Flash ADC is the **fastest** ADC because it performs quantization in a single comparator-propagation delay. All $2^N - 1$ comparators operate in **parallel** and the result is latched on one clock edge — no successive iteration, no feedback loop. Sampling rates of multi-GSPS are routinely achieved.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Exponential hardware growth** — $2^N - 1$ comparators; an 8-bit Flash needs 255, a 10-bit Flash needs 1023.
2. **High power dissipation and large input capacitance** — limits resolution to typically 6–8 bits and restricts use in portable / implantable bio-devices.

> *(Allocation: 1 mark for speed reason, 1 mark each for the two limitations.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a 3-bit Flash ADC. Show the resistor ladder reference values, the comparator outputs (thermometer code), and the priority encoder output for an input of $V_{in} = 5.2\,\text{V}$ with $V_{REF} = 8\,\text{V}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the theoretical signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR) of an ideal N-bit Flash ADC. If the SQNR is required to be at least 70 dB, what is the minimum resolution in bits? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

For $N = 3$, the Flash ADC has:
- Resistors: $2^3 = 8$
- Comparators: $2^3 - 1 = 7$
- LSB: $\Delta V = V_{REF}/2^3 = 8/8 = 1\,\text{V}$

**Ladder Reference Taps:**

| k | $V_{REF,k}$ (V) | Comparator Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0 | $C_1 = 1$ |
| 2 | 2.0 | $C_2 = 1$ |
| 3 | 3.0 | $C_3 = 1$ |
| 4 | 4.0 | $C_4 = 1$ |
| 5 | 5.0 | $C_5 = 1$ |
| 6 | 6.0 | $C_6 = 0$ |
| 7 | 7.0 | $C_7 = 0$ |

**Thermometer code** = 1111100 (read MSB-first).

**Index of highest 1** = 5.

**Binary Output** of encoder = $D[2:0] = 101_2 = 5$.

The block diagram includes: Resistor ladder → 7 comparators → Priority encoder → 3-bit output. *(*Full marks require explicit mention of the encoder truth table relating 7-bit thermometer code to 3-bit binary.*)*

> *(Mark split: 2 — block diagram; 2 — ladder values & LSB; 2 — thermometer code for $V_{in}=5.2\,$V; 1 — binary output.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

From the derivation in §3.3:

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 N + 1.76$$

For $\text{SQNR} \ge 70\,\text{dB}$:

$$6.02 N + 1.76 \ge 70$$
$$6.02 N \ge 68.24$$
$$N \ge \frac{68.24}{6.02} \approx 11.33$$

$$\boxed{N_{\min} = 12 \text{ bits}}$$

> *(Mark split: 3 — full derivation of SQNR formula; 2 — numerical substitution; 1 — final rounded answer; 1 — interpretation.)*

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Compare Flash ADC, SAR ADC, and Sigma-Delta ADC in terms of speed, resolution, power, and typical bio-instrumentation application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a 4-bit Flash ADC for full-scale range $V_{FS} = 4\,\text{V}$. Compute: (i) LSB, (ii) number of comparators, (iii) ladder bias current if $R = 500\,\Omega$, and (iv) total static power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Parameter | Flash | SAR | Sigma-Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed (MSPS) | 100 – 10 000 | 0.1 – 50 | 0.01 – 1 |
| Resolution (bits) | 4 – 8 | 8 – 18 | 16 – 24 |
| Power | High (mW–W) | Low (µW–mW) | Low (mW) |
| Hardware scaling | $2^N$ | $N$ | Moderate |
| Bio-App | Oscilloscope digitizer, ultrasound | Wearable ECG/PPG | EEG, EMG precision |

**Verdict:** Flash ADC is chosen when *speed dominates*; SAR when *power dominates*; Sigma-Delta when *resolution dominates*.

> *(Mark split: 5 — table; 1 — verdict; 1 — bio-context.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Given $N = 4$, $V_{FS} = 4\,\text{V}$, $R = 500\,\Omega$.

**(i) LSB:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^4} = \frac{4\,\text{V}}{16} = 0.25\,\text{V}$$

**(ii) Number of comparators:**

$$N_c = 2^4 - 1 = 15$$

**(iii) Ladder bias current:**

$$I_{\text{ladder}} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N \cdot R} = \frac{4}{16 \cdot 500} = \frac{4}{8000} = 0.5\,\text{mA}$$

**(iv) Total static power (ladder only — comparator power to be added separately):**

$$P_{\text{ladder}} = V_{FS} \cdot I_{\text{ladder}} = 4 \cdot 0.5\,\text{mA} = 2\,\text{mW}$$

> *(Mark split: 1.5 each subpart + 1 for final summary.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (Flash ADC Problems)**
> 1. **Forgetting the "-1" in comparator count** — Writing "256 comparators for 8-bit" instead of $2^8 - 1 = 255$ loses a full mark.
> 2. **Mixing up Flash with SAR** — Conflating "parallel" with "successive approximation"; remember Flash = single-step parallel, SAR = N-step serial.
> 3. **Ignoring the priority encoder in block diagrams** — A diagram showing only the comparator bank is incomplete; encoder is mandatory.
> 4. **Wrong SQNR derivation** — Using the formula $6.02 N$ alone (without the $1.76\,\text{dB}$ offset) is the most common mistake; the offset arises from the $\tfrac{3}{2}$ factor in the sine-wave RMS treatment.
> 5. **Not specifying full-scale or reference voltage** — Without $V_{REF}$ explicitly stated, the LSB calculation cannot be evaluated; you will get **zero** for that subpart.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module Experiment 2 — Flash ADC)**
> - **Architecture:** Resistor ladder + parallel comparators + priority encoder. *(3 blocks, in that order.)*
> - **Comparators required:** $N_c = 2^N - 1$ *(NOT $2^N$).*
> - **LSB:** $\Delta V = V_{REF}/2^N$. *(Half-step quantization error.)*
> - **Speed advantage:** Single-clock conversion ⇒ GSPS-class sampling.
> - **Speed bottleneck:** Comparator metastability + encoder propagation delay.
> - **Hardware cost:** Doubles for every additional bit of resolution.
> - **Power cost:** Ladder bias + comparator static current; both grow exponentially.
> - **SNR formula:** $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 6.02 N + 1.76$. *(Memorize with the +1.76 dB offset.)*
> - **Thermometer code:** All 1s above the input level, all 0s below.
> - **Encoder:** Converts $2^N - 1$ thermometer bits to N binary bits via XOR reduction (Gray coding preferred).
> - **Bio-instrumentation role:** Front-end digitizer in oscilloscopes, ultrasound, neural recorders, CT/MRI receivers.
