the residual ~5 mV comes from the active Na⁺/K⁺ pump (electrogenic contribution not captured in GHK). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conduction Velocity from Cable Theory For an unmyelinated axon of diameter *d* (cm), axoplasm resistivity $R_i \approx 35\;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}$, membrane capacitance $C_m \approx 1\;\mu\text{F/cm}^2$, and membrane resistance $R_m \approx 1000\;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$: **Space constant:** $$ \lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{R_m \cdot d}{4 R_i}} \;\approx\; \sqrt{\frac{1000 \cdot d}{140}} $$ For a 10 µm axon ($d = 10^{-3}$ cm): $$ \lambda \;\approx\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{140}} \;\approx\; 0.085\;\text{cm} \;\approx\; 0.85\;\text{mm} $$ **Time constant:** $$ \tau \;=\; R_m\,C_m \;\approx\; 1000 \times 10^{-6} \;\approx\; 1\;\text{ms} $$ **Conduction velocity (Hodgkin, 1954):** $$ v \;\approx\; \sqrt{\frac{d}{2 R_i C_m}} \;\approx\; \sqrt{\frac{10^{-3}}{2 \times 35 \times 10^{-6}}} \;\approx\; 3.78\;\text{m/s} $$ This matches the experimental conduction velocity of a small unmyelinated C-fibre. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Filter Selection for EMG vs. ENG | Specification | EMG (Surface) | EMG (Needle) | ENG (Nerve) | ECG | EEG | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Bandwidth (Hz) | **20 – 500** | **20 – 5000** | **100 – 10 000** | 0.05 – 150 | 0.5 – 100 | | Amplitude range | 50 µV – 5 mV | 100 µV – 10 mV | 1 – 100 µV | 0.5 – 5 mV | 5 – 300 µV | | Notch filter | 50 Hz | 50 Hz | 50 Hz | 50/60 Hz | 50/60 Hz | | Sampling rate (min) | 1 kHz | 10 kHz | 50 kHz | 500 Hz | 500 Hz | | ADC resolution | 12–16 bit | 16 bit | 16–24 bit | 12 bit | 12–16 bit | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** A common viva question is *"Why does ENG need a higher sampling rate than EMG?"* Answer: ENG contains higher-frequency components (up to 10 kHz) and lower amplitudes, so we need oversampling to lift the SNR above the **quantisation-noise floor** $V_{LSB} = V_{FS}/2^{N}$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Biopotential Acquisition System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[BIOELECTRICAL
SOURCE
Nerve or Muscle Fibre] --> B[TRANSDUCER
Surface or Needle Electrode] B --> C[INPUT PROTECTION
AND ISOLATION
Optocoupler IEC 60601] C --> D[INSTRUMENTATION
AMPLIFIER
Gain 1000, CMRR 110 dB] D --> E[HIGH PASS FILTER
fc approx 20 Hz] E --> F[MAIN AMPLIFIER
Variable Gain 10 to 500] F --> G[LOW PASS FILTER
fc approx 500 Hz EMG
or 10 kHz ENG] G --> H[NOTCH FILTER
50 or 60 Hz Rejection] H --> I[ADC
16 to 24 bit
fs 1 to 50 kHz] I --> J[MICROCONTROLLER
DSP or PC] J --> K[DISPLAY
LCD or Monitor] J --> L[STORAGE
SD Card or HDD] J --> M[ANALYSIS
Amplitude, Latency, Velocity] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Viva Question:** "What is the purpose of the *notch filter*?" Answer: To suppress **power-line interference at 50 Hz (India/Europe) or 60 Hz (USA)** which is the dominant noise source in shielded but un-grounded patient environments. A *narrow* notch (Q ≈ 30) preserves the diagnostic 20–500 Hz EMG band. --- ### 4.2 Action Potential Phases and Ionic Flux ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[STIMULUS APPLIED] --> P1{Threshold crossed
less than minus 55 mV} P1 -- No --> P0x[No Action Potential
Local graded response] P1 -- Yes --> P2[PHASE 0 DEPOLARISATION
Na channels OPEN
Na ions RUSH IN] P2 --> P3[PEAK approx plus 30 mV
Approaches E_Na] P3 --> P4[PHASE 1 Na INACTIVATION
Na channels CLOSE] P4 --> P5[PHASE 3 REPOLARISATION
K channels OPEN
K ions RUSH OUT] P5 --> P6[APPROACHES E_K
HYPERPOLARISATION
minus 80 mV] P6 --> P7[PHASE 4 REST
Na K pump restores
gradients at cost of ATP] P7 --> P8[READY FOR NEXT STIMULUS
after refractory period] P2 -. Absolute Refractory .-> P4 P5 -. Relative Refractory .-> P6 ``` --- ### 4.3 Signal Conditioning Cascade (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1[ACQUISITION FRONT END] e1[Electrode Pair] e2[Patient Cable Shielded] end subgraph S2[ANALOG SIGNAL CHAIN] a1[Differential INA Gain 10] a2[Pre Filter HPF 0.05 Hz DC Block] a3[Stage 2 Gain 100] a4[LPF fc 500 Hz 4th order Butterworth] a5[Notch 50 Hz Q 30] a6[Output Driver] end subgraph S3[DIGITAL DOMAIN] d1[Anti Alias LPF] d2[ADC 16 bit 2 kS/s] d3[Microcontroller DSP] end subgraph S4[USER INTERFACE] u1[LCD Waveform Display] u2[Threshold Detection] u3[Conduction Velocity Calculator] end e1 --> e2 --> a1 --> a2 --> a3 --> a4 --> a5 --> a6 --> d1 --> d2 --> d3 --> u1 d3 --> u2 d3 --> u3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *Define resting membrane potential. With the help of the Nernst equation, calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37 °C given [K⁺]ₒᵤₜ = 4 mM and [K⁺]ᵢₙ = 140 mM.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Resting membrane potential is the steady-state potential difference (≈ −70 mV) across the plasma membrane of an excitable cell in the absence of stimulation, maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump and selective ionic permeabilities. - **Equation and substitution (1 mark):** $E_K = (61.54/z)\log_{10}\dfrac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}}$ at 37 °C with z = +1. - **Result (1 mark):** $E_K = 61.54 \times \log_{10}(4/140) = 61.54 \times (-1.544) \approx \mathbf{-95.0\;mV}$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *List any six differences between surface EMG recording and needle EMG recording.* **Model Answer (3 marks — tabular form expected):** | Parameter | Surface EMG | Needle EMG | |---|---|---| | Pickup volume | Large, multiple motor units | Small, 1–3 motor units | | Amplitude | 50 µV – 5 mV | 100 µV – 10 mV | | Bandwidth | 20 – 500 Hz | 20 Hz – 5 kHz | | Pain | Non-invasive | Mild pain / discomfort | | Clinical use | Kinesiology, gait analysis | Neuropathy, myopathy | | Selectivity | Poor spatial resolution | High spatial resolution | *(Any 6 valid contrasting points get full 3 marks; 4 points = 2 marks; ≤ 3 points = 1 mark.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete signal-conditioning chain used to acquire an *evoked nerve action potential* from the median nerve of a human subject. Justify the choice of the low-pass and high-pass filter cut-off frequencies. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the Nernst equation from thermodynamic principles. Using the Nernst equation, compute the equilibrium potential for Cl⁻ at body temperature, given [Cl⁻]ₒᵤₜ = 120 mM and [Cl⁻]ᵢₙ = 4 mM. Comment on the role of Cl⁻ in stabilising the resting potential. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — 7 marks** 1. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Draw the cascade: *Electrode pair → Input protection (Zener diodes + optocoupler) → Instrumentation amplifier (AD620, gain 10) → Pre-filter HPF (0.05 Hz) → Main amp (gain 100) → LPF (10 kHz for ENG) → Notch (50 Hz) → ADC → Microcontroller → Display*. (Refer to Fig 4.1 above.) 2. **Justification of HPF cut-off (2 marks):** High-pass filter at **0.05–0.5 Hz** removes the DC electrode half-cell potential (±300 mV for Ag/AgCl) and slow baseline drift due to respiration and perspiration, without distorting the nerve action potential waveform (which has rise-time of ≈ 100 µs). 3. **Justification of LPF cut-off (2 marks):** Low-pass filter at **5–10 kHz** for ENG removes high-frequency thermal noise and RF interference. Cut-off is set ≈ 5× the highest signal frequency of interest (≈ 2 kHz for a typical sensory nerve volley) to preserve the fast rising edge and prevent *ringing artefacts* (use Butterworth, not Chebyshev, to avoid passband ripple). 4. **CMRR requirement (1 mark):** Instrumentation amplifier with CMRR > **100 dB** is mandatory to reject common-mode mains hum picked up by the body acting as an antenna. --- **(b) — 7 marks** **Derivation (4 marks):** At electrochemical equilibrium, the *Gibbs free energy* of the ion must be equal on both sides of the membrane: $$ \mu_{in} \;=\; \mu_{out} $$ The electrochemical potential of ion X with charge z in a phase of electric potential ψ is: $$ \mu \;=\; \mu^{\circ} + RT\,\ln[X] + zF\psi $$ Equating inside and outside: $$ \mu^{\circ}_{in} + RT\,\ln[X]_{in} + zF\psi_{in} \;=\; \mu^{\circ}_{out} + RT\,\ln[X]_{out} + zF\psi_{out} $$ For the same chemical species, $\mu^{\circ}_{in} = \mu^{\circ}_{out}$. Therefore: $$ RT\,\ln[X]_{in} - RT\,\ln[X]_{out} \;=\; zF(\psi_{out} - \psi_{in}) $$ Defining $E_X = \psi_{in} - \psi_{out}$: $$ E_X \;=\; \frac{RT}{zF}\,\ln\!\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} $$ **Numerical evaluation for Cl⁻ (2 marks):** z = −1, [Cl⁻]ₒᵤₜ = 120 mM, [Cl⁻]ᵢₙ = 4 mM. $$ E_{Cl} \;=\; \frac{61.54}{-1}\,\log_{10}\!\frac{120}{4} \;=\; -61.54 \times 1.477 \;=\; \mathbf{-90.9\;mV} $$ **Role of Cl⁻ (1 mark):** Chloride is passively distributed and acts as a *stabilising* ion. The Cl⁻ permeability $P_{Cl}$ counteracts Na⁺ leak and pulls the resting potential *closer* to E_Cl (−90 mV) than would be the case for a Na⁺/K⁺ system alone, which is one reason the measured resting Vₘ is more negative than the value predicted by the Na⁺/K⁺ Nernst potentials alone. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the four ionic phases of a nerve action potential with reference to the Hodgkin–Huxley gating variables *m*, *h*, and *n*. Sketch a labelled waveform indicating the resting, threshold, peak, and hyperpolarisation potentials. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two stimulating electrodes are placed on the median nerve at the wrist, and recording electrodes are placed 28 cm proximal at the elbow. The onset latency of the compound muscle action potential (CMAP) is 8 ms. Calculate the motor nerve conduction velocity. If the patient is suspected of having *carpal tunnel syndrome*, what change in latency would you expect? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — 7 marks** 1. **Resting phase (Phase 4) (1 mark):** Vₘ ≈ −70 mV. Na⁺ channel gates: activation *m* ≈ 0, inactivation *h* ≈ 1 (closed but available). K⁺ channel gate: *n* ≈ 0 (closed). Conductance dominated by K⁺ leak. 2. **Depolarisation (Phase 0) (2 marks):** When Vₘ crosses −55 mV, *m* rapidly rises from 0 to 1 (m³h rises from 0 to ≈ 0.9) within ≈ 0.2 ms, opening Na⁺ channels. Na⁺ influx drives Vₘ toward E_Na. The positive feedback is captured by the *m³* term in the HH equation $I_{Na} = g_{Na}\,m^3 h\,(V - E_{Na})$. 3. **Repolarisation (Phase 3) (2 marks):** Within ≈ 0.5 ms, *h* falls from 1 to ≈ 0.1, *inactivating* the Na⁺ channel. Simultaneously, *n* rises from 0 to ≈ 0.7–0.8, opening K⁺ channels (gating variable *n⁴*). K⁺ efflux returns Vₘ toward E_K. 4. **Hyperpolarisation (after-potential) (1 mark):** Because *n* decays slowly, K⁺ conductance remains briefly elevated, pulling Vₘ ≈ −80 mV (toward E_K), before returning to −70 mV. 5. **Labelled sketch (1 mark):** Y-axis: Vₘ in mV (mark −80, −70, −55, 0, +30). X-axis: time in ms. Show the four phases as smooth curves with Phase 0 (steep upstroke), Phase 3 (downstroke), undershoot below −70 mV. --- **(b) — 7 marks** **Formula (2 marks):** $$ v \;=\; \frac{\text{distance between stimulating and recording electrodes}}{\text{onset latency}} $$ **Substitution (2 marks):** $$ v \;=\; \frac{0.28\;\text{m}}{8 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s}} \;=\; 35\;\text{m/s} $$ **Normal range comment (1 mark):** Normal median motor nerve conduction velocity in the forearm segment is **49–70 m/s**. A value of 35 m/s is *significantly slowed*, suggestive of demyelination. **Carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) discussion (2 marks):** In CTS, the median nerve is *compressed* under the *flexor retinaculum* at the wrist. The compression causes: - **Focal demyelination** → slowing of conduction *across the carpal tunnel segment* (the segment between wrist-stimulus and thenar-muscle recording). - The motor distal latency (from wrist to muscle) becomes prolonged, often **> 4.5 ms** (normal < 4.0 ms). - The *forearm* conduction velocity (elbow to wrist) may remain relatively normal because the compression is focal. - Therefore the expected change is an **increased distal motor latency** with relatively preserved proximal conduction velocity. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (2024 Scheme):** > 1. **Confusing "nerve action potential" (single axon, intracellular, ≈ 100 mV) with "compound nerve action potential" (surface-recorded, ≈ 1–100 µV, the summed volley of many axons in phase).** A student who says "the ENG amplitude is 100 mV" loses 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to invert the chloride concentrations in the GHK equation.** A common slip that flips the sign of the resting potential. > 3. **Confusing "conduction velocity" with "propagation speed of electricity."** The conduction velocity here is *ionic wave propagation* (1–120 m/s), **not** the speed of electromagnetic signal in vacuum (3 × 10⁸ m/s). > 4. **Not stating the operating temperature in the Nernst calculation.** The 61.54 mV constant is *only* valid at 37 °C. If you don't say "at body temperature," the examiner may deduct ½ mark. > 5. **In block diagrams, drawing a "single-ended amplifier" instead of an "instrumentation amplifier (3 op-amp topology)."** The latter is mandatory for any biopotential recording — the high CMRR (> 100 dB) is *only* achievable with a 3-amp IA. > 6. **Computing conduction velocity using total limb length** instead of the *inter-electrode distance*. Examiners explicitly check the units of the numerator. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Three cardinal voltages of an action potential** — Resting **−70 mV**, Threshold **−55 mV**, Peak **+30 mV** (skeletal/nerve). - **Nernst equation — quick form** (37 °C, monovalent ion): $E_X = 61.54 \log_{10}\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$ mV. - **E_Na ≈ +60 mV**, **E_K ≈ −95 mV**, **E_Cl ≈ −90 mV** — memorise these three. - **Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK)** gives the *resting* Vₘ from the weighted log of permeabilities and concentrations; remember the **Cl⁻ swap**. - **Hodgkin–Huxley** four-state model: Na⁺ activation *m* (fast open), Na⁺ inactivation *h* (slower close), K⁺ activation *n* (slow open). - **All-or-none** principle — sub-threshold stimuli produce only a local graded response; supra-threshold stimuli always produce a full-amplitude AP. - **Refractory periods:** Absolute (no stimulus, any strength, can elicit a response) and Relative (only a stronger stimulus can). - **Acquisition chain** is fixed in order — Electrode → Protection → IA → HPF → Main Amp → LPF → Notch → ADC → Display. - **Bandwidths to remember:** EMG surface 20–500 Hz, EMG needle 20 Hz–5 kHz, ENG 100 Hz–10 kHz, ECG 0.05–150 Hz. - **CMRR > 100 dB** is non-negotiable for any biopotential amplifier — use a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. - **Sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; in practice $f_s \approx 5 f_{max}$ for ENG. - **Conduction velocity** is *distance between stimulating cathode and recording electrode* divided by *onset latency* (in metres and seconds respectively) — answer in **m/s**. - **Normal median motor CV** in the forearm: **49–70 m/s**; < 40 m/s suggests demyelination; **distal motor latency > 4.5 ms** suggests carpal tunnel syndrome. - **Safety standard** for any patient-connected instrument: **IEC 60601-1**, leakage current < **10 µA** (Type CF, applied to heart). - **Electrode–skin impedance** must be reduced below **5 kΩ** at 50 Hz by skin abrasion + conductive gel for clean EMG acquisition.

> - **Topic:** Measurement of Nerve Conduction Velocity Using EMG Acquisition System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Nerve Conduction Velocity (NCV)** is the speed at which an electrical impulse (action potential) propagates along a peripheral motor or sensory nerve fiber. It is a quantitative electrophysiological parameter expressed in **metres per second (m/s)** and is measured by recording the compound muscle action potential (CMAP) or sensory nerve action potential (SNAP) in response to a supramaximal electrical stimulus applied at two anatomically distinct sites along the nerve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Statement (Board-Examiner Preferred Wording):**
> *"Nerve conduction velocity is defined as the ratio of the conduction distance between two stimulation sites on a peripheral nerve to the difference in latencies of the evoked compound action potentials recorded at the corresponding muscle."*

**Electromyography (EMG) Acquisition System** is a bioelectronic instrument chain designed to detect, amplify, filter, digitise, and display the bioelectric activity of skeletal muscle fibres and the electrical responses of peripheral nerves to controlled stimulation. The same hardware, when interfaced with a stimulator module, performs NCV studies (a sub-domain of Clinical Neurophysiology).

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Garden Hose Pulse Test**
> Imagine two water taps (Tap A and Tap B) connected to the same hose. You tap the hose gently at Tap A and feel the vibration reach your hand at the far end after time **t₁**. Then you tap at Tap B (closer to the hand) and the vibration arrives after time **t₂**. Since Tap B is closer, **t₂ < t₁**. The "pulse speed" inside the hose equals **(distance between A and B) ÷ (t₁ − t₂)**.
>
> A peripheral nerve behaves exactly like that hose, the electrical stimulator acts as the "tap," and the recording electrode (over the muscle) acts as the "hand." The latency difference reveals how fast the action potential travels.

## 1.3 Standard Clinical Reference Ranges (Bold Constants)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Normal Motor NCV (Median, Ulnar, Peroneal) | **50 – 70** | **m/s** |
| Normal Sensory NCV | **50 – 65** | **m/s** |
| Stimulus Pulse Width | **0.1 – 0.3** | **ms** |
| Stimulus Intensity (Supramaximal) | **20 – 50** | **mA** |
| Skin-Electrode Impedance Target | **< 5 kΩ** | **Ω** |
| Surface Electrode Material | **Ag/AgCl** | — |
| Amplifier Gain (typical) | **1000 – 100 000** | **×** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Two-Point Stimulation Principle

The two-point (also called **inching** or **two-site**) method is the canonical KTU-board-tested procedure:

**Step 1 — Distal Stimulation**
A supramaximal rectangular pulse is delivered through a bipolar stimulator placed over the nerve at the **distal** site (e.g., wrist for median nerve). The evoked CMAP is recorded by surface electrodes over the target muscle (e.g., abductor pollicis brevis). The **onset latency** $L_1$ is measured from the stimulus artefact to the first negative deflection of CMAP.

**Step 2 — Proximal Stimulation**
The stimulator is moved proximally (e.g., elbow) along the same nerve trunk, keeping the recording electrodes fixed. A second CMAP is recorded and the new latency $L_2$ is noted.

**Step 3 — Distance Measurement**
The surface distance between the two cathodal stimulation points is measured with a flexible measuring tape along the anatomical course of the nerve. This is recorded as $D$ in millimetres.

**Step 4 — Computation**
Because the impulse must travel the extra distance $D$, the latency at the proximal site $L_2$ is always greater than $L_1$. The motor NCV is:

$$\text{NCV} \;=\; \frac{D}{L_2 \;-\; L_1}$$

The denominator $(L_2 - L_1)$ is often called the **delta-latency** or **conduction time across the segment**.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (KTU Logic)

- **Step 1 (Distal)** fixes a reference latency that includes the **neuromuscular junction (NMJ) transmission delay** (~1 ms) and the terminal motor-axon conduction. This delay is **constant**, so it cancels out in the subtraction.
- **Step 2 (Proximal)** adds only the conduction time across the nerve segment — no extra NMJ delay is introduced because the muscle and NMJ are unchanged.
- **Step 3** is a *skin-surface* distance, not the true intra-neural path. For straight nerves (median, ulnar) the error is small (<5%); for curved nerves the value is mildly underestimated.
- **Step 4** yields velocity because the unknown NMJ delay is mathematically eliminated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often divide $D$ by $L_2$ alone, or by $L_1$ alone. This gives *wrong* units of 1/time and an incorrect value. **Always use the difference in latencies.**

## 2.3 Components of the EMG Acquisition System (Signal Chain)

A modern EMG/NCV workstation contains the following cascaded stages:

1. **Stimulus Isolator & Constant-Current Source** — delivers 0–100 mA pulses, optically isolated for patient safety (IEC 60601-1 leakage current < 10 µA).
2. **Stimulating Electrodes** — bipolar, cathode distal, anode proximal.
3. **Subject / Patient** — the nerve-muscle preparation.
4. **Recording (Surface) Electrodes** — Ag/AgCl, active (G1) over muscle belly, reference (G2) over tendon, ground (G) on dorsum of hand.
5. **Differential Pre-Amplifier** — high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (**CMRR ≥ 100 dB**), input impedance ≥ 10 MΩ, gain ×100.
6. **Main Amplifier & Band-Pass Filter** — gain ×1000, band-pass **2 Hz – 10 kHz** for motor studies; **20 Hz – 2 kHz** for sensory.
7. **Notch (Mains) Filter** — 50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz rejection.
8. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — 12–16 bit, sampling ≥ 20 kHz.
9. **Microprocessor / DSP** — automatic latency & amplitude markers.
10. **Display (LCD) & Storage (HDD/Cloud)** — waveform and computed NCV.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Motor Nerve Conduction Velocity | $v$ | $v \;=\; \dfrac{D}{L_2 - L_1}$ | **m·s⁻¹** |
| 2 | Sensory NCV | $v_s$ | $v_s \;=\; \dfrac{D}{L_{\text{onset}}}$ (orthodromic) | **m·s⁻¹** |
| 3 | Distance between cathodes | $D$ | Measured along skin with tape | **mm** |
| 4 | Distal latency | $L_1$ | Stimulus artefact → CMAP onset | **ms** |
| 5 | Proximal latency | $L_2$ | Stimulus artefact → CMAP onset | **ms** |
| 6 | CMAP amplitude | $A_{pp}$ | Peak-to-peak voltage | **mV** |
| 7 | CMAP duration | $t_d$ | First departure → return to baseline | **ms** |
| 8 | Stimulus artefact → onset (terminal latency) | $L_t$ | Includes NMJ delay | **ms** |
| 9 | F-wave latency minimum | $L_F$ | $L_F \;=\; 2L_t + 1$ (empirical) | **ms** |
| 10 | Skin–electrode impedance target | $Z$ | $Z \;<\; 5 \times 10^{3}$ | **Ω** |
| 11 | CMRR (dB) | $\rho$ | $\rho \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | **dB** |
| 12 | Required sampling frequency (Nyquist) | $f_s$ | $f_s \;\geq\; 2 f_{\max}$ | **Hz** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** NCV measurement is the gold-standard bedside electrophysiological test for **carpal tunnel syndrome, diabetic peripheral neuropathy, Guillain-Barré syndrome, Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease, and traumatic nerve injury**. Biomedical engineers design the stimulators, isolation amplifiers, and the artefact-rejection firmware used in every neurology clinic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of Motor NCV (Two-Point Method)

Let the nerve segment between the two stimulation cathodes be of length $D$. Let $v$ be the (assumed constant) conduction velocity along this segment. The action potential must traverse this segment in addition to the fixed distal pathway.

**Define:**
- $t_d$ = time from stimulus to first depolarisation at the NMJ (fixed for a given distal site).
- $t_a$ = time to traverse the additional proximal segment of length $D$.
- $L_1$ = distal latency measured at CMAP onset (includes $t_d$).
- $L_2$ = proximal latency measured at CMAP onset (includes $t_d + t_a$).

**From the definition of velocity:**

$$v \;=\; \frac{D}{t_a}$$

**Express the latency observations:**

$$L_1 \;=\; t_d \quad \text{(distal — impulse reaches muscle without traversing segment }D\text{)}$$

$$L_2 \;=\; t_d \;+\; t_a$$

**Subtract to eliminate the unknown NMJ delay $t_d$:**

$$L_2 \;-\; L_1 \;=\; (t_d + t_a) \;-\; t_d \;=\; t_a$$

**Substitute back into the velocity equation:**

$$v \;=\; \frac{D}{L_2 - L_1}$$

**Boxed KTU Final Form:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{NCV} \;=\; v \;=\; \frac{D}{L_2 - L_1}\;\;\bigl[\text{m·s}^{-1}\bigr]\;}$$

Because $D$ is measured in **mm** and latencies in **ms**, the conversion factor $10^{-3}/10^{-3} = 1$ cancels, so the numerical ratio directly gives **m/s**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Question:** During median-nerve motor NCV study on a 35-year-old subject, the distal stimulation at the wrist produced a CMAP onset latency of $L_1 = 3.6\;\text{ms}$. Proximal stimulation at the elbow produced $L_2 = 7.6\;\text{ms}$. The surface distance between the two cathode positions is $D = 200\;\text{mm}$. Calculate the motor NCV and state whether it lies within the normal clinical range.

**Solution (explicit, examiner-friendly steps):**

**Step 1 — Identify given values and convert to consistent units.**

$$D = 200\;\text{mm} = 0.200\;\text{m}$$

$$L_1 = 3.6\;\text{ms} = 3.6 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s}$$

$$L_2 = 7.6\;\text{ms} = 7.6 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the latency difference (conduction time across the segment).**

$$\Delta t \;=\; L_2 - L_1 \;=\; 7.6 \times 10^{-3} \;-\; 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 4.0 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the NCV formula.**

$$\text{NCV} \;=\; \frac{D}{\Delta t} \;=\; \frac{0.200}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{NCV} \;=\; \frac{0.200}{0.0040} \;=\; 50\;\text{m·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Clinical interpretation.**

Since the normal range for upper-limb motor NCV is **50 – 70 m/s**, the value of $50\;\text{m/s}$ is at the **lower normal limit**. Any value **< 50 m/s** would indicate demyelinating pathology (e.g., carpal tunnel syndrome, diabetic neuropathy).

$$\boxed{\;\text{NCV}_{\text{median}} \;=\; 50\;\text{m·s}^{-1}\;-\;\text{Lower normal limit}\;}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choice of Method

| Study Type | Stimulation Site | Recording Site | Latency Used | Formula | Indication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Motor NCV | Distal & Proximal nerve | Muscle (CMAP) | $L_2 - L_1$ | $D / (L_2 - L_1)$ | Motor axon integrity |
| Sensory NCV (orthodromic) | Digit → proximal | Over nerve trunk | $L_{\text{onset}}$ | $D / L_{\text{onset}}$ | Sensory axon integrity |
| Sensory NCV (antidromic) | Proximal → digit | Over digit | $L_{\text{peak}}$ | $D / L_{\text{peak}}$ | Sensory axon integrity |
| F-wave study | Distal only | Muscle (late response) | $L_F$ | Empirical | Proximal/root pathology |
| H-reflex | Popliteal fossa | Soleus muscle | $L_H$ | $D / L_H$ | S1 root / monosynaptic reflex |

## 3.4 Mathematical Note — Why the NMJ Delay Cancels

The terminal latency $L_t$ at the distal site is given by:

$$L_t \;=\; \frac{d_{\text{distal}}}{v} \;+\; t_{\text{NMJ}} \;+\; t_{\text{muscle}}$$

Where $t_{\text{NMJ}}$ is the synaptic delay ($\approx 1$ ms) and $t_{\text{muscle}}$ is the muscle-fibre depolarisation delay. Since these two delays are present in **both** $L_1$ and $L_2$ (the recording electrodes do not move), the subtraction operation $L_2 - L_1$ removes them, yielding a "clean" nerve-conduction time:

$$L_2 - L_1 \;=\; \frac{D}{v} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; v \;=\; \frac{D}{L_2 - L_1}$$

This cancellation is the algebraic reason the two-point method is mathematically valid.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Nerve Conduction Velocity. Mention its clinical significance.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — Examiner's Key Pattern):**

Nerve Conduction Velocity (NCV) is the speed at which an action potential travels along a peripheral nerve fibre. It is mathematically defined as the ratio of the distance between two stimulation points on a nerve to the difference in latencies of the compound muscle action potentials recorded at the muscle. **(1.5 marks)**

$$\text{NCV} \;=\; \frac{D}{L_2 - L_1}\;\;[\text{m·s}^{-1}]$$

**Clinical significance:** NCV measurement is the gold-standard electrophysiological test for evaluating peripheral neuropathies. It helps in the diagnosis of **demyelinating conditions** such as Guillain-Barré syndrome (markedly reduced NCV < 30 m/s), **axonal degenerations** (normal NCV but reduced amplitude), **nerve entrapments** like carpal tunnel syndrome, and traumatic nerve injuries. **(1.5 marks)**

### **Q2. List any six functional blocks of an EMG acquisition system used for NCV measurement.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — Examiner's Key Pattern):**

Any six of the following:

1. Stimulus isolator with constant-current source
2. Bipolar stimulating electrodes (cathode + anode)
3. Surface recording electrodes (active, reference, ground) — Ag/AgCl
4. Differential pre-amplifier (high CMRR ≥ 100 dB, high input impedance)
5. Main amplifier with band-pass filter (2 Hz – 10 kHz)
6. 50 Hz notch filter for mains rejection
7. Analog-to-Digital Converter (12–16 bit)
8. Microprocessor / DSP for latency detection and display
9. LCD / storage module

**(0.5 marks each × 6 = 3 marks)**

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — Students forget to convert mm to m or ms to s. Always show the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **NMJ delay not cancelled** — Using $D/L_2$ alone is a 4-mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong electrode labelling** — Cathode must be placed *distal* to anode; reversal causes anodal hyperpolarisation and artefactual latency shifts.
> 4. **CMRR confusion** — Stating "CMRR = 100" instead of "100 dB" loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing clinical interpretation** — Always end the answer with a one-line clinical comment.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) Describe the two-point stimulation method for measurement of motor nerve conduction velocity with a neat block diagram of the EMG acquisition system.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) The following data are recorded during a median-nerve motor NCV study on a patient:**
- Distal latency, $L_1 = 4.2$ ms
- Proximal latency, $L_2 = 8.6$ ms
- Distance between stimulation sites, $D = 220$ mm

**Calculate the motor nerve conduction velocity and comment on its clinical status.** **(7 marks)**

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
The two-point method stimulates the same peripheral nerve at two different anatomical locations — one distal and one proximal — while the recording electrodes over the target muscle remain fixed. The difference in CMAP onset latencies is the time taken by the action potential to traverse the nerve segment between the two cathodes. The NMJ and muscle delays, being identical in both recordings, cancel out in the subtraction.

**2. Procedure (3 marks):**

- Place bipolar stimulating electrodes with the **cathode distal** to the anode along the nerve course.
- Apply a **supramaximal stimulus** (0.1–0.3 ms rectangular pulse, intensity 20–50 mA) at the **distal site** (e.g., wrist for median nerve).
- Record the evoked CMAP using surface electrodes placed over the muscle belly (active G1) and tendon (reference G2), with a ground electrode on the dorsum of the hand.
- Mark the **onset latency** $L_1$ — the time from stimulus artefact to the first negative departure of the CMAP.
- Reposition the stimulator proximally (e.g., at the elbow) and re-stimulate without moving the recording electrodes.
- Mark the new onset latency $L_2$.
- Measure the surface distance $D$ between the two cathodes using a flexible tape.

**3. Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the EMG acquisition block diagram in Section 4.1. Trace the signal: Stimulator → Bipolar Stimulating Electrodes → Patient → Surface Recording Electrodes → Differential Pre-Amplifier → Main Amplifier + Band-Pass Filter → Notch Filter → ADC → DSP → Microprocessor → Display.

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert to SI units (1 mark):**

$$D = 220\;\text{mm} = 0.220\;\text{m}$$

$$L_1 = 4.2\;\text{ms} = 4.2 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s}$$

$$L_2 = 8.6\;\text{ms} = 8.6 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute delta latency (2 marks):**

$$\Delta t = L_2 - L_1 = 8.6 - 4.2 = 4.4\;\text{ms} = 4.4 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the NCV formula (2 marks):**

$$\text{NCV} = \frac{D}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.220}{4.4 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{NCV} = \frac{0.220}{0.0044} = 50\;\text{m·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Clinical interpretation (2 marks):**

The normal range for upper-limb motor NCV is **50 – 70 m/s**. The calculated value of **50 m/s lies at the lower limit of normal**, suggestive of **borderline / mildly demyelinating median-nerve involvement**, possibly early carpal tunnel syndrome. Further tests (sensory NCV, F-wave) are recommended for confirmation.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the role of each block in the EMG acquisition system used for nerve conduction velocity measurement. Why is a high CMRR essential in the differential pre-amplifier?** **(7 marks)**

**(b) With a neat sketch, describe the placement of stimulating and recording electrodes for median-nerve motor NCV study. State any four precautions to be taken during the recording.** **(7 marks)**

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**1. Block-by-block role (5 marks):**

| Block | Role |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Stimulus Isolator** | Provides galvanic isolation between mains-powered stimulator and the patient; ensures patient-safety leakage current < 10 µA (IEC 60601-1). |
| **Constant-Current Source** | Delivers a stimulus whose current is independent of skin-electrode impedance variations, ensuring a reproducible depolarisation of the nerve. |
| **Stimulating Electrodes** | Two surface electrodes (cathode + anode) deliver the pulse transcutaneously to the underlying nerve trunk. |
| **Recording Electrodes** | Ag/AgCl surface electrodes pick up the low-amplitude (µV–mV) CMAP signal with minimal polarisation artefact. |
| **Differential Pre-Amplifier** | Amplifies the difference between G1 and G2 while rejecting common-mode noise (mains hum, environmental interference). |
| **Main Amplifier + Band-Pass Filter** | Provides further gain and restricts the bandwidth to **2 Hz – 10 kHz** to remove DC offset and high-frequency noise. |
| **Notch Filter** | Provides sharp **50 Hz** rejection of mains interference. |
| **ADC** | Digitises the analog waveform for digital processing. |
| **DSP / Microprocessor** | Automatically detects onset latency, peak amplitude, and computes NCV. |
| **Display / Storage** | Visualises the waveform and stores patient records. |

**2. Why high CMRR is essential (2 marks):**

The EMG signal is of the order of **microvolts to millivolts**, while environmental electromagnetic interference (50 Hz mains, RF, electrostatic pickup) can be several volts. Because both the signal and the interference are picked up nearly equally by both surface electrodes (G1 and G2), they appear as **common-mode** voltages. A differential amplifier with high CMRR (≥ 100 dB) rejects these common-mode voltages while amplifying the differential bioelectric signal, thereby extracting a clean EMG waveform from a noisy environment.

Mathematically:

$$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$$

A CMRR of 100 dB means the amplifier rejects common-mode signals **100 000 times** more than it amplifies the differential signal.

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**1. Electrode placement diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the functional block view in Section 4.3. Stimulating electrodes — cathode on the **wrist** (distal) over the median nerve, anode 2–3 cm proximal. Recording electrodes — active (G1) on the **belly of abductor pollicis brevis**, reference (G2) on the **base of the thumb / tendon**, ground (G) on the **dorsum of the hand**. Then the stimulator is moved to the **elbow** (proximal) for the second stimulation.

**2. Four precautions during recording (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Skin preparation** — Clean the skin with alcohol, lightly abrade with gel, and ensure **skin-electrode impedance < 5 kΩ** to minimise artefact.
2. **Cathode orientation** — Always keep the **cathode distal** to the anode to avoid anodal hyperpolarisation which can block the action potential.
3. **Supramaximal stimulus** — Gradually increase stimulus intensity until the CMAP amplitude reaches a plateau, then add 20% extra to ensure all axons are recruited.
4. **Patient relaxation and temperature** — Keep the limb **warm (≥ 32 °C skin temperature)** because cooling slows conduction and gives falsely low NCV values. The patient must be relaxed to avoid EMG contamination.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NCV is not a velocity of a single ion**, but the *propagation speed of the compound action potential* along a peripheral nerve trunk.
- The **golden formula** for two-point motor NCV is $v = D / (L_2 - L_1)$. **Memorise this verbatim** — it appears in almost every KTU board paper.
- Always place the **cathode distal** to the anode; reversal causes anodal block.
- The **CMRR of the pre-amplifier must be ≥ 100 dB** to reject 50 Hz mains interference.
- **Band-pass filter setting** for motor NCV = **2 Hz – 10 kHz**; for sensory NCV = **20 Hz – 2 kHz**.
- The **NMJ delay (~1 ms)** and the **muscle depolarisation delay** cancel out in the two-point method because the recording electrodes do not move between stimulations.
- Always perform the calculation in **SI units** (metres and seconds) to avoid silly mark deductions.
- Normal upper-limb motor NCV range: **50 – 70 m/s**; values **< 50 m/s** indicate demyelinating neuropathy; values **< 30 m/s** suggest severe demyelination.
- A **reduced CMAP amplitude with normal NCV** points to *axonal degeneration*; a **prolonged latency with reduced NCV** points to *demyelination*.
- Always state a **clinical interpretation** in the final line of any numerical answer — examiners allocate up to 2 marks for this.
- Keep the limb **warm (≥ 32 °C)**; cold limbs slow conduction and give misleading results.
- The **stimulus artefact** must be clearly visible at time = 0 on the trace to allow accurate latency marking.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electroencephalography (EEG) — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EEG (Electroencephalography)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique that records the spontaneous electrical activity of the brain over a period of time, using multiple electrodes placed on the scalp. The resulting trace is called an **electroencephalogram**, and it represents the summed postsynaptic graded potentials of populations of cortical neurons (primarily pyramidal cells of layers III and V), detected as tiny voltage fluctuations at the scalp surface.

| **Parameter** | **Typical Value** |
|---|---|
| Signal amplitude | **1 – 100 µV** (commonly 10 – 50 µV at scalp) |
| Frequency range | **0.5 – 100 Hz** (clinical interest: 0.5 – 30 Hz) |
| Electrodes used | Ag/AgCl disc electrodes (standard) |
| Reference standard | International **10–20 electrode placement system** |
| Output impedance of scalp | ~ 5 kΩ (must be reduced to < 5 kΩ by skin prep) |

## 1.2 Evoked Potentials (EP) — The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> An **Evoked Potential (EP)** is the time-locked electrical response of the central nervous system to a specific external sensory stimulus (visual, auditory, somatosensory, or cognitive). Because the EP signal is buried within the much larger ongoing background EEG, it must be extracted using **time-domain signal averaging** across many stimulus repetitions.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — "Hearing a Whisper in a Stadium":** Imagine sitting in a packed cricket stadium where 50,000 people are shouting (this is your **background EEG noise**, ~ 50 µV). Suddenly one friend whispers your name (this is the **evoked potential**, ~ 1–5 µV). You cannot hear the whisper over the roar. But if your friend whispers your name **1,000 times in exact sync** with a flash, and you *add up* all the recordings, the random shouting cancels out while the whispers reinforce each other — this is **signal averaging**.
>
> **Analogy 2 — "EEG vs. EP — Photograph vs. Flash Snapshot":** A normal EEG is like a **continuous video** of the brain's idle chatter. An Evoked Potential is a **single still photograph** taken at a precise instant after a flash (stimulus) — the flash captures a frozen moment the video alone could not reveal.

## 1.4 The Three Sub-Topics of the Experiment

| **Type of EP** | **Stimulus Modality** | **Sensory Pathway Tested** | **Principal Landmark Wave** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VEP** (Visual EP) | Alternating checkerboard pattern reversal | Retina → Optic nerve → LGN → Primary visual cortex (Oz) | **P100** (positive peak at ~ 100 ms) |
| **BAEP / AEP** (Brainstem Auditory EP) | Repetitive click / tone burst | Cochlea → CN VIII → Pons → Midbrain (inferior colliculus) | **Wave V** (~ 5–6 ms) |
| **MLAEP** (Middle-Latency AEP) | Click stimulus | Primary auditory cortex (temporal lobe) | **Na, Pa** (~ 18–30 ms) |
| **LLAEP** (Long-Latency AEP) | Click / tone | Auditory cortex + association areas | **N1, P2, N2** (50–300 ms) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 lab examination, **VEP and AEP (Brainstem)** are the two modalities most commonly asked. The student must record the raw EEG, superimpose a stimulus trigger, and demonstrate the time-locked averaging process.

# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 10–20 International Electrode Placement System

The **10–20 system** is the universally accepted method for scalp electrode placement, so named because electrodes are positioned at **10 % or 20 % of the total distance** between standard anatomical landmarks on the skull.

**Key anatomical landmarks (always measured first):**
- **Nasion (Nz)** → depression at the top of the nose
- **Inion (Iz)** → bony prominence at the back of the skull
- **Pre-auricular points (A1, A2)** → in front of each ear tragus

**Reference labels:**
- **Fp** = Frontal pole
- **F** = Frontal
- **C** = Central
- **P** = Parietal
- **T** = Temporal
- **O** = Occipital
- **Odd numbers** = Left hemisphere
- **Even numbers** = Right hemisphere
- **"z"** = midline (zero)

### 2.1.1 Standard Electrode Montages for VEP and AEP

| **Test** | **Active Electrode** | **Reference Electrode** | **Ground Electrode** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VEP (Pattern Reversal)** | **Oz** (occipital midline) | **Fz** (frontal midline) | **Cz** (vertex) |
| **AEP / BAEP** | **Cz** (vertex) — for vertex-recorded BAEP | **A1 or A2** (earlobe / mastoid) | **Fpz** (frontal pole) |
| **Long-Latency AEP** | **Cz** or **C3 / C4** | **A1 / A2** (linked earlobes) | **Fpz** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for VEP recording:** *"**O**ccipital looks at the eyes → **F**rontal is reference, **C**entral is ground"*.

## 2.2 EEG Rhythm Classification — Foundation for EP Analysis

| **Rhythm** | **Frequency** | **Amplitude** | **State of Subject** | **Origin (approx.)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | 20 – 200 µV | Deep sleep (infants, coma) | Thalamus, cortex |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz | 5 – 100 µV | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation | Hippocampus |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz | 20 – 60 µV | Awake, relaxed, eyes closed | Occipital / parietal |
| **Mu (μ)** | 8 – 13 Hz | < 50 µV | Awake, rest, motor imagery | Sensorimotor cortex |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz | < 20 µV | Active thinking, alert | Frontal / central |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz | Very low | Higher cognitive tasks, perception binding | Cortical networks |

## 2.3 Derivation of SNR Improvement by Signal Averaging

This is the **single most important equation** for any Evoked Potential experiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption:** The signal (S) is **time-locked** to the stimulus and is therefore **deterministic** — when N sweeps are added, the signal adds **linearly**. The background noise (n) is **random (zero-mean, uncorrelated)** — when N sweeps are added, the noise adds as the **square root of N**.

Let,
- $S$ = amplitude of the time-locked evoked potential
- $\sigma_n$ = standard deviation of the random background EEG noise
- $N$ = number of sweeps averaged

After **one sweep**:
$$SNR_{in} = \dfrac{S}{\sigma_n}$$

After **N sweeps** (coherent addition of signal, incoherent addition of noise):
- Summed signal = $N \cdot S$
- Summed noise RMS = $\sigma_n \sqrt{N}$

Therefore:
$$SNR_{out} = \dfrac{N \cdot S}{\sigma_n \sqrt{N}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \dfrac{S}{\sigma_n} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_{in}$$

$$\boxed{SNR_{out} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_{in}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **In decibels (dB):** $\text{Improvement (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(N) \text{ dB}$
>
> - 100 sweeps → +20 dB improvement
> - 1000 sweeps → +30 dB improvement
> - 4096 sweeps → +36.1 dB improvement

## 2.4 The Bandwidth-Time Product and Time Constant

A practical bioamplifier does not amplify **all** frequencies equally. The gain rolls off at the high and low ends, which we describe by the **lower cutoff (f$_L$)** and **upper cutoff (f$_H$)**.

The **low-frequency time constant** is:
$$\tau = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_L}$$

The **time-domain response** to a step of amplitude $V_0$ at a high-pass filter is:
$$V_{out}(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

If the sweep duration is $T_s$, the percentage baseline drift caused by the high-pass filter at the end of the sweep is:
$$\% \text{ Drift} = \left( 1 - e^{-T_s / \tau} \right) \times 100\%$$

## 2.5 Sampling and Quantization in Digital EEG

The digitized signal must obey the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**:
$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

Typical settings for clinical EEG / EP machines:

| **Setting** | **EEG (Routine)** | **BAEP** | **VEP (Pattern)** | **LLAEP** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low-cut filter (Hz) | 0.5 | 100 – 150 | 1 | 0.1 |
| High-cut filter (Hz) | 70 | 3000 | 100 | 70 |
| Sampling rate | 256 – 512 Hz | 20 – 50 kHz | 1 – 2 kHz | 500 Hz |
| Sweep duration | 1 s | 10 – 15 ms | 300 ms | 500 ms |
| # of sweeps averaged | – | 1000 – 2000 | 100 – 200 | 50 – 100 |
| Stimulus rate | – | 10 – 30 Hz | 1 – 2 Hz | 0.5 – 2 Hz |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Use / Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SNR improvement | $SNR_{out} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_{in}$ | How many sweeps to average |
| 2 | SNR gain in dB | $\Delta SNR = 10 \log_{10}(N)$ | Express averaging in decibels |
| 3 | Noise reduction factor | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Background noise shrinks by this factor |
| 4 | Nyquist sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate |
| 5 | Time constant | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_L}$ | Low-frequency response |
| 6 | High-pass drift | $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ | Baseline drift in EP sweeps |
| 7 | RMS noise after N averages | $\sigma_{out} = \dfrac{\sigma_{in}}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Residual noise level |
| 8 | Inter-peak latency | $\Delta t = t_{late} - t_{early}$ | Diagnostic for demyelination |
| 9 | Inter-amplitude ratio | $V_5 / V_1$ | BAEP diagnostic index |
| 10 | Maximum frequency alias-free | $f_{Nyq} = f_s / 2$ | Practical upper recording limit |

# 📐 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Why Averaging Works Mathematically

We begin with a single recorded sweep $x_k(t)$ that contains the wanted EP signal $s(t)$ buried in random noise $n_k(t)$:
$$x_k(t) = s(t) + n_k(t)$$

**Step 1 — The time-locked nature of the signal:**
Because the stimulus triggers the recording at $t=0$ on every sweep, the signal $s(t)$ is **identical on every trial**. The noise $n_k(t)$, however, is a different random realization on each sweep $k$.

**Step 2 — Add N independent sweeps together:**
$$X(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} x_k(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} s(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{N} n_k(t)$$

Since $s(t)$ does not depend on $k$, it factors out:
$$X(t) = N \cdot s(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{N} n_k(t)$$

**Step 3 — Average by dividing by N (what the instrument actually does):**
$$\bar{x}(t) = \dfrac{1}{N} X(t) = s(t) + \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} n_k(t)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the expected value (mean) and the variance:**
Because each $n_k$ has zero mean ($E[n_k] = 0$) and is uncorrelated with other samples of noise:
$$E[\bar{x}(t)] = s(t) + \dfrac{1}{N} \cdot 0 = s(t)$$

The variance of the average of $N$ independent, identically distributed noise samples is:
$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \dfrac{\sigma_n^2}{N}$$

So the standard deviation of the residual noise is:
$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma_n}{\sqrt{N}}$$

**Step 5 — Form the output SNR:**
$$SNR_{out} = \dfrac{\text{signal}}{\text{noise}} = \dfrac{s}{\sigma_n / \sqrt{N}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sigma_n}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \; SNR_{out} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_{in}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** This is why we can extract a **0.5 µV** evoked potential from beneath a **30 µV** background EEG — but only by averaging hundreds of trials. Going from 100 to 10 000 sweeps only doubles the SNR (a factor of 10 in N gives only a factor of 3.16 in SNR), so there are diminishing returns.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Computing Required Sweeps

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A patient has a background EEG of $\sigma_{in} = 30 \text{ µV}$ RMS. The expected P100 VEP amplitude is $S = 5 \text{ µV}$. The display requires an output SNR of at least 4 to clearly identify the waveform. Find the minimum number of sweeps $N$ to average.

**Step 1 — Compute input SNR:**
$$SNR_{in} = \dfrac{S}{\sigma_{in}} = \dfrac{5}{30} = 0.1667$$

**Step 2 — Set up the requirement:**
$$SNR_{out} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_{in} \geq 4$$
$$\sqrt{N} \geq \dfrac{4}{0.1667} = 24$$
$$N \geq 576$$

**Step 3 — Round up to a practical engineering value:**
$$\boxed{N = 600 \text{ sweeps (minimum)}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify noise reduction factor:**
$$\sigma_{out} = \dfrac{30}{\sqrt{600}} = 1.225 \text{ µV}$$

A 5 µV signal on 1.225 µV noise is clearly visible.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Time Constant and Baseline Drift

> **Problem:** A VEP machine is set with a low-cut (high-pass) frequency of $f_L = 1 \text{ Hz}$ and a sweep duration of $T_s = 300 \text{ ms}$. Compute (a) the time constant $\tau$ and (b) the percentage drift at the end of one sweep.

**Step 1 — Time constant from the high-pass cutoff:**
$$\tau = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_L} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 1}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 0.159 \text{ s} = 159 \text{ ms}}$$

**Step 2 — Drift at end of sweep $t = T_s = 0.3 \text{ s}$:**
$$\dfrac{V_{out}(T_s)}{V_0} = e^{-T_s / \tau} = e^{-0.3 / 0.159}$$

$$= e^{-1.886} = 0.1520$$

So only **15.2 %** of the step remains; the baseline has drooped by:
$$\boxed{\% \text{ Drift} = (1 - 0.1520) \times 100\% \approx 84.8\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A 1 Hz low-cut distorts slow cortical DC shifts. For LLAEP, $f_L$ is set to 0.1 Hz (so $\tau$ = 1.59 s, drift < 17 % over 300 ms) to preserve the slow N2/P3 components.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — BAEP Inter-Peak Latency

> **Problem:** A brainstem AEP trace shows Wave I at $t_I = 1.7 \text{ ms}$ and Wave V at $t_V = 5.9 \text{ ms}$. The stimulus rate was 11.1 Hz, click intensity 70 dB nHL. Compute the **central conduction time (CCT)**.

**Step 1 — Formula for inter-peak latency (IPL):**
$$CCT = t_V - t_I$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$CCT = 5.9 - 1.7 = 4.2 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compare to normative data:**

| **Parameter** | **Normal Adult Mean ± SD** | **This Patient** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wave I absolute latency | 1.7 ± 0.15 ms | 1.7 ms | Normal (peripheral auditory nerve) |
| Wave V absolute latency | 5.7 ± 0.25 ms | 5.9 ms | Borderline-normal |
| I–V inter-peak latency (CCT) | 4.0 ± 0.4 ms | **4.2 ms** | Upper limit of normal → no central demyelination |
| I–III inter-peak latency | 2.1 ± 0.15 ms | – | Afferent / lower brainstem |
| III–V inter-peak latency | 1.9 ± 0.18 ms | – | Upper brainstem |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 4 — Aliasing Check

> **Problem:** You sample the EEG at $f_s = 200$ Hz. Will a 50 Hz mains component alias?

**Step 1 — Nyquist frequency:**
$$f_{Nyq} = \dfrac{f_s}{2} = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Since 50 Hz < 100 Hz**, mathematically it will not alias, but it will pass through and contaminate the recording.

**Step 3 — Solution:** Use a **notch filter at 50 Hz** (or 60 Hz in some countries) to remove the line interference.

> [!WARNING]
> If the sampling rate were 80 Hz and the interference 50 Hz, the **aliased frequency** would appear at $|50 - 80| = 30$ Hz, which lies inside the EEG band and is impossible to remove by filtering later. **Always $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.**

## 3.6 The VEP / AEP Waveform Nomenclature — A Decoder Table

| **Label** | **Polarity** | **Typical Latency (ms)** | **Anatomical / Functional Correlate** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VEP N75** | Negative (up in -ve convention) | ~ 75 | Striate cortex (V1) initial excitation |
| **VEP P100** | Positive | ~ 100 | V1 / V2 — pattern-sensitive, **most diagnostic** |
| **VEP N145** | Negative | ~ 145 | V1 / extrastriate — motion-sensitive |
| **BAEP Wave I** | – | 1.5 | Distal auditory nerve (extracranial) |
| **BAEP Wave III** | – | 3.7 | Pons / superior olive |
| **BAEP Wave V** | – | 5.7 | **Inferior colliculus** — most stable, robust |
| **AEP Na** | Negative | 18 | Mesencephalic reticular formation |
| **AEP Pa** | Positive | 30 | Primary auditory cortex (temporal) |
| **AEP N1** | Negative | 100 | Auditory cortex (Tl, T2) |
| **AEP P2** | Positive | 180 | Auditory association cortex |
| **AEP N2 / P300** | Mixed | 250 – 400 | Cognitive / attention-dependent |

# 🧩 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete EEG / EP Acquisition Chain — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Brain - Bioelectric Source] --> B[SCALP Electrode 10-20 system]
    B --> C[Pre-amplifier INA118 INA128]
    C --> D[Driven Right Leg DRL Circuit]
    D --> E[High-pass Filter fL 0.5 Hz]
    E --> F[Notch Filter 50 Hz line removal]
    F --> G[Low-pass Filter fH 30 Hz for EEG or 3 kHz for BAEP]
    G --> H[Programmable Gain Amplifier PGA]
    H --> I[16-bit ADC 1 kSps to 50 kSps]
    I --> J[Microprocessor / DSP TMS320C5505]
    J --> K[Trigger Synchronization Unit]
    K --> L[Stimulus Generator Checkerboard or Click]
    L --> M[Display and Storage]
    J --> N[Signal Averager Running Sum of N sweeps]
    N --> O[Final Evoked Potential Waveform P100 or Wave V]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow is strictly left-to-right. The patient receives a sensory stimulus (block **L**). The brain generates a time-locked EP (block **A**). The electrodes pick it up (block **B**), the analog front-end cleans it (blocks **C–H**), the ADC digitizes it (block **I**), and the DSP aligns and averages it against the trigger from block **K** to produce the final clean EP in block **O**.

## 4.2 Signal-Averaging Algorithm — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start new acquisition session] --> B[Initialize buffer N 0 and sweep counter i 0]
    B --> C[Wait for stimulus trigger from photic or auditory unit]
    C --> D[Acquire one sweep of EEG 300 ms for VEP, 10 ms for BAEP]
    D --> E[Add sample by sample into running buffer]
    E --> F[Increment sweep counter i i+1]
    F --> G{i greater than equal to N target}
    G -- No --> C
    G -- Yes --> H[Divide running buffer by N to compute mean]
    H --> I[Compute SNR and residual noise]
    I --> J[Display final averaged EP waveform with peak markers]
    J --> K[Save trace and print report]
```

## 4.3 The 10-20 Electrode Map — Mermaid Top-Down Scalp View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRONT
        A1[Fp1] --- A2[Fpz] --- A3[Fp2]
    end
    A1 --- B1[F7]
    A3 --- B2[F8]
    A2 --- C1[Fz]
    B1 --- C1
    B2 --- C1
    C1 --- D1[Cz]
    D1 --- E1[Pz]
    E1 --- F1[Oz]
    B1 --- G1[T3]
    B2 --- G2[T4]
    G1 --- H1[T5]
    G2 --- H2[T6]
    H1 --- I1[O1]
    H2 --- I2[O2]
    I1 --- F1
    I2 --- F1
    H1 --- J1[P3]
    H2 --- J2[P4]
    J1 --- E1
    J2 --- E1
    B1 --- K1[C3]
    B2 --- K2[C4]
    K1 --- D1
    K2 --- D1
    G1 --- K1
    G2 --- K2
```

> [!TIP]
> **VEP / AEP recording sites at a glance:**
> - **VEP active site:** **Oz** (occipital midline, on top of the visual cortex at the back of the head)
> - **BAEP active site:** **Cz** (vertex, on top of the head)
> - **BAEP reference site:** **A1 or A2** (earlobes / mastoid)

# 🎯 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define Evoked Potential. Why is signal averaging required to extract it from the background EEG?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** An Evoked Potential (EP) is the time-locked electrical response of the central nervous system to a specific external sensory stimulus (visual, auditory or somatosensory). It is a small, reproducible waveform that occurs at a fixed latency after the stimulus.
2. **Why averaging is required (2 marks):** The amplitude of an EP is only **0.1 – 20 µV**, while the background EEG that overlies it is **20 – 100 µV** — about 5–50 times larger. The EP cannot be seen by inspection. By repeating the stimulus N times and time-locked averaging, the **signal adds coherently (× N)** while the random noise adds only as **√N**, giving an **SNR improvement of √N**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Name and locate the principal recording electrode for (a) Pattern Reversal VEP and (b) Brainstem AEP using the 10-20 system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Test | Active Electrode | Location | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| (a) **VEP** | **Oz** | Midline of occiput, 10 % above inion | Fz |
| (b) **BAEP** | **Cz** | Vertex (top of head) | A1 / A2 (earlobe) |

The choice of **Oz** for VEP exploits its proximity to the **primary visual cortex (V1)** in the occipital lobe, and the choice of **Cz** for BAEP gives a far-field, vertex-positive recording that captures all five brainstem waves (I–V).
