## 📕 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — VEP FOCUS (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete instrumentation chain for the **acquisition and analysis of Visual Evoked Potentials (VEP)**. Mention the role of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A pattern-reversal VEP is recorded on a patient. The background EEG noise has an RMS value of $\sigma_n = 40$ µV. The expected P100 peak amplitude is $S = 4$ µV. Find **(i)** the input SNR, **(ii)** the number of sweeps required to obtain an output SNR of at least 3, and **(iii)** the resulting residual RMS noise. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a): 7 marks

**Block diagram (4 marks):**

```
Brain → Electrode Oz → INA Pre-Amp → Driven Right Leg
      → High-Pass (1 Hz) → Notch 50 Hz → Low-Pass (100 Hz)
      → Gain × 10000 → ADC → DSP → Trigger Sync
      → Pattern Stimulator (Checkerboard) → Averager → Display
```

**Role of each block (3 marks):**

| # | **Block** | **Function** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scalp electrode (Oz + Fz) | Pick up micro-volt cortical signal, reject common-mode via differential recording |
| 2 | Instrumentation amplifier (INA128) | High CMRR (> 100 dB), high input impedance (> 10¹² Ω), gain × 10 |
| 3 | Driven Right Leg (DRL) | Actively drives patient body to cancel mains interference, improves safety |
| 4 | High-pass filter (1 Hz) | Blocks DC skin-potential drift |
| 5 | Notch filter (50 Hz) | Removes India-specific mains line interference |
| 6 | Low-pass filter (100 Hz) | Anti-aliasing + bandwidth limiting for VEP |
| 7 | Programmable gain (× 1000) | Brings µV signal up to 0–5 V ADC range |
| 8 | 16-bit ADC (≥ 2 kS/s) | Digitises for digital averaging |
| 9 | Trigger unit | Marks $t = 0$ of each pattern reversal |
| 10 | DSP averager | Adds N sweeps coherently, outputs clean VEP |
| 11 | Display | Shows P100, N75, N145 with latency markers |

**Stimulus parameters (must mention for full marks):**
- Checkerboard pattern with **15' or 30' check size**
- Reversal rate **1 – 2 Hz** (1 reversal/second is standard)
- Monocular stimulation
- Patient fixes on central red dot at 1 m distance
- 100 – 200 sweeps averaged
- Sweep duration **300 ms**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b): 7 marks

**Given:** $\sigma_n = 40$ µV, $S = 4$ µV, $SNR_{out, \min} = 3$

**Step 1 — Input SNR (2 marks):**
$$SNR_{in} = \dfrac{S}{\sigma_n} = \dfrac{4}{40} = 0.1$$

**Step 2 — Number of sweeps (3 marks):**
Using $SNR_{out} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_{in}$:
$$\sqrt{N} = \dfrac{SNR_{out}}{SNR_{in}} = \dfrac{3}{0.1} = 30$$
$$N = 30^2 = 900 \text{ sweeps}$$

**Step 3 — Residual noise (2 marks):**
$$\sigma_{out} = \dfrac{\sigma_n}{\sqrt{N}} = \dfrac{40}{\sqrt{900}} = \dfrac{40}{30}$$
$$\boxed{\sigma_{out} = 1.333 \text{ µV}}$$

**Verification:** $SNR_{out} = 4 / 1.333 = 3$ ✓

---

### ⭐ Question B — AEP / BAEP FOCUS (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **principle of Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential (BAEP) recording**. List the five principal waves with their neural generators and typical latencies. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the equation showing how **signal-to-noise ratio improves with averaging** of N sweeps. A patient has a 60 Hz line interference of 25 µV picked up at the scalp. Calculate the **minimum number of 1-ms-square clicks** to be delivered at 10 Hz so that the AEP wave V (amplitude 0.5 µV) becomes clearly visible above the noise. Assume a peak-SNR of 4 is needed for confident identification. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a): 7 marks

**Principle (3 marks):**
BAEPs are the **far-field potentials** generated by the **auditory pathway** in the first 10 ms following an acoustic click stimulus. They are recorded from **vertex (Cz)** referenced to the **earlobe (A1/A2)** of the stimulated ear. The contralateral ear is masked with white noise. Because the potentials are extremely small (0.1 – 0.5 µV), 1000 – 2000 sweeps are averaged.

**Waves and generators (4 marks):**

| **Wave** | **Latency (ms)** | **Neural Generator** |
|---|---|---|
| **I** | 1.5 ± 0.1 | Distal auditory nerve (extracranial portion, just outside IAM) |
| **II** | 2.8 ± 0.2 | Proximal auditory nerve (intracranial, near brainstem) |
| **III** | 3.7 ± 0.2 | Pons (superior olivary complex, cochlear nucleus) |
| **IV** | 5.0 ± 0.2 | Pons / midbrain junction (lateral lemniscus) |
| **V** | 5.7 ± 0.2 | **Inferior colliculus** (midbrain) — most robust wave |

**Clinical use:** Diagnosis of acoustic neuroma (Wave V delayed or absent), multiple sclerosis (prolonged I–V inter-peak latency), and intra-operative monitoring of CN VIII.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b): 7 marks

**Derivation (3 marks):** Already shown in **Section 3.1** above, $SNR_{out} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_{in}$.

**Numerical problem (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Given:** $S = 0.5$ µV, $\sigma_n = 25$ µV, $SNR_{out, \min} = 4$, click rate = 10 Hz.

**Step 2 — Input SNR:**
$$SNR_{in} = \dfrac{0.5}{25} = 0.02$$

**Step 3 — Required N:**
$$\sqrt{N} = \dfrac{4}{0.02} = 200$$
$$\boxed{N = 40\,000 \text{ clicks}}$$

**Step 4 — Practical recording time:**
$$T = \dfrac{N}{\text{rate}} = \dfrac{40\,000}{10} = 4000 \text{ s} \approx 66.7 \text{ min}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is clinically impractical.** In practice, BAEP systems use **narrow-band filtering (100 – 3000 Hz)** to first reduce the effective $\sigma_n$ by a factor of 5–10, which then allows only **1000 – 2000** clicks to be sufficient. The student must always pair the SNR formula with the practical consideration of bandwidth filtering.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on EP questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "evoked" with "event-related" (ERP).** VEP and AEP are **exogenous** sensory responses, locked to physical stimulus. P300 / N400 are **endogenous ERPs**, locked to the **subject's decision**, not the stimulus. Examiner penalises if these are mixed up.
> 2. **Omitting the *√N* factor** in the SNR derivation and writing $SNR_{out} = N \cdot SNR_{in}$. The signal adds **linearly (N × S)** but the noise adds in **quadrature (√N × σ)** — the ratio is therefore **√N**, not N.
> 3. **Wrong electrode choice** — writing "Fz" for VEP or "Oz" for AEP. Remember: VEP → **Occipital** (Oz), AEP → **Vertex** (Cz).
> 4. **Forgetting the Nyquist criterion** when describing the ADC. Always state $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, with the relevant number for the test (e.g. 5 kS/s for BAEP whose highest useful component is ~ 2 kHz).
> 5. **Not mentioning the *Driven Right Leg* circuit** in the block diagram. It is the single most examiner-trap question. DRL reduces 50 Hz interference by 20–40 dB.
> 6. **Not stating stimulus parameters** (rate, intensity, sweep length, number of averages). A VEP answer missing "1 Hz reversal, 100 sweeps, 300 ms sweep" loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **EEG** records spontaneous brain activity, amplitude **10 – 100 µV**, bandwidth **0.5 – 30 Hz**.
- **EP** is a time-locked sensory response, amplitude **0.1 – 20 µV**, hidden in EEG noise.
- **Visual EP** is best recorded at **Oz – Fz**, with **P100** as the diagnostic peak at ~ **100 ms**.
- **Auditory EP (BAEP)** is best recorded at **Cz – A1/A2**, with **Wave V** at ~ **5.7 ms** as the most robust peak.
- **10-20 system** places electrodes at **10 % or 20 %** of the skull's nasion-to-inion and pre-auricular distances; **odd = left**, **even = right**, **z = midline**.
- **Signal-averaging equation:** $SNR_{out} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_{in}$ — signal adds **linearly**, noise adds as **√N**.
- To improve SNR by a factor of **10**, you need **N = 100** sweeps; for factor of **100**, you need **N = 10 000** sweeps — diminishing returns.
- **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; for BAEP, sample at **≥ 20 kHz**; for VEP, sample at **≥ 1 kHz**.
- **Driven Right Leg (DRL)** circuit actively drives the patient body to cancel common-mode 50/60 Hz mains interference — must appear in the block diagram.
- **CMRR of pre-amp** must be **> 100 dB**, input impedance **> 10¹² Ω**, noise **< 1 µV RMS**.
- **Filters:** High-pass 0.5 – 1 Hz (VEP), 100 – 150 Hz (BAEP); Low-pass 100 Hz (VEP), 3 kHz (BAEP); Notch 50 Hz always.
- **BAEP diagnostic indices:** Absolute latency of Wave V; I–V inter-peak latency (Central Conduction Time, normal ≤ 4.4 ms); V/I amplitude ratio.
- **VEP diagnostic indices:** P100 latency, P100 amplitude, inter-ocular difference (must be < 8 ms); delay suggests **optic neuritis / MS**.
- **Sweep parameters:** VEP → 300 ms, 100–200 sweeps, 1 Hz reversal. BAEP → 10–15 ms, 1000–2000 clicks, 10–30 Hz click rate.
- **Ground electrode** is always placed at **Fpz** for EP recordings.
- **Patient safety:** isolation amplifier (IEC 60601), leakage current **< 10 µA**, battery operation preferred.
- **Digitisation:** **16-bit ADC minimum**, with dynamic range ≥ ±5 V after PGA gain of × 1000.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acquisition of ECG and PPG Signals - Concurrent Analysis of Signals (e.g., Pulse Transit Time)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL607 - BIO INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Acquisition of ECG and PPG Signals - Concurrent Analysis of Signals (e.g., Pulse Transit Time)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electrocardiogram (ECG) — Formal Definition

The **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is a bioelectric signal that records the **summed electrical activity of cardiac myocytes** (heart muscle cells) from the body surface using surface electrodes. It is acquired as a differential voltage potential between carefully placed electrode pairs, amplified, band-pass filtered (typically **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz**), and digitized at a clinical sampling rate of **f_s ≥ 500 Hz** (research-grade up to 1000 Hz) to satisfy the **Nyquist criterion** for the highest-frequency QRS component.

The canonical waveform consists of the **P – Q – R – S – T – U** deflections, with the **R-peak** being the highest-amplitude, sharpest feature used as the fiducial marker for cardiac timing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEBL607):** The ECG is the *electrical* trigger event, while the PPG is the *mechanical/vascular* response. The TIME GAP between them is the diagnostic goldmine.

## 1.2 Photoplethysmogram (PPG) — Formal Definition

The **Photoplethysmogram (PPG)** is an optically obtained volumetric signal that measures **changes in blood volume within the microvascular bed of tissue** (typically the fingertip, earlobe, or toe). It works on the principle of **optical absorption / scattering** of light (usually **660 nm red** or **940 nm infrared**) emitted by an LED and detected by a photodiode after passing through (transmission mode) or reflecting off (reflectance mode) the tissue.

The PPG has two components:
- **DC component:** slowly varying baseline representing average blood volume, skin, bone, and venous/diastolic blood.
- **AC component:** pulsatile waveform synchronized with each cardiac cycle, representing arterial blood inflow during systole.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Acquisition Site:** Index finger (transmission mode) and forehead/wrist (reflectance mode) are most common in KTU lab setups.

## 1.3 Pulse Transit Time (PTT) — Formal Definition

**Pulse Transit Time (PTT)** is defined as the **time delay between the arrival of the pressure pulse at a proximal arterial site (the heart, indicated by the ECG R-peak) and the arrival of the same pulse wave at a distal peripheral site (indicated by a characteristic feature of the PPG)**.

$$\text{PTT} = t_{\text{PPG feature}} - t_{\text{ECG R-peak}}$$

PTT is most commonly expressed in **milliseconds (ms)** and typically falls in the range of **150 ms – 300 ms** in healthy adults at rest.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Heart as a Drum, The Arteries as Garden Hoses"

Imagine you strike a drum (**heart contracts → ECG R-peak**). The sound wave travels down a long garden hose (**aorta → peripheral arteries**) and makes the far end of the hose wobble (**arterial pulse at fingertip → PPG systolic peak**).

- The **ECG** is the moment your hand hits the drum.
- The **PPG peak** is the moment the far end of the hose wobbles.
- The **time gap** between them is the **Pulse Transit Time**.

> **Stiffer hose (arteriosclerosis / high BP) → faster wave → SHORTER PTT.**
> **Softer hose (elastic young arteries) → slower wave → LONGER PTT.**

This is exactly why PTT is clinically valuable: it is an **indirect, cuffless surrogate for arterial stiffness and blood pressure** — the holy grail of wearable health monitoring.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence of the Experiment

The KTU lab experiment for **concurrent ECG + PPG acquisition and PTT analysis** follows a strict sequence:

### Step 1 — Hardware Setup & Electrode Placement (ECG Channel)
- Clean the skin with **isopropyl alcohol** to reduce electrode-skin impedance below **5 kΩ**.
- Place **three Ag/AgCl disposable electrodes** in **Lead II configuration**:
  - **RA (Right Arm):** right infraclavicular fossa.
  - **LA (Left Arm):** left infraclavicular fossa.
  - **RL (Right Leg / DRIVE):** right lower rib cage — this is the driven right leg (DRL) ground reference used to improve **CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio)** to > **100 dB**.
- Connect leads to the **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., **AD620, INA128**) with gain set between **100 – 1000**.

### Step 2 — Optical Probe Placement (PPG Channel)
- Clip the **transmission-mode PPG probe** on the **distal phalanx of the index finger** of the LEFT hand (opposite the ECG limb electrodes to avoid motion cross-talk).
- Ensure the LED is aligned with the photodetector; the optical path passes through the **nail bed and pulp tissue**.

### Step 3 — Synchronized Analog Front-End (AFE)
- ECG signal: pre-amplified → **0.5 Hz HPF** (to remove DC electrode offset) → **40 Hz LPF (or 35 Hz)** → **50 Hz notch filter** (to suppress Indian mains power-line interference) → ADC.
- PPG signal: photodiode current-to-voltage conversion (TIA, e.g., **LM358 / OPA380**) → **0.5 – 10 Hz BPF** (the PPG AC component resides in this band) → ADC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 0.5 – 10 Hz for PPG?** The cardiac pulsation frequency at resting heart rate (60 – 100 bpm) = **1 – 1.67 Hz**; the filter preserves the fundamental and up to ~6th harmonic.

### Step 4 — Concurrent Digitization (Shared Time Base)
- **CRITICAL REQUIREMENT:** Both ECG and PPG signals MUST be sampled by a **single ADC or a multi-channel ADC with a shared clock**.
- Sampling rate: **f_s = 500 Hz** is the KTU-standard choice.
- Use the **same trigger/start signal** to ensure sample #1 of the ECG and sample #1 of the PPG correspond to the SAME physical instant.
- A common platform is **NI ELVIS / DAQ card / Arduino + MATLAB** where the two channels are read in a single software loop.

### Step 5 — Digital Signal Processing (MATLAB / Python equivalent)
1. **Baseline correction:** subtract the moving median or polynomial fit to remove DC drift.
2. **Bandpass filtering:** apply a **4th-order Butterworth filter** (0.5 – 40 Hz for ECG, 0.5 – 10 Hz for PPG).
3. **R-peak detection (ECG):** **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** (bandpass → derivative → squaring → moving window integration → adaptive thresholding).
4. **PPG systolic peak / foot detection:** find the maximum within a 1-second window after the R-peak, OR use the **second-derivative "b-point"** for foot detection.
5. **PTT computation:** for each cardiac cycle *n*:

$$t_{\text{R-peak}}[n] = \text{time index of R-peak}$$
$$t_{\text{PPG-peak}}[n] = \text{time index of next PPG systolic peak}$$
$$\text{PTT}[n] = \frac{t_{\text{PPG-peak}}[n] - t_{\text{R-peak}}[n]}{f_s} \quad [\text{seconds}]$$

### Step 6 — Statistical Reporting
- Compute the **mean (μ) and standard deviation (σ) of PTT** over 30 – 60 seconds of recording.
- Display the **histogram** and **beat-to-beat time series** of PTT.
- Optionally, estimate **Pulse Wave Velocity (PWV)** and **systolic blood pressure** using empirical regression.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PTT definition | $\text{PTT} = t_{\text{PPG}} - t_{\text{R-peak}}$ | seconds (s) or ms | Computed per cardiac cycle |
| 2 | PTT in samples | $\text{PTT}_\text{samples} = n_{\text{PPG}} - n_{\text{R}}$ | samples | Convert using $n / f_s$ |
| 3 | Pulse Wave Velocity (PWV) | $\text{PWV} = \dfrac{L}{\text{PTT}}$ | m/s | $L$ = arterial path length (heart to fingertip) ≈ **0.75 – 1.0 m** |
| 4 | Moens–Korteweg equation | $\text{PWV} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot h}{2 \cdot r \cdot \rho}}$ | m/s | $E$ = Young's modulus, $h$ = wall thickness, $r$ = radius, $\rho$ = blood density (**1060 kg/m³**) |
| 5 | BP estimation (linear) | $\text{SBP} = a - b \cdot \text{PTT}$ | mmHg | $a, b$ = subject-specific calibration constants |
| 6 | Sample index → time | $t[n] = \dfrac{n}{f_s}$ | seconds | $f_s$ = 500 Hz for KTU labs |
| 7 | Heart rate from R-R | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_s}{N_{\text{RR}}}$ | bpm | $N_{\text{RR}}$ = samples between consecutive R-peaks |
| 8 | Beat-to-beat PTT | $\text{PTT}_n = \dfrac{n_{\text{PPG},n} - n_{\text{R},n}}{f_s}$ | seconds | $n$ indexes cardiac cycle |
| 9 | Perfusion index | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{\text{AC}_{\text{PPG}}}{\text{DC}_{\text{PPG}}} \times 100$ | % | Indicator of peripheral perfusion |
| 10 | Optical absorption (Beer–Lambert) | $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon \cdot c \cdot d}$ | W/m² | $\varepsilon$ = absorption coefficient, $c$ = concentration, $d$ = path length |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** PTT is the cornerstone of every modern **cuffless blood pressure wearable** (smartwatches, fitness bands). It is also used in **sleep apnea detection**, **arterial stiffness screening**, and **autonomic nervous system assessment**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Samples to Physical PTT (in milliseconds)

We are given a synchronized digitized recording where the **R-peak of the QRS complex in the ECG** occurs at sample index $n_R = 1247$ and the **systolic peak of the PPG** from the same heartbeat occurs at sample index $n_{\text{PPG}} = 1371$. The shared sampling rate is $f_s = 500$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Identify the lag in samples.**

$$\Delta n = n_{\text{PPG}} - n_R = 1371 - 1247 = 124 \quad [\text{samples}]$$

**Step 2 — Convert sample lag to time lag using the time-stamp formula.**

Since $t[n] = \dfrac{n}{f_s}$, the time difference is:

$$\text{PTT} = \frac{n_{\text{PPG}}}{f_s} - \frac{n_R}{f_s} = \frac{n_{\text{PPG}} - n_R}{f_s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\text{PTT} = \frac{124}{500} = 0.248 \quad [\text{seconds}]$$

**Step 4 — Express in milliseconds (the clinical convention).**

$$\text{PTT} = 0.248 \times 1000 = 248 \quad [\text{ms}]$$

**Step 5 — Validation against physiological range.** A value of **248 ms** lies within the standard healthy resting range (**150 – 300 ms**), so the measurement is physiologically valid.

> **Logical conclusion:** A PTT of 248 ms means the pressure wave took 248 ms to travel from the aortic valve to the fingertip after the heart's electrical depolarization.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pulse Wave Velocity (PWV) from PTT

**Given:** Arterial path length from heart to fingertip $L = 0.85$ m, and the PTT computed above $= 0.248$ s.

**Step 1 — State the PWV definition.**

$$\text{PWV} = \frac{L}{\text{PTT}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values with unit consistency (m and s).**

$$\text{PWV} = \frac{0.85 \text{ m}}{0.248 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerically.**

$$\text{PWV} \approx 3.43 \quad [\text{m/s}]$$

**Step 4 — Compare with literature.** Aortic PWV in healthy young adults ranges **4 – 8 m/s**; peripheral (arm-to-finger) PWV is **2 – 5 m/s**. Our value of 3.43 m/s is consistent with a **healthy peripheral arterial system**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverse Relationship Between PTT and Blood Pressure

The **Moens–Korteweg equation** (simplified, for a thin-walled elastic tube) states:

$$\text{PWV} = \sqrt{\frac{E \cdot h}{2 \cdot r \cdot \rho}}$$

where the **incremental Young's modulus** $E$ is related to the **transmural pressure** $P$ by the empirical **Hughes equation**:

$$E = E_0 \cdot e^{\gamma P}$$

with $E_0$ being the zero-pressure modulus and $\gamma$ a vessel-specific constant.

**Step 1 — Combine PWV and PTT.**

$$\text{PTT} = \frac{L}{\text{PWV}} = L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot r \cdot \rho}{E \cdot h}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the pressure-dependent modulus.**

$$\text{PTT} = L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot r \cdot \rho}{E_0 \cdot e^{\gamma P} \cdot h}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$\text{PTT} = L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot r \cdot \rho}{E_0 \cdot h}} \cdot e^{-\gamma P / 2}$$

**Step 4 — Observe the monotonic relationship.**

$$\text{PTT} \propto e^{-\gamma P / 2}$$

This mathematically proves the well-known **inverse relationship**:
- **↑ Blood Pressure** $\Rightarrow$ arterial wall stiffness ↑ $\Rightarrow$ PWV ↑ $\Rightarrow$ **PTT ↓**.
- **↓ Blood Pressure** $\Rightarrow$ arterial wall compliance ↑ $\Rightarrow$ PWV ↓ $\Rightarrow$ **PTT ↑**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Linearized Cuffless BP Estimation

Because the exponential relationship is highly non-linear, KTU labs typically use a **linearized regression model** after a one-time cuff calibration:

$$\text{SBP}_{\text{predicted}} = a - b \cdot \text{PTT}$$

For a healthy 25-year-old male subject, after calibration with a standard sphygmomanometer, we found:

- $a = 178$ mmHg
- $b = 0.34$ mmHg/ms

**Step 1 — Substitute the measured PTT = 248 ms.**

$$\text{SBP} = 178 - 0.34 \times 248$$

**Step 2 — Multiply.**

$$0.34 \times 248 = 84.32 \quad [\text{mmHg}]$$

**Step 3 — Subtract.**

$$\text{SBP} = 178 - 84.32 = 93.68 \quad [\text{mmHg}]$$

**Step 4 — Clinical interpretation.** Predicted SBP of ~94 mmHg is within the **normal range (90 – 120 mmHg)**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Fiducial Markers

| Fiducial Pair | ECG Marker | PPG Marker | Typical PTT Range (ms) | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R-peak → Systolic peak** | R | Systolic maximum | 200 – 350 | Easiest to detect | Affected by wave reflection |
| **R-peak → Foot point** | R | Foot (onset) of PPG | 100 – 250 | Most physically meaningful (true transit) | Harder to detect (low slope) |
| **R-peak → dicrotic notch** | R | Dicrotic notch | 400 – 600 | Captures full pulse cycle | Slow, not "transit" |
| **QRS onset → Foot** | Q | Foot | 150 – 280 | Standard in cuffless BP literature | Q-onset detection is harder than R-peak |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Concurrent ECG + PPG Acquisition & PTT Analysis System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Human Subject] --> B1[ECG Electrodes - Lead II]
    A1 --> B2[PPG Probe - Finger]
    B1 --> C1[Instrumentation Amplifier - Gain 1000]
    C1 --> C2[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    C2 --> C3[50 Hz Notch Filter]
    B2 --> D1[LED Driver 660 nm]
    D1 --> A1
    A1 --> D2[Photodiode + Transimpedance Amplifier]
    D2 --> D3[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 10 Hz]
    C3 --> E1[Multichannel ADC - 500 Hz]
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Shared Time Base - Shared Clock]
    F1 --> G1[Digital Signal Processing - MATLAB]
    G1 --> G2[R-peak Detection - Pan Tompkins]
    G1 --> G3[PPG Peak Detection]
    G2 --> H1[PTT Computation Engine]
    G3 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[PTT Time Series Display]
    H1 --> I2[PWV and BP Estimation]
    H1 --> I3[Statistical Report - mean and std]
```

## 4.2 Signal Flow / Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J1[Raw ECG and Raw PPG - Synchronized] --> K1[Baseline Wander Removal]
    K1 --> K2[4th Order Butterworth Bandpass]
    K2 --> L1[ECG R-peak Detection]
    K2 --> L2[PPG Systolic Peak Detection]
    L1 --> M1[Temporal Alignment Check]
    L2 --> M1
    M1 --> N1[Per-beat PTT Calculation]
    N1 --> O1[Median Filter on PTT - Remove Outliers]
    O1 --> P1[Mean and Standard Deviation]
    P1 --> Q1[Final Result Display]
```

## 4.3 Data Acquisition Architecture (Hardware Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[ECG Front End - AD620] --> S1[Analog MUX]
    R2[PPG Front End - TIA LM358] --> S1
    S1 --> T1[16 bit ADC - ADS1115]
    T1 --> U1[Microcontroller - Arduino or STM32]
    T1 --> V1[Shared Crystal Oscillator - 8 MHz]
    U1 --> W1[Serial UART to PC]
    W1 --> X1[Host PC - MATLAB LabVIEW Processing]
    X1 --> Y1[Display - Oscilloscope and Plots]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Shared Crystal Oscillator** is the engineering safeguard that guarantees both channels are sampled with the SAME time reference, which is the **fundamental requirement** for valid PTT measurement.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Pulse Transit Time (PTT). How is it obtained from concurrent ECG and PPG recordings?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Pulse Transit Time (PTT) is the time interval between the **electrical depolarization of the ventricles** (indicated by the **R-peak of the ECG**) and the **arrival of the resulting pressure pulse at a peripheral arterial site** (indicated by a characteristic feature of the **PPG signal**, usually the **systolic peak** or the **foot**).

**Method of obtaining (2 marks):** PTT is obtained by **simultaneously acquiring** the ECG and PPG signals using a **common time base (shared clock)** at a sampling rate of typically **500 Hz**. The R-peak in the ECG and the subsequent PPG systolic peak in the same cardiac cycle are identified. The **time difference** between the two fiducial points, divided by the sampling interval, gives PTT:

$$\text{PTT} = \frac{n_{\text{PPG-peak}} - n_{\text{R-peak}}}{f_s}$$

For each cardiac cycle, one PTT value is computed, and the **mean and standard deviation** over 30–60 seconds of recording are reported.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Briefly explain the working principle of a PPG sensor. Why is the AC component the clinically useful part?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Working principle (2 marks):** A PPG sensor consists of an **LED (light source)** and a **photodetector (photodiode)** placed on either side of a tissue (e.g., fingertip) in **transmission mode**, or on the same side in **reflectance mode**. The LED emits light (typically **660 nm red** or **940 nm infrared**) which is absorbed, scattered, and transmitted through the tissue. The amount of light reaching the photodetector depends on the **blood volume** in the illuminated microvascular bed. With each heartbeat, the arterial blood volume increases during systole and decreases during diastole, modulating the received light intensity and producing the **pulsatile PPG waveform**.

**Why the AC component matters (1 mark):** The DC component represents the **static absorption** by skin, bone, venous blood, and other non-pulsatile tissues — it carries no cardiac timing information. The **AC component** corresponds to the **pulsatile arterial blood volume change** with each heartbeat and therefore contains the **systolic peak and dicrotic notch** that are required for PTT computation and pulse-rate measurement.

---

## **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the simultaneous acquisition of **ECG and PPG signals** from a human subject. Explain the role of the **instrumentation amplifier**, the **50 Hz notch filter**, and the **shared time base** in ensuring a valid PTT measurement.

**(b) [7 marks]** From a synchronized recording sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz, the R-peak of the ECG occurred at sample index $n_R = 2103$ and the corresponding PPG systolic peak occurred at sample index $n_{\text{PPG}} = 2242$. Compute the **PTT in milliseconds** and the **Pulse Wave Velocity (PWV)** assuming the arterial path length is $L = 0.90$ m. State whether the PWV is within the normal peripheral range.

---

### **Model Answer to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
ECG Electrodes (Lead II) → Instrumentation Amplifier (AD620, gain = 1000)
                           → 0.5 Hz HPF → 40 Hz LPF → 50 Hz Notch → ADC CH1 ─┐
                                                                                │
PPG Probe (Finger) → LED Driver → Photodiode → TIA → 0.5–10 Hz BPF → ADC CH2 ─┤
                                                                                │
                                                          SHARED CLOCK (500 Hz) ┘
                                                                                │
                                                                       Host PC (MATLAB)
                                                                                │
                                              R-peak detection ──→ PTT computation
```

**Instrumentation Amplifier (2 marks):**
The ECG signal is in the range of **0.5 mV – 5 mV** and is buried in **large common-mode noise** (50 Hz mains interference, motion artefact, electrode half-cell potentials). The **instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620)** provides:
- **High differential gain** (typically 100 – 1000) to amplify the small ECG.
- **High CMRR (> 100 dB)** to reject the common-mode 50 Hz interference.
- **High input impedance (> 1 GΩ)** so the amplifier does not load the high-impedance electrode-tissue interface.

**50 Hz Notch Filter (1 mark):**
The Indian mains power line operates at **50 Hz** and couples capacitively into the body and electrode cables. A **50 Hz notch (band-reject) filter** with a narrow bandwidth of ±2 Hz is inserted after the bandpass filter to suppress this interference without distorting the QRS complex.

**Shared Time Base (2 marks):**
For PTT computation, both ECG and PPG samples must be **time-correlated**. If the two ADCs use independent clocks, even a 1 ms drift between them will corrupt the PTT measurement. A **shared crystal oscillator** driving both ADCs (or a **multi-channel simultaneous-sampling ADC** like the ADS131M04) ensures that **sample #1 of the ECG and sample #1 of the PPG correspond to the exact same physical instant**. This makes the PTT computation mathematically and physically valid.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute the sample lag (1 mark).**

$$\Delta n = n_{\text{PPG}} - n_R = 2242 - 2103 = 139 \quad [\text{samples}]$$

**Step 2 — Convert to PTT in seconds (2 marks).**

$$\text{PTT} = \frac{\Delta n}{f_s} = \frac{139}{500} = 0.278 \quad [\text{s}]$$

**Step 3 — Convert to milliseconds (1 mark).**

$$\text{PTT} = 0.278 \times 1000 = 278 \quad [\text{ms}]$$

**Step 4 — Compute PWV (2 marks).**

$$\text{PWV} = \frac{L}{\text{PTT}} = \frac{0.90 \text{ m}}{0.278 \text{ s}} \approx 3.24 \quad [\text{m/s}]$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark).**
The computed PWV of **3.24 m/s** lies within the **normal peripheral (arm-to-finger) range of 2 – 5 m/s** for a healthy adult, indicating normal arterial elasticity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** for real-time QRS (R-peak) detection. List all the major processing stages and state the role of the **adaptive thresholding** stage.

**(b) [7 marks]** A subject's PTT is measured as **220 ms** at rest. The same subject's PTT rises to **265 ms** after a 10-minute session of slow yogic breathing. Using the **Moens–Korteweg framework**, qualitatively explain the change in PTT and what it implies about **arterial stiffness and blood pressure**.

---

### **Model Answer to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** (1985) is the gold-standard real-time QRS detector. Its stages are:

1. **Bandpass Filter (1–15 Hz) (1 mark):** Removes baseline wander, 50 Hz mains noise, and high-frequency muscle artefact. Isolates the QRS energy band.
2. **Derivative Operator (1 mark):** Five-point derivative $[-1, -2, 0, 2, 1]$ emphasizes the **steep slopes** of the R-wave, suppressing P and T waves (which are gentler).
3. **Squaring (1 mark):** Makes all values positive and **non-linearly amplifies** the large-amplitude QRS relative to smaller features.
4. **Moving-Window Integration (1 mark):** A 150 ms moving average produces a **single smooth pulse** for each QRS, capturing its energy envelope.
5. **Adaptive Thresholding (2 marks):** Two thresholds — a high threshold that triggers an R-peak detection and a lower threshold that searches for missed beats. The thresholds are **continuously updated** based on the running estimates of the **signal level** and **noise level** of the integrated waveform. This makes the algorithm **robust to changing ECG amplitudes** caused by respiration, motion, or electrode drift.
6. **Searchback and Refractory Period Logic (1 mark):** A 200 ms **refractory period** prevents double-counting a single QRS; a searchback (360 ms) re-scans for missed beats.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Observed change (1 mark):** PTT INCREASED from **220 ms → 265 ms** (Δ = +45 ms, a ~20% increase).

**Qualitative analysis (4 marks):**
Using the **Moens–Korteweg** relation:

$$\text{PTT} \propto e^{-\gamma P / 2}$$

a **longer PTT** corresponds to a **lower effective arterial stiffness** ($E$) and/or a **lower blood pressure** ($P$). During slow yogic breathing:
- **Parasympathetic (vagal) tone increases** → **heart rate drops** and **peripheral resistance drops**.
- **Arterial smooth muscle relaxes** → effective Young's modulus $E$ **decreases** → wave travels more slowly.
- **Systolic and diastolic blood pressure both fall** slightly.

**Clinical implication (2 marks):**
The increase in PTT from 220 → 265 ms reflects a **reduction in arterial stiffness and a fall in blood pressure** induced by the breathing exercise. This is the **physiological evidence** that slow yogic breathing has a **vasorelaxant and antihypertensive effect**, validating PTT as a sensitive non-invasive biomarker of cardiovascular state.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes that COST MARKS in the KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** — writing PTT in samples or in seconds when the question asks for **milliseconds**. Always check the requested unit. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Using the wrong fiducial** — confusing **R-peak → PPG peak** with **R-peak → PPG foot**. The examiner's key is strict. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Ignoring the shared time base requirement** — describing the two channels as independent ADCs. The examiner EXPECTS the student to explicitly mention the **shared clock / simultaneous sampling ADC**. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Sign error in BP linear regression** — writing SBP = a + b·PTT (positive slope) instead of a − b·PTT. PTT is INVERSELY related to BP. **(−2 marks)**
> 5. **No mention of the DC vs AC components** in PPG — at least one line on this is mandatory to score full marks on PPG theory. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Omitting the 50 Hz notch filter** in the signal conditioning chain — Indian mains frequency, MUST appear. **(−1 mark)**

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **ECG** = bioelectric signal; fiducial for PTT is the **R-peak**; bandwidth **0.05 – 150 Hz**; sampling rate **≥ 500 Hz**.
- ✅ **PPG** = optical volumetric signal; fiducial for PTT is the **systolic peak OR foot**; LED wavelengths **660 nm / 940 nm**.
- ✅ **PPG has a DC component (static tissue) and an AC component (pulsatile arterial blood)** — the AC component is the clinically useful one.
- ✅ **PTT = t_PPG − t_R-peak**; clinically expressed in **milliseconds**; healthy resting range **150 – 300 ms**.
- ✅ **PTT is INVERSELY related to BP** and **INVERSELY related to arterial stiffness**; **PTT is DIRECTLY related to arterial compliance**.
- ✅ **PWV = L / PTT**; peripheral PWV ≈ **2 – 5 m/s**; aortic PWV ≈ **4 – 8 m/s**.
- ✅ **Moens–Korteweg** gives the theoretical foundation: $\text{PWV} = \sqrt{E h / (2 r \rho)}$.
- ✅ **Hughes equation:** $E = E_0 e^{\gamma P}$ — the **exponential link** between wall stiffness and blood pressure.
- ✅ **Concurrent acquisition** REQUIRES a **shared clock / simultaneous-sampling ADC** — independent clocks corrupt PTT.
- ✅ **Signal chain (ECG):** electrodes → INA → 0.5 Hz HPF → 40 Hz LPF → 50 Hz notch → ADC.
- ✅ **Signal chain (PPG):** LED + photodiode → TIA → 0.5–10 Hz BPF → ADC.
- ✅ **Pan-Tompkins** = standard QRS detector: bandpass → derivative → square → integrate → adaptive threshold.
- ✅ **50 Hz notch filter** is essential in India for **mains-line interference rejection**.
- ✅ **PPG is in transmission mode** at the fingertip and **reflectance mode** at the wrist/forehead.
- ✅ **Perfusion Index (PI) = (AC/DC) × 100%** — quick vitality indicator of the PPG signal.
- ✅ **Beat-to-beat PTT variability** is a marker of **autonomic nervous system function** — a hot research topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Level Analysis (Colorimeter, Defibrillator, Ultra Sound Scanner, Power Supply Unit, X-Ray Machine)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL607 - BIO INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** System Level Analysis (Colorimeter, Defibrillator, Ultra Sound Scanner, Power Supply Unit, X-Ray Machine)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "System Level Analysis" in Bio-Instrumentation?

**System Level Analysis** is the structured study of a medical/biomedical instrument by breaking it down into its functional building blocks (sub-systems), understanding the **signal flow** between these blocks, and evaluating how each block contributes to the final diagnostic or therapeutic output. In the KTU 2024 Bio-Instrumentation Lab context, system-level analysis means drawing the **functional block diagram**, identifying the **input/output signal** at every stage, and explaining the **interconnection** between sensing, signal-conditioning, processing, and display stages.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exam-Worthy):** "System level analysis of a bio-medical instrument is the process of representing the instrument as a series of interconnected functional blocks, where each block performs a specific operation (sensing, amplification, filtering, processing, display) on a well-defined input signal to produce the desired physiological, diagnostic, or therapeutic output."

## 1.2 The Five Biomedical Systems Under Study

This experiment covers the system-level analysis of **five** representative biomedical instruments, each chosen to illustrate a different category of medical electronics:

| # | Instrument | Category | Primary Function | Physiological Variable Measured/Affected |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Colorimeter** | *Diagnostic – In-vitro* | Quantifies concentration of a chemical species in a fluid sample by measuring light absorption. | **Absorbance / Concentration** of biochemicals in blood/urine. |
| 2 | **Defibrillator** | *Therapeutic – Emergency* | Delivers a controlled, high-energy DC shock to the heart to re-establish normal sinus rhythm. | **Cardiac ECG waveform** (input) → **Truncated Exponential / Monophasic/Biphasic pulse** (output). |
| 3 | **Ultrasound Scanner** | *Diagnostic – In-vivo Imaging* | Uses high-frequency sound waves and their echoes to construct real-time 2D images of internal body structures. | **Acoustic Impedance** at tissue interfaces → **B-mode brightness image.** |
| 4 | **Power Supply Unit (PSU)** | *Support Subsystem* | Converts and regulates mains AC into the multiple stable DC rails required by every electronic medical instrument. | **Mains AC (230 V, 50 Hz)** → **±5 V, ±12 V, ±15 V, HV rails** (regulated DC). |
| 5 | **X-Ray Machine** | *Diagnostic – Radiographic Imaging* | Produces controlled X-ray photons that pass through the body to form a projection image on a detector. | **Tissue Radiodensity** → **Grayscale radiographic image.** |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies (Plain English Explanation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Instrument is like a Restaurant Kitchen"**
>
> Think of any biomedical instrument as a **professional kitchen preparing a single dish**:
> - The **Input Block** (sensor/transducer) is the **raw ingredient** arriving at the kitchen door.
> - The **Signal Conditioning Block** is the **prep station** (wash, chop, measure).
> - The **Processing Block** is the **chef** (the actual cooking logic).
> - The **Output/Display Block** is the **plated dish** served to the customer (clinician).
> - The **Power Supply Unit (PSU)** is the **electricity + gas pipeline** that powers every appliance in the kitchen.
>
> System-level analysis is the act of writing down the **recipe flowchart** of that kitchen — which station hands what to whom, in what order, with what timing.

**Per-Instrument Quick-Intuition:**

- **Colorimeter** = A "smart window" that looks at a colored solution and tells you *how concentrated* the dye is, by measuring *how much light got through*.
- **Defibrillator** = A "medical jump-starter for the heart" — like jumper cables for a car battery, but synchronized to the exact moment the heart's natural pacemaker fires.
- **Ultrasound Scanner** = A "submarine sonar on a stick" that shouts inaudible sound pulses into the body and listens for the echoes to map internal organs.
- **Power Supply Unit** = A "voltage translator" — the body of a car (mains AC) cannot directly power a smartphone (DC microelectronics); the PSU is the adapter in between.
- **X-Ray Machine** = A "shadow projector" — because bones absorb X-rays more than flesh, the body's silhouette appears on a film, just like a hand blocks light to cast a shadow on a wall.

## 1.4 Common Vocabulary of System-Level Blocks (KTU Standard Terms)

Every bio-instrument can be abstracted into a chain of these canonical blocks. Mastering these terms is **mandatory for KTU viva voce**.

- **Transducer / Sensor** – Converts a physiological variable into an electrical signal.
- **Signal Conditioner** – Amplifies, filters, and linearizes the raw transducer signal.
- **Signal Processor** – Applies mathematical operations (A/D conversion, FFT, demodulation).
- **Display / Recorder** – Presents the result (LCD, CRO, printer, speaker).
- **Power Supply** – Energizes all active stages.
- **Controller / Microprocessor** – Supervises timing, gain, safety interlocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (used across all five systems):**
> - **Frequency range** of bioelectric signals: **0.05 Hz to 10 kHz** (ECG/EEG/EMG).
> - **Mains supply in India:** **230 V AC, 50 Hz** (per IS 1293).
> - **Ultrasound diagnostic frequency:** **1 MHz to 20 MHz**.
> - **Defibrillator energy range:** **2 J to 360 J** (adult).
> - **X-ray tube voltage:** **30 kVp to 150 kVp** (diagnostic).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Colorimeter — System Level Analysis

### 2.1.1 Purpose
A colorimeter is an **optical analytical instrument** that measures the **absorbance** (or transmittance) of a colored solution to determine the **concentration of an analyte** in a biological fluid (e.g., haemoglobin, glucose, cholesterol, bilirubin in blood).

### 2.1.2 Block Diagram (Conceptual)

```
Light Source → Monochromator/Filter → Cuvette (Sample) → Photodetector → Amplifier → ADC → Microprocessor → Display
```

### 2.1.3 Block-by-Block Operation

1. **Light Source:** Tungsten-Halogen lamp (visible range 340–700 nm) or Deuterium lamp (UV range 190–400 nm). Provides a stable, broad-spectrum beam.
2. **Monochromator / Optical Filter:** Isolates a single wavelength (bandwidth typically 5–10 nm) corresponding to the absorption peak of the analyte of interest. Implemented as an **interference filter**, **prism**, or **diffraction grating**.
3. **Cuvette Holder:** Holds the **sample cuvette** (standard path length **l = 1 cm**, made of optical glass or quartz). A matched cuvette containing the **blank (reference)** is used for zeroing.
4. **Sample Compartment:** The reagent-analyte chemical reaction produces a **chromogen** whose color intensity is proportional to analyte concentration.
5. **Photodetector:** Converts the transmitted light intensity into an electrical signal. Common types: **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)** for high sensitivity, or **Silicon Photodiode** for routine use.
6. **Preamplifier + Log Amplifier:** Amplifies the photocurrent. A **logarithmic amplifier** is critical because the Beer–Lambert law is logarithmic; the log amp linearizes the relationship to concentration.
7. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Digitizes the absorbance value.
8. **Microprocessor / Digital Signal Processor:** Performs calibration, applies the calibration curve, and computes concentration in **mg/dL** or **mmol/L**.
9. **Display:** LCD showing **Absorbance (A)** and **Concentration (C)**.

### 2.1.4 Governing Law — Beer–Lambert Law

$$ A = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l $$

where:
- $A$ = **Absorbance** (dimensionless, sometimes in AU)
- $I_0$ = Intensity of incident light
- $I$ = Intensity of transmitted light
- $\varepsilon$ = **Molar absorptivity** (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹) — wavelength-dependent
- $c$ = **Concentration** of analyte (mol/L)
- $l$ = **Optical path length** through the cuvette (cm)

**Transmittance** is defined as:

$$ T = \frac{I}{I_0} $$

and **Percentage Transmittance** is:

$$ \%T = 100 \cdot T = 100 \cdot \frac{I}{I_0} $$

The relationship between Absorbance and Transmittance:

$$ A = -\log_{10}(T) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) $$

## 2.2 Defibrillator — System Level Analysis

### 2.2.1 Purpose
A defibrillator is a **therapeutic device** that delivers a brief, high-energy **DC electrical shock** across the chest (or directly to the heart in open-chest surgery) to depolarize a critical mass of myocardial cells, thereby terminating **ventricular fibrillation (VF)** or **pulseless ventricular tachycardia (VT)** and allowing the heart's natural pacemaker (SA node) to resume a normal sinus rhythm.

### 2.2.2 Two Operating Modes

- **Synchronized Cardioversion (Elective):** Shock is delivered on the **R-wave** of the patient's ECG (timed to avoid the vulnerable T-wave period, which could induce VF).
- **Defibrillation (Emergency):** Shock is delivered **asynchronously**, as soon as the operator presses the button, because the patient has no organized rhythm.

### 2.2.3 Block Diagram

```
Mains → Step-up Transformer → HV Rectifier → Charging Capacitor (C) → Discharge Switch (SCR/IGBT) → Patient (via Paddles/Electrodes)
                                                                                            ↘
                                                                                             → ECG Monitor + R-wave Detector (Sync)
```

### 2.2.4 Block-by-Block Operation

1. **Mains Step-up Transformer + HV Rectifier:** Converts 230 V AC into a high DC voltage (**typically 2000 V to 7000 V DC**) required to charge the energy storage capacitor.
2. **Energy Storage Capacitor (C):** Large-value capacitor (**C ≈ 16 µF to 200 µF**, typically 32 µF) stores the defibrillation energy. The energy stored is given by $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$.
3. **Charging Circuit (DC-DC Converter / Cockcroft-Walton Multiplier):** Charges the capacitor to a precisely controlled voltage corresponding to the selected energy level.
4. **Discharge Switch:** A high-power **SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier)** or **IGBT (Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistor)** that closes the circuit at the commanded moment.
5. **Pulse Shaping Inductor:** In series with the patient to shape the discharge waveform (limits peak current, controls waveform tilt). Produces **Edmark, Gurvich, or Trapezoidal** waveforms.
6. **Paddles / Pads:** Apply the shock through the chest. Two standard positions: **anterior-apex (sternum-apex)** and **anterior-posterior**.
7. **ECG Monitor + Synchronizer:** Continuously displays the patient's ECG. The **R-wave detector** triggers the discharge only on detection of an R-wave (sync mode).
8. **Microcontroller + Safety Interlocks:** Enforces charging time, energy selection, patient impedance compensation, and discharge synchronization.

### 2.2.5 Key Formulas

**Energy stored in capacitor:**

$$ E = \frac{1}{2}\,C\,V^2 $$

**Energy actually delivered to patient** (accounting for patient thoracic impedance, typically $Z_p = 25$ to $150\,\Omega$):

$$ E_{\text{delivered}} = E_{\text{stored}} \cdot \frac{Z_p}{Z_p + Z_{\text{internal}}} $$

For an ideal pulse forming network matched to a 50 Ω patient:

$$ E_{\text{delivered}} = \frac{V^2}{2 R} \cdot \tau \quad \text{(for a square pulse of duration }\tau\text{)} $$

**Peak discharge current:**

$$ I_{\text{peak}} = \frac{V_{\text{capacitor}}}{R_{\text{total}}} $$

## 2.3 Ultrasound Scanner — System Level Analysis

### 2.3.1 Purpose
An ultrasound scanner (also called a **sonograph** or **echograph**) is a **non-invasive, non-ionizing real-time imaging modality** that uses high-frequency mechanical sound waves (typically 2–15 MHz clinically) to visualize soft tissues, blood flow, and fetal development.

### 2.3.2 Block Diagram

```
Pulser/Transmitter → T/R Switch → Transducer (Probe) ⇄ Patient
       ↑                                              ↓ Echo
Microcontroller/Beamformer ← Preamplifier ← T/R Switch
       ↓
Signal Processor (Envelope Detection, Compression) → Scan Converter → Display
```

### 2.3.3 Block-by-Block Operation

1. **Pulser / Transmitter:** Generates a short, high-voltage **excitation pulse** (typically ±100 V, 0.1–1 µs wide) at a precise PRF (Pulse Repetition Frequency) of **1 kHz to 10 kHz**. Each pulse contains 2–5 cycles of the chosen frequency.
2. **T/R Switch (Transmit/Receive Switch):** A high-isolation electronic switch that **protects the sensitive receive amplifier** from the high-voltage transmit pulse (up to 100+ dB isolation required).
3. **Transducer Probe:** The heart of the system. Made of a **piezoelectric crystal** (lead zirconate titanate — PZT, or newer PVDF polymer). Converts electrical pulses to acoustic pulses and vice versa. The probe may be:
   - **Linear array** (rectangular image, high-frequency, shallow)
   - **Curvilinear / Convex array** (sector, abdominal)
   - **Phased array** (small sector, cardiac)
   - **Endocavitary** (transvaginal, transrectal)
4. **Beamformer:** Electronic steering and focusing of the ultrasound beam. Applies precise **time delays** to each element of the array to steer the beam and dynamically focus on a chosen depth.
5. **Preamplifier + Time-Gain Compensation (TGC):** Amplifies the weak returning echo. TGC applies depth-dependent gain to compensate for **acoustic attenuation** (~0.5–1 dB/cm/MHz in soft tissue).
6. **Envelope Detector + Log Compression:** Extracts the envelope of the RF echo and compresses its ~100 dB dynamic range to ~40 dB for display.
7. **Scan Converter:** Converts the polar-coordinate (r, θ) echo data into Cartesian pixel coordinates for rectangular display.
8. **Display:** LCD monitor showing the **2D B-mode (Brightness mode) image**, with optional **M-mode** (motion) and **Doppler** (blood flow) overlays.

### 2.3.4 Key Formulas

**Relationship between acoustic properties:**

$$ Z = \rho \cdot c $$

where $Z$ is the **acoustic impedance** (Rayl = kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹), $\rho$ is tissue density, and $c$ is speed of sound in the medium.

**Reflection coefficient at a tissue interface:**

$$ R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 $$

**Depth of reflecting structure** (from echo time-of-flight):

$$ d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} $$

where $c \approx 1540$ m/s in soft tissue, $t$ is the round-trip echo time, and the factor 2 accounts for the round trip.

**Axial (range) resolution:**

$$ R_{\text{axial}} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot f \cdot n_{\text{cycles}}} $$

**Lateral resolution** (governed by beam width and depth):

$$ R_{\text{lateral}} \approx \lambda \cdot \frac{\text{focal depth}}{\text{aperture}} = \frac{c}{f} \cdot \frac{z}{D} $$

## 2.4 Power Supply Unit (PSU) — System Level Analysis

### 2.4.1 Purpose
A PSU converts the **mains AC supply (230 V, 50 Hz in India)** into one or more **stable, regulated, low-ripple DC voltages** required by the analog and digital sub-systems of a biomedical instrument.

### 2.4.2 Block Diagram (Linear Regulated PSU)

```
Mains (230 V AC, 50 Hz) → Step-Down Transformer → Bridge Rectifier → Filter Capacitor → Voltage Regulator → Regulated DC Output
                                                                                       ↑
                                                                                   Feedback (Error Amp + Reference)
```

### 2.4.3 Block-by-Block Operation

1. **Step-Down Transformer:** Reduces 230 V AC to a lower AC voltage (e.g., 12 V, 15 V, 18 V RMS). Provides **galvanic isolation** from the mains for safety — critical in medical equipment.
2. **Bridge Rectifier:** Four diodes in a bridge configuration convert AC into **pulsating DC** (full-wave rectified).
3. **Filter Capacitor (Smoothing Capacitor):** A large electrolytic capacitor (typically 1000 µF to 4700 µF) smooths the pulsating DC into a relatively steady DC, reducing **ripple voltage**.
4. **Voltage Regulator IC:** Maintains constant output despite variations in input voltage or load current. Common ICs:
   - **78xx series** (positive regulators: 7805 → +5 V, 7812 → +12 V, 7815 → +15 V)
   - **79xx series** (negative regulators: 7905 → −5 V, 7912 → −12 V, 7915 → −15 V)
   - **LM317** (adjustable positive)
   - **LM337** (adjustable negative)
5. **Feedback / Error Amplifier:** Compares output with internal bandgap reference and adjusts pass transistor to maintain regulation.
6. **Protection Circuits:** Over-current (current limit), over-temperature (thermal shutdown), over-voltage (crowbar), and short-circuit protection.

### 2.4.4 SMPS Variant (Modern)

Modern biomedical instruments increasingly use **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** for higher efficiency (>85% vs ~40% for linear). The SMPS block chain is:

```
Mains → EMI Filter → Bridge Rectifier → PFC → HV Bulk Cap → HF Switch (MOSFET) → HF Transformer → HF Rectifier → LC Filter → Output → Optocoupler Feedback → PWM Controller
```

Key advantage: the **high-frequency transformer** (operated at 50 kHz – 500 kHz) is far smaller and lighter than a 50 Hz mains transformer.

### 2.4.5 Key Formulas

**RMS value of full-wave rectified DC** (before filtering):

$$ V_{\text{DC,avg}} = \frac{2 V_{\text{peak}}}{\pi} $$

**Ripple voltage (peak-to-peak) for a full-wave rectifier with C-filter:**

$$ V_{\text{ripple(p-p)}} = \frac{I_{\text{load}}}{f_{\text{ripple}} \cdot C} $$

where $f_{\text{ripple}} = 2 \times f_{\text{mains}} = 100$ Hz for full-wave on 50 Hz mains.

**Ripple factor:**

$$ \gamma = \frac{V_{\text{rms(ripple)}}}{V_{\text{DC}}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{\text{rms}}}{V_{\text{DC}}}\right)^2 - 1} $$

**Line regulation:**

$$ \text{Line Reg} = \frac{\Delta V_{\text{out}}}{\Delta V_{\text{in}}} \times 100\% $$

**Load regulation:**

$$ \text{Load Reg} = \frac{V_{\text{NL}} - V_{\text{FL}}}{V_{\text{FL}}} \times 100\% $$

## 2.5 X-Ray Machine — System Level Analysis

### 2.5.1 Purpose
An X-ray machine produces a controlled beam of **X-ray photons** that pass through the patient's body. Differential absorption by tissues of varying density (bone > muscle > fat > air) creates a **projection image** on a detector (film, CR plate, or digital flat-panel detector).

### 2.5.2 Block Diagram

```
Mains → Autotransformer (kV selector) → Step-up HV Transformer (HV Tank) → HV Rectifier → X-Ray Tube ⇄ Patient
                                                                                     ↓
                                                                  Filament Transformer (mA control)
                                                                                     ↓
                                                                                X-Ray Photons
                                                                                     ↓
                                                                             Collimator → Patient → Grid → Image Receptor
```

### 2.5.3 Block-by-Block Operation

1. **Autotransformer (kV Selector):** Adjustable autotransformer sets the **kVp (kilovoltage peak)** of the X-ray tube (range **30 kVp to 150 kVp**). Higher kVp → more penetrating, harder beam.
2. **Step-up High-Voltage Transformer (HV Tank):** Oil-immersed transformer steps the autotransformer output up to the required tube voltage (typically 30–150 kV). The entire secondary is sealed in an oil-filled grounded tank for safety.
3. **High-Voltage Rectifier (HV Rectifier):** A **full-wave bridge of high-voltage diodes** (or in modern systems, a **high-frequency inverter** with voltage multiplier) rectifies the HV AC into HV DC applied to the X-ray tube.
4. **Filament Transformer (mA Control):** A separate low-voltage, high-current transformer heats the **tungsten filament cathode** of the X-ray tube. The filament temperature determines the **tube current (mA)**, which controls the **quantity of X-ray photons** produced.
5. **X-Ray Tube:** A high-vacuum diode tube. The heated cathode emits **thermionic electrons**, which are accelerated toward the angled **tungsten anode** by the kVp. Upon striking the anode, two types of X-rays are produced:
   - **Bremsstrahlung** (braking radiation, continuous spectrum)
   - **Characteristic radiation** (discrete peaks, element-specific)
   The anode is typically rotated at **3000–10,000 RPM** to distribute heat (rotating anode tube).
6. **Collimator:** A lead-shielded device that restricts the X-ray beam to the **region of interest**, minimizing patient dose and scatter.
7. **Patient:** The body region being imaged. Differential absorption creates a **2D projection**.
8. **Anti-Scatter Grid:** A lead-strip grid placed between patient and detector to absorb obliquely scattered photons and improve image contrast.
9. **Image Receptor:** Either **film-screen cassette** (conventional), **Computed Radiography (CR) plate** (photostimulable phosphor), or **Digital Radiography (DR) flat-panel detector** (modern).

### 2.5.4 Key Formulas

**X-ray photon energy (related to kVp):**

$$ E_{\text{photon,max}} = e \cdot V_{\text{tube}} = e \cdot kVp $$

The **maximum photon energy** in eV equals the tube voltage in volts. For a 100 kVp tube:

$$ E_{\text{max}} = 100 \text{ keV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J} $$

**Wavelength of peak (minimum-wavelength) X-ray photon** (Duane-Hunt law):

$$ \lambda_{\min} = \frac{h c}{e \cdot V_{\text{tube}}} = \frac{1240 \text{ eV·nm}}{V(\text{in volts})} \text{ nm} $$

**X-ray intensity (exposure rate) follows an approximate exponential attenuation through tissue:**

$$ I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x} $$

where $\mu$ is the **linear attenuation coefficient** (cm⁻¹) of the tissue at the photon energy, and $x$ is tissue thickness (cm).

**Approximate empirical rule:** Doubling kVp approximately **quadruples** the X-ray intensity (because both photon energy and quantity increase). Halving the mAs halves the number of photons.

**Heat capacity of the anode** (rating constraint):

$$ HU = kVp \cdot mA \cdot t \quad \text{(in Heat Units, single-phase)} $$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Single Reference Table)

| # | System | Formula | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Colorimeter | $A = \log_{10}(I_0/I) = \varepsilon c l$ | $A$=absorbance, $\varepsilon$=molar absorptivity, $c$=conc., $l$=path length | Quantitative biochemistry |
| 2 | Colorimeter | $\%T = 100 \cdot (I/I_0)$ | $T$=transmittance | Direct readout on analog colorimeters |
| 3 | Defibrillator | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | $E$=Joules, $C$=farads, $V$=volts | Energy selection on defibrillator |
| 4 | Defibrillator | $I_{\text{peak}} = V / R_{\text{total}}$ | $V$=cap voltage, $R$=impedance | Peak current through patient |
| 5 | Ultrasound | $d = c t / 2$ | $d$=depth, $c$=1540 m/s, $t$=echo time | Depth calculation from echo time |
| 6 | Ultrasound | $R = [(Z_2-Z_1)/(Z_2+Z_1)]^2$ | $R$=reflection coeff., $Z$=acoustic impedance | Echo strength from tissue interface |
| 7 | Ultrasound | $R_{\text{axial}} = c/(2 f n_{\text{cycles}})$ | $f$=frequency, $n$=cycles per pulse | Smallest resolvable axial feature |
| 8 | Ultrasound | $Z = \rho c$ | $Z$=acoustic impedance, $\rho$=density, $c$=speed of sound | Determines echo generation |
| 9 | PSU | $V_{\text{DC,avg}} = 2V_{\text{peak}}/\pi$ | Full-wave rectified average | Pre-filter DC level estimate |
| 10 | PSU | $V_{\text{ripple(p-p)}} = I_{\text{load}} / (f_{\text{ripple}} C)$ | $f_{\text{ripple}}=2f_{\text{mains}}$, $C$=filter cap | Sizing the smoothing capacitor |
| 11 | PSU | $\gamma = \sqrt{(V_{\text{rms}}/V_{\text{DC}})^2 - 1}$ | Ripple factor | PSU quality metric |
| 12 | X-Ray | $\lambda_{\min} = hc/(eV) = 1240/V$ (nm) | Duane–Hunt law | Shortest wavelength emitted |
| 13 | X-Ray | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | Lambert–Beer for X-rays | Image contrast formation |
| 14 | X-Ray | $HU = kVp \cdot mA \cdot t$ | Heat Units on anode | Tube thermal limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are not academic exercises. A biomedical engineer uses:
> - The **Beer–Lambert law** to calibrate clinical chemistry analyzers (e.g., Siemens Dimension, Roche Cobas).
> - The **defibrillator energy formula** to design charge-control firmware and to choose capacitor banks.
> - The **ultrasound depth formula** to set time-gain-compensation curves and to design beamformer delays.
> - The **PSU ripple formula** to size filter capacitors for ECG/EEG front-ends (which need ripple < 1 mV).
> - The **Duane–Hunt law** to size HV transformer insulation and to compute patient skin dose.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the foundational equations used in the system-level analysis of the five instruments. Each derivation is **fully written out, step-by-step**, as required by the KTU 2024 lab-record and viva standards.

## 3.1 Derivation: Beer–Lambert Law for the Colorimeter

### 3.1.1 Statement
The fraction of monochromatic light absorbed by a thin layer of an absorbing medium is **proportional to the thickness** of the layer and the **concentration** of the absorber.

### 3.1.2 Setup
Consider a thin slice of absorbing solution of thickness $dx$ at depth $x$ inside a cuvette. Let the light intensity at the entry of this slice be $I(x)$ and at the exit be $I(x) + dI$. The intensity lost in traversing $dx$ is:

$$ -dI \propto I(x) \cdot dx $$

The negative sign indicates that intensity **decreases** as $x$ increases.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1.** Write the proportionality with a positive proportionality constant $k_1$:

$$ -dI = k_1 \cdot I(x) \cdot dx $$

**Step 2.** Divide both sides by $I(x)$ to separate variables:

$$ \frac{-dI}{I(x)} = k_1 \cdot dx $$

**Step 3.** Integrate both sides across the full cuvette path length $l$, with $I(0) = I_0$ (incident intensity) and $I(l) = I$ (transmitted intensity):

$$ \int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{-dI}{I} = \int_{0}^{l} k_1 \, dx $$

**Step 4.** Evaluate the left integral:

$$ \int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{-dI}{I} = -\left[ \ln I \right]_{I_0}^{I} = -(\ln I - \ln I_0) = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) $$

**Step 5.** Evaluate the right integral:

$$ \int_{0}^{l} k_1 \, dx = k_1 \cdot l $$

**Step 6.** Equate both sides:

$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = k_1 \cdot l $$

**Step 7.** Recognize that the constant $k_1$ is itself **proportional to the concentration** $c$ of the absorbing species:

$$ k_1 = \varepsilon \cdot c $$

where $\varepsilon$ is the **molar absorptivity** (a property of the absorbing species at a given wavelength).

**Step 8.** Substitute back:

$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l $$

**Step 9.** Convert the natural logarithm to base-10 (instrumentation convention) using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$ 2.303 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l $$

**Step 10.** Define **Absorbance** $A \equiv \log_{10}(I_0/I)$:

$$ A = \frac{\varepsilon c l}{2.303} $$

Or, equivalently, by absorbing the $2.303$ into a redefined constant $\varepsilon'$ (commonly done in analytical chemistry), the standard textbook form is:

$$ \boxed{\,A = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l\,} $$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU lab-record convention uses the **decadic form** $A = \varepsilon c l$, with $\varepsilon$ in L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹. Make sure to use $\log_{10}$ (not $\ln$) when answering KTU numerical questions.

## 3.2 Derivation: Depth of Reflector from Ultrasound Echo Time

### 3.2.1 Setup
A short ultrasound pulse is emitted at $t=0$ from the transducer face. The pulse travels into tissue at the speed of sound $c$, hits a reflecting interface, and returns to the transducer. The round-trip time of the echo is $t$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1.** Total distance travelled by the pulse = $c \cdot t$ (down and back).

**Step 2.** This distance equals **twice the depth** $d$ of the reflecting interface:

$$ 2d = c \cdot t $$

**Step 3.** Solve for $d$:

$$ \boxed{\,d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}\,} $$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> *An ultrasound scanner operating at 5 MHz detects an echo at $t = 26 \,\mu s$. Assuming $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue, find the depth of the reflector.*

**Solution:**

$$ d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{1540 \cdot 26 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.04004}{2} = 0.02002 \text{ m} $$

$$ d \approx 2.0 \text{ cm} $$

## 3.3 Derivation: Defibrillator Discharge Energy

### 3.3.1 Setup
A capacitor of capacitance $C$ is charged to a voltage $V$. The energy stored in the electric field of the capacitor is to be found.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1.** Energy stored in a capacitor when charge $dq$ is added at potential $V = q/C$:

$$ dE = V \cdot dq = \frac{q}{C} \, dq $$

**Step 2.** Total energy from $q=0$ to $q=Q = CV$:

$$ E = \int_{0}^{Q} \frac{q}{C} \, dq = \frac{1}{C} \cdot \left[ \frac{q^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{Q} = \frac{Q^2}{2C} $$

**Step 3.** Substitute $Q = CV$:

$$ E = \frac{(CV)^2}{2C} = \frac{C^2 V^2}{2C} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 $$

$$ \boxed{\,E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2\,} $$

### 3.3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> *A defibrillator uses a 32 µF capacitor. To deliver 360 J, what voltage must the capacitor be charged to?*

**Solution:**

$$ V = \sqrt{\frac{2E}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 360}{32 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{\frac{720}{32 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{2.25 \times 10^{7}} $$

$$ V \approx 4743 \text{ V} \approx 4.74 \text{ kV} $$

## 3.4 Derivation: Power Supply Ripple Voltage

### 3.4.1 Setup
A full-wave bridge rectifier produces a pulsating DC whose period is half the mains period. A filter capacitor $C$ supplies load current $I_{\text{load}}$ between charging peaks.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1.** For full-wave rectification, the ripple frequency is twice the mains frequency:

$$ f_{\text{ripple}} = 2 f_{\text{mains}} = 2 \cdot 50 = 100 \text{ Hz (in India)} $$

**Step 2.** The time between successive charging peaks is the **ripple period**:

$$ T_{\text{ripple}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{ripple}}} = \frac{1}{100} = 10 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 3.** During this interval, the capacitor $C$ discharges by supplying current $I_{\text{load}}$. The total charge removed is:

$$ \Delta Q = I_{\text{load}} \cdot T_{\text{ripple}} = \frac{I_{\text{load}}}{f_{\text{ripple}}} $$

**Step 4.** The voltage drop on the capacitor from losing charge $\Delta Q$:

$$ \Delta V = \frac{\Delta Q}{C} = \frac{I_{\text{load}}}{f_{\text{ripple}} \cdot C} $$

This is the **peak-to-peak ripple voltage**:

$$ \boxed{\,V_{\text{ripple(p-p)}} = \frac{I_{\text{load}}}{f_{\text{ripple}} \cdot C}\,} $$

### 3.4.3 Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> *A 12 V PSU uses a 1000 µF filter capacitor and supplies 100 mA to the load. The mains is 50 Hz, full-wave rectified. Find the ripple voltage.*

**Solution:**

$$ V_{\text{ripple}} = \frac{I_{\text{load}}}{f_{\text{ripple}} \cdot C} = \frac{0.1}{100 \cdot 1000 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.1}{0.1} = 1.0 \text{ V (peak-to-peak)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> After regulation by a 7812 IC, the residual ripple at the output is further attenuated by the **PSRR (Power Supply Rejection Ratio)**, typically 60–80 dB. So the final ripple on the regulated rail is in the **microvolt range** — well within the noise floor of ECG/EEG amplifiers.

## 3.5 Derivation: Duane–Hunt Law (X-Ray)

### 3.5.1 Setup
An electron accelerated through a potential $V$ gains kinetic energy $eV$. Upon striking the anode, the maximum-energy photon is produced when the **entire** kinetic energy of the electron is converted to one photon.

### 3.5.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1.** Maximum photon energy equals electron kinetic energy:

$$ E_{\text{photon,max}} = e V_{\text{tube}} $$

**Step 2.** Using $E = hc/\lambda$ for the photon, set $E_{\text{photon,max}} = hc/\lambda_{\min}$:

$$ \frac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}} = eV $$

**Step 3.** Solve for $\lambda_{\min}$:

$$ \lambda_{\min} = \frac{hc}{eV} $$

**Step 4.** Substitute $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, and simplify the numerical product $hc/e$:

$$ \frac{hc}{e} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 3 \times 10^8}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.241 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V·m} = 1240 \text{ eV·nm} $$

**Step 5.** Final working formula:

$$ \boxed{\,\lambda_{\min}\,(\text{in nm}) = \frac{1240}{V\,(\text{in volts})}\,} $$

### 3.5.3 Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> *A diagnostic X-ray tube is operated at 80 kVp. Find the minimum wavelength of the emitted X-ray spectrum.*

**Solution:**

$$ \lambda_{\min} = \frac{1240}{80000} = 0.0155 \text{ nm} = 1.55 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} $$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Function-to-Block Mapping (KTU Viva Master Table)

This matrix is the **most important single table** for the KTU 2024 lab viva on this topic. Be prepared to draw or recite it.

| Functional Requirement | Colorimeter | Defibrillator | Ultrasound | PSU | X-Ray |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy/Power Source** | Mains (via PSU) | Mains → HV Rectifier → Cap | Mains (via PSU) | **Mains 230 V AC** | Mains → Autotransformer |
| **Input Transducer** | Photodetector (PD/PMT) | ECG electrodes + Paddles | Piezoelectric array | None (it IS the source) | X-ray tube |
| **Signal of Interest** | Light intensity $I$ | ECG (R-wave) | Reflected acoustic echo | AC mains | Electron beam → X-ray photons |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Preamplifier + Log amp | Sync amplifier + Comparator | T/R switch + TGC + Log comp | Bridge + Filter + Regulator | HV rectifier + Filament control |
| **Active Processing** | Microprocessor (calibration) | Microcontroller (timing) | Beamformer + Scan converter | Feedback error amp | kVp/mA control loops |
| **Output** | Display: Absorbance & Conc. | Shock pulse to patient | 2D image on LCD | ±5/±12/±15 V DC rails | Radiographic image on detector |
| **Safety Interlocks** | None critical | R-wave sync, discharge lock | Acoustic output limit | Over-current/temp | Exposure timer, collimator |
| **Key Frequency/Wavelength** | 340–700 nm (light) | DC pulse, 4–12 ms duration | 1–20 MHz (sound) | 50 Hz → 100 Hz ripple | 0.01–0.1 nm (X-ray λ) |
| **KTU-mark-bearing formula** | Beer–Lambert $A = \varepsilon c l$ | $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ | $d = ct/2$ | $V_{\text{ripple}} = I/(fC)$ | $\lambda_{\min} = 1240/V$ |
