<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **Q1.** State Beer–Lambert law. What are the units of molar extinction coefficient?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Beer–Lambert law states that the absorbance of a monochromatic light beam by a solution is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species and the optical path length:
> $$A = \varepsilon c l$$
> where $A$ is absorbance (dimensionless), $\varepsilon$ is the molar extinction coefficient, $c$ is molar concentration (mol/L), and $l$ is the path length (cm). The units of $\varepsilon$ are **L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**. (1 mark for statement, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for units).

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q2.** What is the theoretical Nernst slope of a pH electrode at 25 °C, and why does the measured slope fall below this value in a degraded electrode?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The theoretical Nernst slope at 25 °C is **59.16 mV per pH unit** (2 marks). In a degraded glass electrode, the ion-exchange sites on the membrane become depleted, reducing the activity of the hydrogen-ion transfer and lowering the slope (e.g., to 50 mV/pH). This is detected by two-buffer calibration. (1 mark for explanation).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** on every constant (Beer–Lambert: cm, mol/L; Nernst: V; ESU: Hz). Loss: **0.5 – 1 mark each.**
> 2. **Skipping the "why"** behind a formula. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests *understanding* (Bloom's L3 – L4), not just substitution. Loss: **2 – 3 marks.**
> 3. **Drawing a circuit without labels** (component values, test points). Loss: **1 – 2 marks.**
> 4. **Ignoring safety** in ESU questions. Always mention REM, isolation, and patient-plate monitoring. Loss: **1 mark.**
> 5. **Failing to verify** the linearity range of Beer–Lambert (0.1 < A < 1.0). Loss: **1 mark.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Colorimeter Focus

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a clinical colorimeter. Derive the relationship between absorbance and concentration. Mention any three common faults and their remedies. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A clinical colorimeter is used to estimate haemoglobin concentration by the cyanmethaemoglobin method. The cuvette path length is 1.0 cm and the molar extinction coefficient at 540 nm is $11.0 \times 10^{3}\ \text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$. The measured absorbance of a patient's sample is 0.55. Calculate the molar concentration of haemoglobin. If the molecular weight of haemoglobin is 64 458 g/mol, what is the concentration in g/dL? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the same block diagram as shown in **Section 4.1** (Lamp → Monochromator → Cuvette → Photodetector → Amplifier → Log Amp → ADC → Display). Label each block clearly.
2. **Working (2 marks):** Monochromatic light of intensity $I_0$ passes through the cuvette. Some is absorbed; the transmitted intensity $I$ is measured by the photodiode. The transimpedance amplifier converts the photodiode current to voltage, the log amplifier computes $A = -\log_{10}(I/I_0)$, and the ADC digitises it.
3. **Beer–Lambert derivation (1 mark):** Show the integration $\mathrm{d}I/I = -kc\, \mathrm{d}x$ leading to $A = \varepsilon c l$.
4. **Three common faults and remedies (1 mark):**
   - Lamp dim → replace lamp.
   - Cuvette scratched / dirty → clean or replace.
   - Photodiode drift → recalibrate with blank, or replace detector.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $l = 1.0\ \text{cm}$
- $\varepsilon = 11.0 \times 10^{3}\ \text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$
- $A = 0.55$
- Molecular weight $M = 64\,458\ \text{g/mol}$

**Step 1.** Apply Beer–Lambert law:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \varepsilon c l \\
c &= \frac{A}{\varepsilon l} \\
c &= \frac{0.55}{11.0 \times 10^{3} \times 1.0} \\
c &= 5.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mol/L}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{c = 5.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mol/L}}$$

**Step 2.** Convert to g/L:

$$c = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 64\,458 = 3.223\ \text{g/L}$$

**Step 3.** Convert to g/dL (1 g/L = 0.1 g/dL):

$$c = 3.223 \times 0.1 = 0.3223\ \text{g/dL}$$

$$\boxed{c \approx 0.32\ \text{g/dL}}$$

This is **below the normal range** of haemoglobin (12 – 17 g/dL), indicating severe anaemia. (1 mark for clinical interpretation).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ESU and pH Meter Focus

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
> **(a)** Explain the working of an electrosurgical unit (ESU) with a block diagram. Discuss the function of the Return Electrode Monitor (REM) and list any three safety considerations. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A pH electrode is calibrated at 25 °C using two buffer solutions: pH 7.00 (cell EMF = 0 mV) and pH 4.00 (cell EMF = +177.5 mV). Calculate the slope. If the electrode is then immersed in a blood sample at 37 °C and the EMF is +89.0 mV, compute the pH of the sample using the temperature-corrected Nernst equation. Normal blood pH is 7.35 – 7.45. Is the sample normal, acidic, or alkaline? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Block diagram (3 marks):** Same as **Section 4.3** (Mains → Isolation → Rectifier → DC bus → Oscillator → Driver → PA → Output transformer → Handpiece → Patient → Return plate → REM). Label all blocks.
2. **Working (2 marks):** The Colpitts oscillator produces RF at 300 kHz – 3 MHz. The class-C amplifier boosts this to tens-of-watts. The surgeon activates the handpiece; RF current passes through tissue and returns via the patient plate. Cut mode uses continuous sine; coag uses damped bursts; blend uses a mix.
3. **REM function (1 mark):** The REM injects a small (5 mA) low-frequency current through the patient plate and monitors continuity. If the plate contact degrades, REM opens the safety relay within milliseconds, preventing patient burns at the return site.
4. **Three safety considerations (1 mark):**
   - Use the lowest effective power.
   - Ensure proper patient-plate contact with conductive gel.
   - Never activate near flammable anaesthetics.
   - Inspect handpiece insulation regularly.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1.** Compute the slope from the calibration data:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta \text{pH} &= 7.00 - 4.00 = 3.00 \\
\Delta E &= 0 - 177.5 = -177.5\ \text{mV} \\
S_{25} &= \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta \text{pH}} = \frac{177.5}{3.00} = 59.17\ \text{mV/pH}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{S_{25} \approx 59.17\ \text{mV/pH}}$$

This matches the theoretical **59.16 mV/pH** within calibration tolerance — the electrode is healthy.

**Step 2.** Compute the temperature-corrected slope at 37 °C (310.15 K):

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{37} &= S_{25} \times \frac{T}{298.15} \\
&= 59.16 \times \frac{310.15}{298.15} \\
&= 59.16 \times 1.0403 \\
&\approx 61.55\ \text{mV/pH}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Apply the Nernst equation to the blood sample:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= E^\circ - S_{37} \cdot \text{pH} \\
\text{pH} &= \frac{E^\circ - E}{S_{37}} \\
&= \frac{0 - 89.0}{61.55} \\
&= -1.446\ \text{pH (offset only)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Use the **pH 7 reference point** as the new zero:

$$\text{pH} = 7.00 + \frac{0 - 89.0}{61.55} = 7.00 - 1.446 = 5.55$$

$$\boxed{\text{pH} \approx 5.55}$$

**Step 5 — Clinical interpretation (1 mark):**
The normal blood pH range is 7.35 – 7.45. A pH of 5.55 is **severely acidic** — this is incompatible with life. In practice, such a low value would suggest either a faulty electrode, gross contamination, or a mis-applied calculation. The student must always cross-check with a second buffer. (Note: in exam settings, a more realistic reading of, say, 7.25 would indicate mild acidosis).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Beer–Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon c l$ — valid for $0.1 < A < 1.0$.
- [ ] **Transmittance:** $T = I / I_0 = 10^{-A}$.
- [ ] **Nernst slope at 25 °C:** $S_{25} = 59.16\ \text{mV/pH}$ — verify with two-buffer calibration.
- [ ] **Temperature-corrected slope:** $S_T = 0.05916 \times (T / 298.15)$ V/pH.
- [ ] **Colpitts oscillator frequency:** $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{C_1 + C_2}{L\,C_1 C_2}}$.
- [ ] **ESU operating frequency:** **300 kHz – 3 MHz** (typically 400 kHz cut, 1 MHz coag).
- [ ] **REM (Return Electrode Monitor):** detects patient-plate contact loss, opens safety relay.
- [ ] **pH electrode input impedance:** $\geq 10^{12}\ \Omega$ — always check with a high-Z electrometer.
- [ ] **Photodiode transimpedance amp:** $V_{out} = -I_{PD} \cdot R_f$.
- [ ] **CMRR of pH instrumentation amp:** $\geq 80\ \text{dB}$ (medical-grade: $\geq 100\ \text{dB}$).
- [ ] **Colorimeter linearity test:** plot $A$ vs $c$ for 4 standards; check $R^2 \geq 0.999$.
- [ ] **Safety protocols:** never work on ESU without discharging the HV DC bus; always use a properly rated RF probe.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 skill outcomes tested:** signal-tracing, fault-isolation, and circuit-level simulation (analytical or SPICE).
- [ ] **Always state the working principle first** in any 14-mark question before the block diagram.
- [ ] **Always end with a clinical interpretation** in numerical questions — KTU examiners reward applied thinking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Safety Analysis (ECG/EMG/EEG Machine, Electro Surgical Unit)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL607 - BIO INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Safety Analysis (ECG/EMG/EEG Machine, Electro Surgical Unit)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Electrical Safety Analysis** in the context of biomedical instrumentation is the systematic evaluation of a medical device's interaction with the patient and the operator to ensure that leakage currents, grounding integrity, insulation resistance, and dielectric strength remain within physiologically safe limits prescribed by international standards (IEC 60601-1, IS/IEC 60601). For sensitive patient-connected devices such as the **ECG (Electrocardiograph)**, **EMG (Electromyograph)**, and **EEG (Electroencephalograph)** machines, and for high-power devices like the **Electro Surgical Unit (ESU)**, electrical safety analysis identifies and mitigates the risks of **macroshock** and **microshock** hazards, ensuring patient and operator protection during diagnostic and therapeutic procedures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the Bio-Instrumentation Lab (PCEBL607), Experiment 2 emphasizes *practical safety analysis* — students must measure leakage currents, verify grounding continuity, and interpret the safety classification (Type B, Type BF, Type CF) of bioelectric amplifiers and electrosurgical units.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard)

| Term | Definition | Clinical Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Macroshock** | Current passing through intact skin, causing pain, muscle contraction, or ventricular fibrillation. | Threshold: $\geq 1$ mA perceptible, $\geq 100$ mA dangerous. |
| **Microshock** | Current applied directly to the myocardium (e.g., via catheter or pacing lead) at currents as low as $10\ \mu A$ can induce fibrillation. | 1000× more sensitive than macroshock. |
| **Leakage Current** | Unintended current that flows through protective earth or patient connections due to insulation capacitance/resistance imperfections. | Limited to **$0.1$ mA** for Type CF applied parts. |
| **Auxiliary Current** | Current flowing between patient electrodes not intended for therapeutic use. | Must be $< 0.1$ mA (CF) / $< 0.5$ mA (BF). |
| **Isolated Patient Circuit (F-Type)** | The patient-connected section is galvanically isolated from earth; only a small current can flow via isolation impedance. | Mandatory for ECG/EMG/EEG and ESU return electrode. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Leaky Faucet and the Rubber Glove"**
> 
> Imagine your home's plumbing (the mains electricity). The water flowing through pipes is meant to come out only at the tap (the intended load). However, due to worn washers and old pipe joints, small amounts of water *leak* through the walls (this is **leakage current**).
> 
> - The **earth wire** is the drain pipe — it safely carries away any stray leakage.
> - The **isolation barrier** is the rubber glove between the plumber (patient) and the pipe — it prevents current from reaching the plumber's skin.
> - **Macroshock** is a sudden burst of water on your hand — you feel it immediately.
> - **Microshock** is a tiny drop landing directly on an open wound or a heart catheter — even one drop is fatal.
> 
> Just as plumbers wear gloves and test for leaks, biomedical engineers must measure leakage currents and verify isolation barriers before any device touches a patient.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Safety Thresholds

- **Mains Frequency (India/EU)**: $f = 50$ Hz
- **Mains Voltage (RMS)**: $V_{\text{mains}} = 230$ V
- **DC Body Resistance (wet skin)**: $R_{\text{body}} \approx 1$ k$\Omega$
- **Internal Body Resistance (limb-to-limb)**: $R_{\text{int}} \approx 300$ – $500\ \Omega$
- **Microshock Fibrillation Threshold**: $I_{\text{fib}} \geq \mathbf{10\ \mu A}$ (at $50$ Hz, direct cardiac application)
- **Macroshock "Let-Go" Threshold (adult male)**: $I_{\text{let-go}} \approx \mathbf{16\ mA}$ (RMS, 50/60 Hz)
- **Ventricular Fibrillation Threshold (macroshock)**: $I_{\text{fib}} \approx \mathbf{100\ mA}$ for 1 s, 50 Hz

## 1.5 Why ECG/EMG/EEG Need Special Safety Consideration

These three devices share critical commonalities that elevate their safety profile:

1. **Direct skin/electrode contact** — electrodes provide a low-impedance path (electrolyte gel, $R \approx 5$ k$\Omega$) into the body.
2. **High-gain amplifiers** ($\text{Gain} = 1000$ – $100{,}000$) amplify tiny bioelectric signals ($\mu V$ to mV), but this gain does **not** amplify mains interference (a $230$ V fault would be catastrophic).
3. **Right Leg Drive (RLD) / Driven Ground** circuits in ECG actively inject current back through the patient — any fault here could drive current into the heart.
4. **Long cable runs** act as antennas, picking up $50$ Hz interference and creating common-mode voltages that must be rejected by $> 80$ dB CMRR.

## 1.6 The Electro Surgical Unit (ESU) — A Special Case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ESU** is a high-frequency ($300$ kHz – $3$ MHz), high-power ($50$ – $400$ W) device that **intentionally** passes current through the patient to cut or coagulate tissue. This is the **only** medical device designed to deliver potentially lethal current in a controlled manner. Its safety depends on:
> 1. **Return Electrode Monitoring (REM/CQM)** — verifies the dispersive pad is properly attached.
> 2. **High-frequency isolation** — capacitive coupling to ground must be minimized to prevent alternate-site burns.
> 3. **Isolated patient circuit** — no direct connection between the active electrode circuit and earth ground.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Electrical Hazard in a Biomedical Environment

The hazard chain in any medical device can be decomposed into four sequential elements:

1. **Source** — Mains supply ($230$ V, $50$ Hz) with a grounded neutral.
2. **Coupling Path** — Stray capacitance to chassis/earth; degraded insulation; faulted transformer; broken protective earth.
3. **Patient Interface** — Electrode, catheter, conductive gel, or direct skin contact.
4. **Outcome** — Macroshock (muscle contraction, burns, respiratory paralysis, ventricular fibrillation) or Microshock (silent fibrillation).

### 2.1.1 The Three Primary Leakage Currents

A medical device has **three definable leakage currents** as per IEC 60601-1, each measured under specific fault conditions:

| Leakage Current | Symbol | Path | Normal (NC) Limit | Single Fault (SFC) Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Earth Leakage Current** | $I_{\text{EL}}$ | Mains $\rightarrow$ protective earth conductor | $\leq 0.5$ mA (Class I) | $\leq 1$ mA |
| **Enclosure (Touch/Chassis) Leakage Current** | $I_{\text{EC}}$ | Mains $\rightarrow$ accessible chassis (via $1$ k$\Omega$ body model) | $\leq 0.1$ mA (Type B) | $\leq 0.5$ mA |
| **Patient Leakage Current** | $I_{\text{PL}}$ | Mains $\rightarrow$ patient electrode $\rightarrow$ patient $\rightarrow$ ground | $\leq 0.1$ mA (Type CF) | $\leq 0.5$ mA (Type CF) |
| **Patient Auxiliary Current** | $I_{\text{PA}}$ | Between patient electrodes, no intended function | $\leq 0.1$ mA (Type CF) | $\leq 0.5$ mA (Type CF) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Normal Condition (NC)** = device operating normally.
> **Single Fault Condition (SFC)** = one protection means has failed (e.g., earth wire broken).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Analysis

### Step 1 — Equivalent Circuit of a Faulted Medical Device

Consider a Class I ECG machine with a **broken protective earth** (Single Fault Condition). The chassis of the device, normally held at earth potential, now "floats" via the filter capacitance $C_f$ and insulation leakage resistance $R_{\text{ins}}$ to the live mains conductor.

The voltage appearing on the chassis $V_{\text{chassis}}$ relative to earth is determined by the impedance divider formed by $C_f$, $R_{\text{ins}}$, and the patient body resistance $R_{\text{body}}$.

If a patient touches the chassis (e.g., hand on the ECG housing) and is also connected to a grounded electrode on their leg, current flows from mains $\rightarrow$ chassis $\rightarrow$ patient $\rightarrow$ ground.

### Step 2 — Macroshock Current Calculation

The macroshock current through the patient is given by:

$$
I_{\text{macro}} = \frac{V_{\text{chassis}}}{R_{\text{body}} + R_{\text{contact}}}
$$

Where $R_{\text{body}}$ (limb-to-limb, wet skin) is approximately $1$ k$\Omega$, and $R_{\text{contact}}$ is the contact resistance at the chassis (typically $1$ – $10$ k$\Omega$ dry, $< 1$ k$\Omega$ wet).

### Step 3 — Microshock Pathway

In a microshock scenario, the patient has a **central venous catheter** or **pacing lead** directly contacting the myocardium. The current path is from mains $\rightarrow$ stray capacitance $\rightarrow$ patient cable $\rightarrow$ catheter tip $\rightarrow$ myocardium $\rightarrow$ body tissue $\rightarrow$ ground.

The relevant resistance is the **endocardial-to-ground resistance**, $R_{\text{endo}} \approx 50$ – $500\ \Omega$ (much lower than skin resistance).

### Step 4 — Why Frequency Matters (Skin Impedance Model)

Human skin impedance is a parallel combination of a resistor $R_s$ and a capacitor $C_s$:

$$
Z_{\text{skin}} = \frac{R_s}{1 + j\omega R_s C_s}
$$

At DC and low frequency ($< 1$ kHz), $Z_{\text{skin}}$ is high ($> 10$ k$\Omega$) — skin acts as a barrier.
At high frequency ($> 100$ kHz), $C_s$ short-circuits $R_s$, so $Z_{\text{skin}}$ drops dramatically. This is why **ESU burns at the dispersive electrode** are a major hazard — high-frequency current can pass through skin with negligible sensation or muscle effect, but with significant **dielectric heating** at the tissue level.

### Step 5 — The Role of the Right Leg Drive (RLD) in ECG Safety

The ECG's Right Leg Drive circuit actively drives the patient's right leg to a potential close to the common-mode voltage of the body. If a fault causes the patient cable's center conductor to short to mains, the RLD amplifier (typically op-amp output swing $\pm 15$ V, current limit $5$ – $40$ mA) limits the current. However, **modern ECG machines still require isolation amplifiers** (transformer, optocoupler, or capacitive isolation) rated to withstand $\geq 5000$ V RMS for $1$ minute.

## 2.3 Electro Surgical Unit (ESU) Safety — The REM/CQM System

The **Return Electrode Monitoring (REM)**, also called **Contact Quality Monitoring (CQM)**, is a safety subsystem exclusive to ESUs.

### Operating Principle
1. The ESU delivers RF current ($500$ kHz, $400$ W peak) through the **active electrode** (small surface, high current density, cuts/coagulates).
2. Current returns via the **dispersive (return) electrode** (large surface, low current density, no burn).
3. The REM system passes a small pilot current ($< 5$ mA, at a separate low frequency $\sim 100$ kHz) through the return electrode and measures the impedance.
4. If impedance exceeds threshold ($\sim 150\ \Omega$ — indicating partial detachment), the ESU **disables RF output** within $< 1$ second and sounds an alarm.

### Two Types of REM Systems
- **Single-plate with REM**: One large plate + impedance monitoring.
- **Dual-plate (split)**: Two plates separated by an insulator; ESU measures inter-plate impedance. More reliable, but the entire plate must be in contact.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse REM with a *temperature sensor*. REM is an **impedance-based** monitor, not a thermal cutoff. Burn injury from ESU is not prevented by temperature; it is prevented by maintaining low current density at the return electrode.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = \frac{V}{R_{\text{body}}}$ | Current through body for a given voltage | A |
| 2 | $I_{\text{macro}} = \frac{V_{\text{leak}}}{R_{\text{skin}} + R_{\text{int}}}$ | Macroshock current through intact skin | A |
| 3 | $I_{\text{micro}} = \frac{V_{\text{leak}}}{R_{\text{endo}}}$ | Microshock current (catheter to heart) | A |
| 4 | $Z_{\text{skin}} = \frac{R_s}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_s C_s)^2}}$ | Skin impedance magnitude | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | $I_{\text{leak}} = j \omega C_f V_{\text{mains}}$ | Capacitive earth leakage current | A |
| 6 | $V_{\text{applied}} = I \cdot R_{\text{iso}} + j I \cdot \frac{1}{\omega C_{\text{iso}}}$ | Voltage across isolation barrier | V |
| 7 | $P_{\text{density}} = \frac{I^2 R_{\text{tissue}}}{A_{\text{electrode}}}$ | Power density at electrode-tissue interface | W/m² |
| 8 | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | dB |
| 9 | $\eta = \frac{P_{\text{delivered}}}{P_{\text{forward}}}$ | ESU power delivery efficiency (matching) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_{\text{fib}} = I_{\text{fib}} \cdot R_{\text{body}}$ | Voltage to cause fibrillation | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For boundary conditions: $I_{\text{fib, micro}} = 10\ \mu A$ at $50$ Hz (lowest physiological threshold), and $I_{\text{let-go}} = 16$ mA RMS at 50 Hz. These are **standard physiological constants** and must be quoted verbatim in KTU answers.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Electrical Safety Audits**: Biomedical engineers use **Electrical Safety Analyzers** (e.g., Fluke ESA612, Rigel 288+) to verify each device's compliance with IEC 60601-1.
- **Defibrillator Protection**: ECG amplifiers must survive a $5$ kV defibrillator pulse without damage to the patient or operator. This is achieved with high-voltage spark gaps, transient voltage suppressors (TVS), and isolation barrier rated to $\geq 10$ kV.
- **MRI Conditional Equipment**: Devices used in MRI must be non-magnetic and have no eddy current heating — stricter safety rules apply.
- **ESU in Laparoscopy**: Here, the active electrode is inside a gas-filled cavity; the return path may use a capacitive return to the abdominal wall — a faulty dispersive pad can cause **alternate-site burns** at the ECG electrode or any other skin-electrode contact.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Capacitive Earth Leakage Current

The **dominant** source of leakage current in a well-insulated (Class I) medical device is the **Y-capacitor** placed across the line and neutral for EMI filtering. These capacitors are typically $C_f = 1\ \text{nF}$ – $4.7\ \text{nF}$.

### Step 1: Write the capacitive reactance

The impedance of the filter capacitor at angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$ is purely reactive:

$$
X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C_f} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C_f}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Ohm's law to find the leakage current

The mains RMS voltage $V_{\text{mains}}$ drives a current through this reactance:

$$
I_{\text{leak}} = \frac{V_{\text{mains}}}{X_C} = \frac{V_{\text{mains}}}{\dfrac{1}{2\pi f C_f}} = 2\pi f C_f V_{\text{mains}}
$$

### Step 3: Insert numerical values for a typical case

Take $C_f = 2.2\ \text{nF}$, $V_{\text{mains}} = 230$ V RMS, $f = 50$ Hz:

$$
I_{\text{leak}} = 2 \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 2.2 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 230
$$

Compute the coefficient:

$$
2\pi \cdot 50 = 100\pi \approx 314.159
$$

Then:

$$
I_{\text{leak}} = 314.159 \cdot 2.2 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 230
$$

$$
= 314.159 \cdot 506 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
= 1.5896 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{\text{leak}} \approx 159\ \mu\text{A} = 0.159\ \text{mA}}
$$

### Step 4: Compare with safety limit

For a Type BF applied part (e.g., ECG), single-fault enclosure leakage limit is $0.5$ mA. Our calculated $0.159$ mA is **within the limit** but leaves only a small margin. This is why **good design** keeps $C_f \leq 2.2$ nF and adds a **dual-Y** configuration with center tap to chassis to reduce leakage further.

## 3.2 Derivation — Voltage Threshold for Microshock

### Step 1: Recognize the microshock current threshold

The minimum current known to cause ventricular fibrillation when applied directly to the myocardium is:

$$
I_{\text{fib, micro}} = 10\ \mu\text{A} = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}
$$

### Step 2: Recognize the endocardial resistance

The resistance from a catheter tip inside the heart to the body surface is approximately:

$$
R_{\text{endo}} = 500\ \Omega
$$

### Step 3: Compute the voltage that would cause fibrillation

By Ohm's law:

$$
V_{\text{fib, micro}} = I_{\text{fib, micro}} \cdot R_{\text{endo}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{fib, micro}} = (10 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 500
$$

$$
= 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{fib, micro}} = 5\ \text{mV}}
$$

### Step 4: Interpret the result

**A 5 mV signal — 1000× smaller than a flashlight battery's voltage — can cause cardiac fibrillation if it reaches the heart via a catheter.** This is why isolation barriers must have insulation resistance $> 10^{10}\ \Omega$ and the patient leakage current must be limited to $0.1$ mA (Type CF) — a single 5 mV fault could kill.

## 3.3 Derivation — Total Patient Leakage Under Single Fault

### Step 1: Set up the equivalent circuit

Consider a Type CF ECG with the protective earth broken (SFC). The leakage paths to the patient are:
- Through the input coupling capacitor $C_{\text{in}} = 100$ pF
- Through the insulation resistance $R_{\text{ins}} = 10^{10}\ \Omega$ (good design)
- Through the skin-electrode resistance $R_{\text{electrode}} = 5$ k$\Omega$ (with gel)

### Step 2: Compute the capacitive path current

$$
I_C = 2\pi f C_{\text{in}} V_{\text{mains}} = 2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 230
$$

$$
= 314.159 \cdot 23 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
= 7.226 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A} = 7.23\ \mu\text{A}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the resistive path current

$$
I_R = \frac{V_{\text{mains}}}{R_{\text{ins}}} = \frac{230}{10^{10}} = 2.3 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{A} = 23\ \text{nA}
$$

### Step 4: Sum the contributions (they add in magnitude for worst case)

$$
I_{\text{total}} = I_C + I_R = 7.23\ \mu\text{A} + 0.023\ \mu\text{A} \approx 7.25\ \mu\text{A}
$$

### Step 5: Compare with safety limit

For Type CF, the patient leakage current SFC limit is $50\ \mu\text{A}$ (per IEC 60601-1, 3rd edition). Our calculated $7.25\ \mu\text{A}$ is well within safe limits, demonstrating the effectiveness of high-impedance isolation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Safety Classification of Devices

| Device | Type | Applied Part | Patient Connection | Max Patient Leakage (SFC) | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ECG | CF (typically BF) | Type CF electrodes | Skin electrodes (and sometimes precordial) | $50\ \mu$A | Risk of catheter-coupled microshock in cath lab |
| EMG | BF | Needle or surface electrode | Skin / muscle | $500\ \mu$A | No direct cardiac contact in routine use |
| EEG | CF (ideally BF) | Scalp electrodes | Skin (scalp) | $50\ \mu$A | Neonatal/neurosurgical patients may have intracranial leads |
| ESU | CF (the patient circuit) | Active + dispersive electrode | Skin (large area) | $50\ \mu$A | Direct contact with patient, high-power |
| Defibrillator | CF | Paddles / pads | Skin (with gel) | $50\ \mu$A | Must withstand and deliver high voltage |
| Pulse Oximeter | BF | Finger/toe sensor | Skin | $500\ \mu$A | Low risk; non-cardiac contact |
| Infusion Pump | CF | IV line | Vascular access | $50\ \mu$A | Current can reach central circulation |

## 3.5 Derivation — ESU Power Density at the Active vs Dispersive Electrode

### Step 1: Define the variables

- ESU power output: $P = 100$ W
- Active electrode area (small): $A_a = 10\ \text{mm}^2 = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$
- Dispersive electrode area (large): $A_d = 100\ \text{cm}^2 = 0.01\ \text{m}^2$

### Step 2: Power density at active electrode

$$
P_{\text{d,a}} = \frac{P}{A_a} = \frac{100}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 10^7\ \text{W/m}^2
$$

### Step 3: Power density at dispersive electrode

$$
P_{\text{d,d}} = \frac{P}{A_d} = \frac{100}{0.01} = 10^4\ \text{W/m}^2
$$

### Step 4: Ratio

$$
\frac{P_{\text{d,a}}}{P_{\text{d,d}}} = \frac{10^7}{10^4} = 1000
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **active electrode has 1000× higher power density** than the dispersive electrode. This is why it cuts/coagulates tissue (localized vaporization at $\sim 100$°C) while the dispersive pad remains cool. If the dispersive pad partially detaches, its effective area drops, and power density rises — risking a **burn injury**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define microshock. Why is it considered more dangerous than macroshock?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Microshock** is the passage of small electrical current ($10\ \mu A$ to $1$ mA) directly into the myocardium, typically via an internal conductor such as a cardiac catheter, pacemaker lead, or saline-filled central line. (1.5 marks)

It is **more dangerous than macroshock** because:
1. The current bypasses the skin's natural resistance (which is the body's primary defense), reaching the heart directly.
2. The fibrillation threshold is as low as **$10\ \mu A$** at $50$ Hz — about 1000 times lower than macroshock.
3. The patient is often sedated or unconscious, so the warning signs (muscle contraction, pain) are absent.
4. Cardiac muscle is uniquely susceptible to $50/60$ Hz current due to the relative refractory period of the ventricular myocytes. (1.5 marks)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**List any three leakage currents defined by IEC 60601-1. State one limit for each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Leakage Current | Path | Limit (Type CF, SFC) |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Earth Leakage Current ($I_{\text{EL}}$) | Mains $\rightarrow$ protective earth conductor | $1$ mA (Class I, SFC) |
| 2. Enclosure Leakage Current ($I_{\text{EC}}$) | Mains $\rightarrow$ accessible chassis | $0.5$ mA (CF, SFC) |
| 3. Patient Leakage Current ($I_{\text{PL}}$) | Mains $\rightarrow$ patient electrode $\rightarrow$ ground | $50\ \mu A$ (CF, SFC) |

(3 marks — 1 mark per row)

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice

> **Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme allows the student to answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B (each for 14 marks). Both are provided below.

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the working of an Electro Surgical Unit (ESU) and the importance of the Return Electrode Monitoring (REM) system.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) An ESU dispersive pad has a contact area of $100\ \text{cm}^2$ with the patient. If the ESU delivers $80$ W of power and $60\%$ of it is delivered to the active electrode area of $5\ \text{mm}^2$, calculate the power density at the dispersive electrode. Comment on whether this can cause a burn. Given skin burn threshold = $5 \times 10^3\ \text{W/m}^2$.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Working of ESU and REM System (7 marks):**

The **Electro Surgical Unit (ESU)** is a high-frequency ($300$ kHz to $3$ MHz) electrosurgical generator that uses RF current to cut tissue (Continuous mode) or coagulate tissue (Intermittent mode). (1 mark)

**Block diagram and working:**
1. **Oscillator section** — Generates $500$ kHz sinusoidal RF waveform at $50$ – $400$ W power. (0.5 mark)
2. **Power amplifier** — Boosts the signal to deliver the required cutting/coagulation power. (0.5 mark)
3. **Output transformer with high isolation** — Provides galvanic isolation ($\geq 1500$ V RMS) between mains and patient. (0.5 mark)
4. **Active electrode** — Small surface area ($\sim 5\ \text{mm}^2$), high current density $\sim 10^7\ \text{W/m}^2$, vaporizes intracellular water and cuts tissue. (0.5 mark)
5. **Dispersive (return) electrode** — Large surface area ($\sim 100\ \text{cm}^2$), low current density $\sim 10^4\ \text{W/m}^2$, returns current safely to the ESU. (0.5 mark)

**REM (Return Electrode Monitoring) System** (2.5 marks):
- The REM system passes a small pilot current ($< 5$ mA) at a separate low frequency (e.g., $100$ kHz) through the dispersive electrode.
- It continuously measures the impedance of the patient-pad interface.
- If impedance exceeds a threshold of $\sim 150\ \Omega$ (indicating partial detachment or drying of gel), the REM disables the RF output within $< 1$ s and sounds an audible alarm.
- Modern **dual-plate (split-pad) REM** systems use two inter-digitated plates and measure the inter-plate resistance, providing superior detection of partial detachment.
- **Importance:** Without REM, a partially detached pad can concentrate current density to a small area, causing severe **alternate-site burns** (often at the ECG electrode site, hip, or other grounded contact).

**Burn Threshold Check** (1.5 marks):
- Calculated $P_d$ at the active electrode is $9.6 \times 10^6\ \text{W/m}^2$ — far exceeds the burn threshold; this is intentional, since the active electrode is designed to vaporize tissue.
- Calculated $P_d$ at the dispersive electrode is $320\ \text{W/m}^2$ — well below the burn threshold of $5 \times 10^3\ \text{W/m}^2$. Hence the dispersive pad does not cause a burn **provided full contact is maintained**, validating the role of REM.

---

**(b) Power Density Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Given data** (1 mark)
- Total ESU power: $P_{\text{total}} = 80$ W
- Active electrode area: $A_a = 5\ \text{mm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$
- Dispersive electrode area: $A_d = 100\ \text{cm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 = 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^2$

**Step 2: Power at dispersive electrode** (2 marks)

Since $60\%$ is delivered to the active electrode, the remaining $40\%$ reaches the dispersive electrode (assuming all delivered power eventually returns via the dispersive pad; the small loss to radiated heat is ignored):

$$
P_d = 0.40 \times 80\ \text{W} = 32\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 3: Power density at dispersive electrode** (2 marks)

$$
P_{\text{density}} = \frac{P_d}{A_d} = \frac{32}{10^{-2}} = 3200\ \text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Compare with burn threshold** (1 mark)

Burn threshold: $P_{\text{threshold}} = 5 \times 10^3\ \text{W/m}^2 = 5000\ \text{W/m}^2$

$$
P_{\text{density}} = 3200\ \text{W/m}^2 < P_{\text{threshold}} = 5000\ \text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 5: Comment** (1 mark)

The power density at the dispersive electrode ($3200\ \text{W/m}^2$) is **below** the burn threshold of $5000\ \text{W/m}^2$, so **no burn is expected at the dispersive pad under normal full-contact conditions**. However, if the pad partially detaches and the effective contact area drops to, say, $25\%$ of its original value, the power density would rise to $12{,}800\ \text{W/m}^2$ — causing a severe burn. This is precisely why the **REM system is critical**.

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Explain the difference between macroshock and microshock with suitable examples. Why is $50/60$ Hz current more dangerous than DC or higher frequency AC?** **(7 marks)**

**(b) In a single-fault condition, the chassis of a Class I ECG machine is at a potential of $100$ V RMS (relative to earth) due to a filter capacitor of $2.2\ \text{nF}$. A patient touches the chassis with a wet hand ($R_{\text{skin}} = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$) and is also connected to a grounded right-leg electrode. Calculate the current through the patient and identify the physiological effect.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Macroshock vs Microshock and Frequency Effects (7 marks):**

**Macroshock** (1.5 marks):
- Definition: Current passing through the body via intact skin contact (e.g., hand touching a faulty chassis while feet are grounded).
- Pathway: $\text{Mains} \rightarrow \text{chassis} \rightarrow \text{skin} \rightarrow \text{body tissue} \rightarrow \text{ground}$.
- Key thresholds at $50$ Hz: Perception $\approx 1$ mA, pain $\approx 5$ mA, "let-go" $\approx 16$ mA, respiratory paralysis $\approx 50$ mA, ventricular fibrillation $\approx 100$ mA (1 s exposure).
- Example: A nurse touching a faulty defibrillator with wet hands receives a painful shock.

**Microshock** (1.5 marks):
- Definition: Current applied directly to the heart via an internal conductor (catheter, pacemaker lead, saline-filled central line), bypassing the skin's resistance.
- Pathway: $\text{Mains} \rightarrow \text{stray coupling} \rightarrow \text{catheter} \rightarrow \text{myocardium} \rightarrow \text{ground}$.
- Key threshold: Ventricular fibrillation at $\sim 10\ \mu A$ ($50$ Hz).
- Example: A patient with a central venous catheter receives a microshock from a faulty infusion pump; this can cause silent fibrillation and death.

**Why 50/60 Hz is most dangerous** (4 marks):
1. **Cellular refractory period match**: The relative refractory period of ventricular myocytes is $\sim 50$ – $100$ ms, corresponding to $10$ – $20$ Hz. A $50$ Hz signal can repeatedly re-trigger disorganized contractions (fibrillation). (1 mark)
2. **DC offset**: At DC, nerves accommodate; sustained muscle contraction occurs but heart cells repolarize between excitations. At $50$ Hz, the half-cycle is $10$ ms — too short to allow complete repolarization, leading to sustained depolarization and fibrillation. (1 mark)
3. **Higher frequency ($> 1$ kHz)**: At high frequency, the cell membrane capacitance shorts out the applied voltage; the cell cannot follow the rapid alternations. Hence ESU at $500$ kHz can pass through tissue with little muscle or nerve effect — only heating. (1 mark)
4. **Skin impedance at $50$ Hz is moderate** ($\sim 5$ k$\Omega$ wet, $\sim 100$ k$\Omega$ dry), allowing substantial current to enter the body. At DC, the impedance is higher; at high frequency, it is lower but the physiological effect diminishes. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Patient Current Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Given data** (1 mark)
- Chassis fault voltage: $V_{\text{chassis}} = 100$ V RMS
- Skin resistance (wet hand): $R_{\text{skin}} = 1\ \text{k}\Omega = 1000\ \Omega$
- The patient is grounded via the right-leg electrode, so the body internal resistance is in series. Use the body-to-ground resistance $R_{\text{body}} \approx 500\ \Omega$ (limb-to-limb with gel electrode).

**Step 2: Total loop resistance** (1 mark)

$$
R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{skin}} + R_{\text{body}} = 1000 + 500 = 1500\ \Omega
$$

**Step 3: Compute current** (2 marks)

$$
I = \frac{V_{\text{chassis}}}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{100}{1500}
$$

$$
= 0.0667\ \text{A} = 66.7\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 4: Physiological interpretation** (3 marks)

Comparing with the macroshock thresholds at $50$ Hz:
- $66.7$ mA $> 50$ mA (respiratory paralysis threshold)
- $66.7$ mA $< 100$ mA (ventricular fibrillation threshold for 1 s exposure)
- $66.7$ mA $> 16$ mA ("let-go" threshold — patient cannot release the chassis)
- $66.7$ mA $> 5$ mA (pain threshold — extreme pain)

**Physiological effect**: The patient experiences **severe pain, involuntary muscle contraction (inability to let go), and respiratory paralysis** if exposure exceeds a few seconds. With sustained exposure, **ventricular fibrillation is likely** (since $66.7$ mA is close to the $100$ mA fibrillation threshold, especially for exposures $> 1$ s or for patients with pre-existing cardiac conditions). **This scenario is potentially lethal** and would be flagged as a critical safety failure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse the "let-go" current ($16$ mA) with the perception current ($1$ mA). Many students mix up the thresholds. Memorize them in ascending order: Perception ($1$ mA) $\to$ Pain ($5$ mA) $\to$ Let-Go ($16$ mA) $\to$ Respiratory Paralysis ($50$ mA) $\to$ Fibrillation ($100$ mA).
> 2. **Always specify "RMS"** when quoting mains currents; peak values are $\sqrt{2}$ higher.
> 3. For microshock, the threshold is **$10\ \mu A$** at $50$ Hz — students often write "1 mA" by mistake.
> 4. In calculations, **include both the skin and body resistance** in series; using only $R_{\text{skin}}$ is a common error.
> 5. For ESU power density, remember to **convert $\text{mm}^2$ and $\text{cm}^2$ to $\text{m}^2$** (factor of $10^{-6}$ and $10^{-4}$ respectively).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 Experiment 2**

- [ ] **Macroshock** = current through intact skin; threshold for fibrillation $\sim 100$ mA at $50$ Hz.
- [ ] **Microshock** = current direct to heart via catheter; fibrillation threshold $\sim 10\ \mu A$ at $50$ Hz.
- [ ] **50/60 Hz is most dangerous** due to match with the ventricular refractory period.
- [ ] **DC** = sustained muscle contraction, no fibrillation (cells repolarize).
- [ ] **High frequency ($> 100$ kHz)** = reduced nerve/muscle effect, but causes **dielectric heating** (ESU).
- [ ] **Three leakage currents**: Earth ($I_{\text{EL}}$), Enclosure ($I_{\text{EC}}$), Patient ($I_{\text{PL}}$).
- [ ] **Type B** (body) — no patient contact (e.g., X-ray, MRI).
- [ ] **Type BF** (body floating) — skin contact, no direct cardiac (e.g., EMG, ultrasound).
- [ ] **Type CF** (cardiac floating) — direct cardiac contact (e.g., ECG in cath lab, ESU, defibrillator).
- [ ] **Patient leakage limit (CF, SFC)**: $50\ \mu A$ (3rd edition IEC 60601-1).
- [ ] **ESU REM system** monitors return-electrode impedance; disables RF if $Z > 150\ \Omega$.
- [ ] **Active electrode area** $\sim 5\ \text{mm}^2$ (small, hot, cuts); **dispersive electrode** $\sim 100\ \text{cm}^2$ (large, cool, safe).
- [ ] **Power density at active electrode** $\sim 10^7\ \text{W/m}^2$ (vaporizes tissue).
- [ ] **Power density at dispersive electrode** $\sim 10^4\ \text{W/m}^2$ (no burn, if full contact).
- [ ] **Y-capacitor leakage** $I = 2\pi f C_f V_{\text{mains}}$ — keep $C_f \leq 2.2$ nF to stay below $0.5$ mA.
- [ ] **Microshock voltage threshold**: $V_{\text{fib, micro}} = 5$ mV (across $R_{\text{endo}} = 500\ \Omega$).
- [ ] **Isolation barrier rating**: $\geq 5000$ V RMS for 1 minute (defibrillator-proof ECG).
- [ ] **Earth continuity resistance** must be $< 0.2\ \Omega$ (Class I devices).
- [ ] **Right Leg Drive (RLD)** actively drives patient right leg to common-mode voltage; limits macroshock current.
- [ ] **CMRR** of ECG amplifier must be $> 80$ dB (typically $100$ – $120$ dB in modern machines).
- [ ] **Standard governing biomedical safety**: **IEC 60601-1** (general), IEC 60601-2-25 (ECG), IEC 60601-2-40 (EMG/EEG), IEC 60601-2-2 (ESU).
- [ ] **Defibrillator protection**: ECG must survive $5$ kV pulse; uses spark gaps, TVS diodes, and barrier rated to $\geq 10$ kV.
- [ ] **Line Isolation Monitor (LIM)**: detects first fault in isolated power systems (used in operating rooms and ICUs); alarms at $\leq 5$ mA hazard current.
- [ ] **Alternate-site burns** in ESU occur at ECG electrodes or other grounded patient contacts when the dispersive pad is faulty.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Electrical Safety Analysis**
> 
> These notes are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for **PCEBL607 — Bio Instrumentation Lab**, Module 2. For laboratory records, ensure your experiment write-up includes: (i) the circuit diagram, (ii) the measured leakage currents under NC and SFC, (iii) the type classification, and (iv) a discussion of whether the device passes the IEC 60601-1 limits.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing and Maintenance of Medical Equipment - Performance Testing (Patient Monitor, Defibrillator, Ventilator, Infusion Pump)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL607 - BIO INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Testing and Maintenance of Medical Equipment - Performance Testing (Patient Monitor, Defibrillator, Ventilator, Infusion Pump)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Performance Testing of Medical Equipment – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** Performance testing of medical equipment is the systematic, documented process of verifying that a medical device meets its manufacturer-specified functional parameters, safety thresholds, and accuracy benchmarks under both simulated and real clinical load conditions, in accordance with national and international standards (IEC 60601-1, IEC 62353, NFPA-99, AAMI, ISO 13485).

In the context of **PCEBL607 – Bio Instrumentation Lab**, Module Experiment 2 focuses on the hands-on **Performance Verification** of four critical care devices that form the backbone of any ICU/CCU setup:

| S.No | Equipment | Primary Clinical Function | Risk Class (as per IEC) |
|------|-----------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Patient Monitor** | Continuous display of vital signs (ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, IBP, Temp, Resp) | Class IIb |
| 2 | **Defibrillator** | Delivers controlled electrical shock to restore normal cardiac rhythm | Class III |
| 3 | **Ventilator** | Mechanically assists or controls patient breathing via positive pressure | Class III |
| 4 | **Infusion Pump** | Delivers precise volumes of fluids/medications at controlled rates | Class IIb |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why performance testing matters:** A defibrillator delivering 360 J when set to 200 J can cause fatal burns. An infusion pump with 15% flow error can cause drug toxicity. Hence, performance testing is not optional — it is a **legal, ethical, and clinical mandate** under the Indian Medical Device Rules 2017 (MDR-2017) and the National Accreditation Board for Hospitals (NABH) standards.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Car Service Center" Model

Imagine a hospital full of medical devices as a **fleet of cars** running 24×7. Just like you take your car for periodic service to check brake pads, oil level, tire pressure, and engine performance, medical equipment undergoes:

- **Performance Test** → Equivalent to the **full vehicle health check-up** (engine, brakes, electricals)
- **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** → Equivalent to **regular oil change and tire rotation**
- **Calibration** → Equivalent to **wheel alignment and speedometer calibration**
- **Safety Test** → Equivalent to **brake test and crash safety inspection**

The **Biomedical Engineer (BMET)** is the "service engineer" of this fleet. Just as a faulty brake cannot be ignored, a faulty defibrillator cannot be sent to the ICU.

## 1.3 General Performance Testing Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Frame:** Every performance test follows the **5-Phase Protocol**:
>
> 1. **Visual & Mechanical Inspection** (housing, cables, connectors, display integrity)
> 2. **Electrical Safety Test** (Earth continuity, Leakage current, Insulation resistance per IEC 60601-1)
> 3. **Functional / Operational Test** (device performs intended function)
> 4. **Performance / Accuracy Test** (output matches set values within tolerance)
> 5. **Documentation & Labeling** (test results logged, next-due-date sticker affixed)

**Standard Reference Metrics (Bold = KTU Frequently Asked):**

- **Earth Resistance:** < **0.1 Ω** (per IEC 60601-1)
- **Earth Leakage Current (Normal Condition):** < **500 µA** (Class I) / < **100 µA** (Class II, type CF)
- **Patient Leakage Current (Normal Condition):** < **10 µA** (Type CF) / < **100 µA** (Type B)
- **Dielectric Strength:** **1500 V AC** for 1 minute (Class I)
- **Accuracy Tolerance (defibrillator energy):** ± **15%** or ± **3 J**, whichever is greater

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Performance Testing – Device-Wise Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 PATIENT MONITOR – Performance Testing

A patient monitor has **multiple parameter modules** (ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, IBP, Temperature, Respiration). Each module is tested independently.

#### A. ECG Module Test
- **Test Equipment Used:** ECG Patient Simulator (e.g., Fluke MPS450, Bio-Tek Index 2)
- **Test Parameters:**
  - Heart Rate accuracy at **30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 200, 240, 300 BPM** (six preset values recommended)
  - Amplitude accuracy (mV)
  - Lead-off detection
  - Pacemaker pulse detection
- **Acceptance Criteria:**
  - Heart Rate error: **± 1 BPM** or **± 1%**, whichever is greater
  - Amplitude error: **± 5%** of full scale
  - Bandwidth: **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** (diagnostic mode)

#### B. SpO₂ Module Test
- **Test Equipment:** SpO₂ Finger Simulator (e.g., Fluke Index 2 SpO₂, Masimo Index)
- **Test Parameters:** SpO₂ values at **70%, 85%, 90%, 95%, 98%, 100%**; Perfusion Index range
- **Acceptance Criteria:** SpO₂ error: **± 2%** over 70–100% range

#### C. NIBP (Non-Invasive Blood Pressure) Module Test
- **Test Equipment:** NIBP Simulator with calibrated cuff (e.g., Fluke BP Pump 2, Cufflink)
- **Test Parameters:** Systolic/Diastolic/Mean at 120/80, 150/100, 90/60, 60/40 mmHg
- **Acceptance Criteria:** **± 3 mmHg** error, cuff inflation/deflation cycle timing check

#### D. IBP (Invasive Blood Pressure) Module Test
- **Test Equipment:** IBP Simulator / Mercury column comparison
- **Test Parameters:** Static pressure at 0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250 mmHg
- **Acceptance Criteria:** **± 1 mmHg** or **± 1%** of reading

### 2.1.2 DEFIBRILLATOR – Performance Testing

The defibrillator delivers a **truncated exponential or monophasic/biphasic waveform** to the patient.

#### A. Energy Output Test
- **Test Equipment:** Defibrillator Analyzer (e.g., Fluke Impulse 7000DP, Metron QA-45)
- **Test Procedure:**
  1. Connect defibrillator paddles/pads to analyzer (set to 50 Ω load per IEC 60601-2-4)
  2. Charge defibrillator to test energies: **2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100, 200, 270, 360 J**
  3. Discharge and record delivered energy
- **Acceptance Criteria:** Delivered energy = Set energy **± 15%** OR **± 3 J** (whichever is greater)
- **Why 50 Ω?** Because the human thorax has an impedance of approximately **25–150 Ω**; the standard 50 Ω load simulates the median patient impedance (IEC 60601-2-4).

#### B. Charge Time Test
- **Test:** Time to charge to 360 J from fully discharged state
- **Acceptance:** **< 7 seconds** for modern biphasic defibrillators; **< 10 seconds** older units

#### C. Synchronized Cardioversion Test
- **Test:** Defibrillator discharges at correct point in R-wave (peak of QRS)
- **Acceptance:** Synchronization delay **< 60 ms** from R-peak

#### D. Pacer Module Test (if present)
- **Test:** Pacing rate (ppm) and pulse amplitude (mA)
- **Acceptance:** Rate ± **1 ppm**; Amplitude ± **10%**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Always test defibrillators with a **50 Ω non-inductive resistive load**. Do not discharge into open circuit (will damage the device) or into a short circuit (will give a false-low energy reading).

### 2.1.3 VENTILATOR – Performance Testing

#### A. Tidal Volume Test
- **Test Equipment:** Ventilator Tester / Calibrated Test Lung (e.g., Fluke VT900A, AccuLung)
- **Test Parameters:** Set Vt = **100, 200, 300, 500, 800, 1000 mL**
- **Acceptance Criteria:** Delivered Vt error: **± 10%** of set value OR **± 10 mL**, whichever is greater

#### B. Respiratory Rate Test
- **Test:** Set RR = 10, 15, 20, 30, 40 breaths/min
- **Acceptance:** ± **1 breath/min** OR ± **5%**

#### C. PEEP (Positive End-Expiratory Pressure) Test
- **Test:** Set PEEP = 0, 5, 10, 15, 20 cmH₂O
- **Acceptance:** ± **2 cmH₂O** or ± **10%**

#### D. FiO₂ (Fraction of Inspired Oxygen) Test
- **Test Equipment:** Oxygen Analyzer (e.g., Maxtec, Teledyne)
- **Acceptance:** ± **3%** of set FiO₂ (e.g., set 60% → acceptable 57–63%)

#### E. Pressure & Flow Waveform Test
- Use test lung to simulate compliance (C = 50 mL/cmH₂O) and resistance (R = 5 cmH₂O/L/s)
- Verify peak inspiratory pressure (PIP) **± 2 cmH₂O**

### 2.1.4 INFUSION PUMP – Performance Testing

#### A. Flow Rate Accuracy Test
- **Test Equipment:** Volumetric measurement (graduated cylinder) or flow analyzer
- **Test Procedure (Gravimetric Method per IEC 60601-2-24):**
  1. Set flow rate = **1, 5, 25, 100, 200, 400, 500, 1000 mL/h**
  2. Run for 60–120 minutes
  3. Measure actual volume delivered
- **Acceptance Criteria (Trumpet Curve):** Flow rate error: **± 5%** of set rate

#### B. Occlusion Alarm Test
- **Test:** Clamp the IV line; time until "occlusion" alarm activates at each pressure limit
- **Acceptance:** Alarm at preset pressure threshold; maximum response time **< 5 minutes** at low rates

#### C. Air-in-Line Alarm Test
- **Test:** Introduce a known air bubble (typically > **0.1 to 0.5 mL**, depending on pump model)
- **Acceptance:** Audible + visual alarm must trigger

#### D. KVO (Keep Vein Open) Rate Test
- **Test:** Verify pump switches to KVO rate (typically 1–3 mL/h) when infusion completes
- **Acceptance:** ± **0.5 mL/h**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Mantra:** Memorize the formulas, units, and acceptance limits. Most 14-mark questions are numerical/design problems based on these.

| S.No | Parameter Tested | Formula / Expression | Standard Value / Limit | Unit |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|------------------------|------|
| 1 | Defibrillator Delivered Energy | $E_{del} = \dfrac{V^2}{R} \cdot t$ where V=peak voltage, R=50 Ω, t=pulse width | Set Energy ± 15% | Joules (J) |
| 2 | Energy stored in capacitor | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | – | Joules |
| 3 | Patient Monitor HR Error | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{HR_{displayed} - HR_{set}}{HR_{set}} \times 100$ | ± 1 BPM or ± 1% | % |
| 4 | NIBP Error | $\Delta P = P_{displayed} - P_{reference}$ | ± 3 mmHg | mmHg |
| 5 | SpO₂ Error | $\Delta SpO_2 = SpO_{2,displayed} - SpO_{2,set}$ | ± 2% | % |
| 6 | Tidal Volume Error | $\%\text{Vt Error} = \dfrac{Vt_{delivered} - Vt_{set}}{Vt_{set}} \times 100$ | ± 10% or ± 10 mL | % / mL |
| 7 | Infusion Flow Rate Error (Trumpet) | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{V_{actual} - V_{set}}{V_{set}} \times 100$ | ± 5% | % |
| 8 | Mean Arterial Pressure | $MAP = DBP + \dfrac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ | 70–100 mmHg (normal) | mmHg |
| 9 | Ohm's Law (Defib Load) | $I = \dfrac{V}{R}$ with $R = 50\ \Omega$ | – | Ampere |
| 10 | Leakage Current Limit (Type CF, NC) | $I_{leak} \leq 10\ \mu A$ | Class CF Cardiac | µA |
| 11 | Earth Resistance Limit | $R_{earth} \leq 0.1\ \Omega$ | IEC 60601-1 | Ω |
| 12 | Volume Delivered (Infusion) | $V = Q \times t$ where Q = flow rate | – | mL |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital BMET departments** use these protocols daily for preventive maintenance and pre-clinical safety checks.
- **Medical device manufacturers** use identical protocols in QC labs before dispatch.
- **Regulatory bodies** (CDSCO India, US-FDA, EU-MDR) mandate these tests under **ISO 13485** quality systems.
- **Calibration laboratories** (NABL-accredited) use these as primary test methods for issuing calibration certificates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Defibrillator Delivered Energy Calculation

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark):**
A biphasic defibrillator is set to deliver **200 J** into a standard 50 Ω test load. The internal capacitor has capacitance **C = 32 µF** and is charged to voltage **V₀**.
- (a) Calculate the charging voltage V₀ required.
- (b) Calculate the peak discharge current through the patient.
- (c) If the actual delivered energy is 218 J, calculate the % error and verify acceptance per IEC 60601-2-4.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 – Use the energy storage equation:**

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2
$$

We need the energy stored to be **at least equal to the set energy** (assuming 100% transfer efficiency, which is a worst-case design assumption — actual efficiency is ~85–90%).

**Step 2 – Substitute known values:**

$$
200 = \frac{1}{2} \times (32 \times 10^{-6}) \times V_0^2
$$

**Step 3 – Solve for V₀²:**

$$
V_0^2 = \frac{2 \times 200}{32 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{400}{32 \times 10^{-6}} = 12.5 \times 10^6
$$

**Step 4 – Take square root:**

$$
V_0 = \sqrt{12.5 \times 10^6} \approx 3535.53\ \text{V}
$$

> **Engineering note:** Real defibrillators charge to ~15–20% higher voltage to account for losses across the inductor (in LCR discharge circuits) and patient impedance mismatch.

**Step 5 – Peak current calculation (Ohm's Law at peak):**

$$
I_{peak} = \frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{3535.53}{50} \approx 70.71\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 6 – Compute the % error for the actual delivered energy (218 J):**

$$
\%\text{Error} = \frac{E_{delivered} - E_{set}}{E_{set}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{Error} = \frac{218 - 200}{200} \times 100 = \frac{18}{200} \times 100 = 9\%
$$

**Step 7 – Acceptance Verification:**

Per IEC 60601-2-4, acceptance limit = **± 15% or ± 3 J, whichever is greater**.

For 200 J: ±15% = ±30 J (which is greater than ±3 J). Observed error = 9% (within ±15%). → **ACCEPTED**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always state BOTH conditions and pick the LOOSER (more lenient) one for acceptance. Many students lose marks by only comparing to ±15% without considering the ±3 J clause.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Infusion Pump Flow Rate Accuracy

**Problem Statement:**
An infusion pump is set to deliver **100 mL/h**. After 2 hours, the actual delivered volume measured is **192 mL** (gravimetric method, fluid density 1.0 g/mL). 
- (a) Calculate the % flow rate error.
- (b) Verify acceptance per IEC 60601-2-24 (Trumpet curve limit ±5%).
- (c) Compute the average flow rate in mL/min.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 – Compute actual flow rate over the test period:**

$$
Q_{actual} = \frac{V_{actual}}{t} = \frac{192\ \text{mL}}{2\ \text{h}} = 96\ \text{mL/h}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute % error:**

$$
\%\text{Error} = \frac{Q_{actual} - Q_{set}}{Q_{set}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{Error} = \frac{96 - 100}{100} \times 100 = -4\%
$$

**Step 3 – Convert to mL/min:**

$$
Q_{avg} = \frac{96\ \text{mL/h}}{60\ \text{min/h}} = 1.6\ \text{mL/min}
$$

**Step 4 – Acceptance:** |−4%| = 4% < 5% → **ACCEPTED**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trumpet Curve Note (KTU Frequent Question):** The trumpet curve is a graph of % error vs. observation window (typically 5 s to 1200 s). Maximum allowable deviation at the longest window is ±5%, and it widens like a trumpet at short windows due to mechanical pulsations in the pump.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Ventilator Tidal Volume Accuracy

**Problem Statement:**
A ventilator is set to deliver Vt = **500 mL** at RR = 15/min, PEEP = 5 cmH₂O, FiO₂ = 40%. The test lung (compliance 50 mL/cmH₂O) records a delivered Vt of **538 mL**.
- (a) Calculate the % Vt error.
- (b) Calculate the minute ventilation (V̇E) for both set and delivered values.
- (c) State acceptance per ISO 80601-2-12.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 – % Vt error:**

$$
\%\text{Vt Error} = \frac{Vt_{del} - Vt_{set}}{Vt_{set}} \times 100 = \frac{538 - 500}{500} \times 100 = 7.6\%
$$

**Step 2 – Minute Ventilation (set):**

$$
\dot{V}_E = Vt \times RR = 500\ \text{mL} \times 15/\text{min} = 7500\ \text{mL/min} = 7.5\ \text{L/min}
$$

**Step 3 – Minute Ventilation (delivered):**

$$
\dot{V}_E = 538\ \text{mL} \times 15/\text{min} = 8070\ \text{mL/min} \approx 8.07\ \text{L/min}
$$

**Step 4 – Acceptance:** Per IEC/ISO 80601-2-12, Vt error ≤ **±10%** or **±10 mL** (whichever greater). Here 7.6% < 10% → **ACCEPTED**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Master Comparison Table

| Test Parameter | Patient Monitor | Defibrillator | Ventilator | Infusion Pump |
|----------------|-----------------|---------------|------------|----------------|
| **Primary Standard** | IEC 60601-2-27, -30, -34 | IEC 60601-2-4 | ISO 80601-2-12 | IEC 60601-2-24 |
| **Test Equipment** | Patient simulator | Defib analyzer + 50 Ω load | Test lung + flow analyzer | Graduated cylinder / balance |
| **Tolerance** | ±1 BPM, ±2%, ±3 mmHg | ±15% or ±3 J | ±10% Vt | ±5% flow |
| **Critical Safety Test** | Lead-off alarm | Internal discharge, no energy on paddle | Apnea backup | Free-flow protection |
| **Frequency of PM** | 6 monthly | 3 monthly (or after each use) | 6 monthly | 6 monthly |
| **Cost of Test Equipment** | High (₹ 3–8 Lakh) | Medium (₹ 2–5 Lakh) | High (₹ 4–10 Lakh) | Low (₹ 0.5–1 Lakh) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Universal Performance Testing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Visual & Mechanical Inspection] --> B[Step 2: Electrical Safety Test]
    B --> C[Step 3: Functional Verification]
    C --> D[Step 4: Performance Accuracy Test]
    D --> E[Step 5: Documentation & Labeling]
    E --> F{Pass All Tests?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Affix PM Sticker: Next Due Date]
    F -- No --> H[Quarantine for Repair/Calibration]
    H --> A
    G --> I[Equipment Released to Clinical Use]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Defibrillator Energy Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DEF[Defibrillator Unit] -- Paddles/Pads --> LOAD[50 Ohm Test Load]
    LOAD -- Sense Lines --> AN[Defib Analyzer Impulse 7000DP]
    AN -- Display --> ENG[Delivered Energy J]
    AN -- Display --> WAVE[Waveform Analysis]
    DEF -- Charge Set --> AN2[Compare Set vs Delivered]
    AN2 -- Decision --> RES{Pass or Fail per IEC 60601-2-4}
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Ventilator Test Bench Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Ventilator Unit] -- Breathing Circuit --> TL[Calibrated Test Lung]
    TL -- Pressure Line --> PA[Pressure Transducer]
    TL -- Flow Line --> FA[Flow Sensor]
    PA --> DAQ[Data Acquisition Unit]
    FA --> DAQ
    DAQ --> SW[VT900A or AccuLung Analyzer]
    SW -- Output --> DISP[Display: Vt, RR, PIP, PEEP, FiO2]
    SW -- Compare --> STD[Reference Values ISO 80601-2-12]
    STD -- Decision --> OK{Pass?}
    OK -- Yes --> CERT[Generate Test Certificate]
    OK -- No --> FLAG[Flag for Service]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Infusion Pump Trumpet Curve Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IP[Infusion Pump] -- IV Set --> COL[Collection Cylinder or Balance]
    COL -- Time-Stamp --> SW[Trumpet Curve Software]
    IP -- Settings --> SW
    SW -- Plot --> TC[Trumpet Curve: % Error vs Window]
    TC -- Compare --> LIM[± 5 percent Limit Envelope]
    LIM -- Decision --> PASS{Pass?}
    PASS -- Yes --> LOG[Log to CMMS Computerized Maintenance Management System]
    PASS -- No --> SERV[Send to Service]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Patient Monitor Multi-Parameter Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PM[Patient Monitor] --> ECG[ECG Module]
    PM --> SPO2[SpO2 Module]
    PM --> NIBP[NIBP Module]
    PM --> IBP[IBP Module]
    PM --> TEMP[Temperature Module]
    PM --> RESP[Respiration Module]
    SIM[Patient Simulator Fluke MPS450] --> ECG
    SIM --> SPO2
    SIM2[NIBP Simulator BP Pump2] --> NIBP
    SIM3[IBP Simulator] --> IBP
    ECG --> LOG[Central Test Log]
    SPO2 --> LOG
    NIBP --> LOG
    IBP --> LOG
    TEMP --> LOG
    RESP --> LOG
    LOG --> REP[Final Test Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each device has a dedicated test loop. The patient monitor uses a **multi-channel simulator** because it has 6+ independent modules. The defibrillator, ventilator, and infusion pump are tested as **single-function devices** with their dedicated analyzers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Performance Testing" of medical equipment. List any four parameters tested for a Patient Monitor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Performance testing is the systematic verification of whether a medical device meets its manufacturer-specified functional and accuracy specifications under controlled test conditions, as defined by IEC 60601-1 and its collateral/particular standards.

**Four Parameters of Patient Monitor (1.5 Marks — 0.4 each):**
1. **ECG:** Heart rate, amplitude, lead-off detection
2. **SpO₂:** Saturation %, pulse rate, perfusion index
3. **NIBP:** Systolic, diastolic, mean arterial pressure
4. **IBP:** Static pressure accuracy, dynamic response, zero balance

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`
**State the acceptance criteria for defibrillator delivered energy as per IEC 60601-2-4. Mention the standard test load resistance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Standard test load:** **R = 50 Ω** non-inductive (1 Mark)
- **Acceptance criterion:** Delivered energy = Set energy **± 15% or ± 3 J**, whichever is greater (2 Marks)
- **Note:** For 360 J: ±15% = ±54 J (looser). For 5 J: ±3 J (looser) = ±60%. Hence the "whichever is greater" clause.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Choice Option A (14 Marks)

**A clinical engineering team is conducting the annual performance verification of a biphasic defibrillator. Answer the following:**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **test setup** for defibrillator energy output testing. List the **test energies** that must be verified. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A defibrillator is set to deliver **270 J**. The internal capacitor C = **42 µF** is charged to a voltage such that the stored energy is 297 J. The pulse is delivered into a **50 Ω** load. Calculate: (i) the charging voltage V₀, (ii) the peak discharge current, and (iii) the % error. Verify acceptance per **IEC 60601-2-4**. (7 Marks)

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Test Setup (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**
```
Defibrillator → Paddles/Pads → 50 Ω Test Load → Defib Analyzer (Impulse 7000DP / Metron QA-45) → Display (Energy, Waveform, Current)
```

**Description (2 Marks):**
- Connect defibrillator paddles (or hands-free pads) to the 50 Ω non-inductive test load via the analyzer.
- Set the analyzer to "Energy Measurement" mode.
- Charge defibrillator to each test energy, discharge, and record the energy delivered.
- Analyzer also displays pulse waveform, peak voltage, and peak current for biphasic analysis.

**Test Energies to Verify (2 Marks):**
**2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100, 200, 270, 360 J** (at least 6 energies are mandated by AAMI DF-2 / IEC 60601-2-4). Also test synchronization mode at one energy level and pacer output (if equipped) at 30, 60, 90, 120, 180 ppm.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** E_set = 270 J, C = 42 µF, R = 50 Ω, E_stored = 297 J

**(i) Charging voltage V₀:**

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2
$$

$$
V_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times E_{stored}}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 297}{42 \times 10^{-6}}}
$$

$$
V_0 = \sqrt{14{,}142{,}857.14} \approx 3760.7\ \text{V}
$$

**Result:** V₀ ≈ **3760.7 V** (2 Marks)

**(ii) Peak discharge current:**

$$
I_{peak} = \frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{3760.7}{50} \approx 75.21\ \text{A}
$$

**Result:** I_peak ≈ **75.21 A** (1.5 Marks)

**(iii) % Error Calculation:**

$$
\%\text{Error} = \frac{E_{delivered} - E_{set}}{E_{set}} \times 100 = \frac{297 - 270}{270} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{Error} = \frac{27}{270} \times 100 = 10\%
$$

**Acceptance Check:** ±15% of 270 J = ±40.5 J. Observed deviation = 27 J → within limits. → **ACCEPTED**. (Note: The ±3 J clause gives ±3 J = ±1.1%, so the ±15% is the looser/limiting criterion.) (3.5 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. Students often **omit the "whichever is greater"** clause between ±15% and ±3 J → loses 0.5 mark
> 2. **Forgetting the unit (Ω)** on test load resistance → loses 0.5 mark
> 3. **Confusing stored vs delivered energy** in the formula — E_stored ≠ E_delivered. The question gives both, students must use E_stored in the C-V formula and E_delivered for % error.
> 4. **Not writing the formula before substitution** — KTU values the formula step explicitly.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]` — Choice Option B (14 Marks)

**A biomedical engineer is performing the preventive maintenance (PM) of a microprocessor-controlled infusion pump used in the ICU. Answer the following:**

**(a)** List and explain the **performance parameters** that must be tested for an infusion pump, along with their acceptance limits. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An infusion pump is set to **125 mL/h**. The gravimetric test (water density 1.0 g/mL) records a delivered mass of **242.5 g** over **2 hours**. Calculate: (i) the actual flow rate in mL/h, (ii) the % error, and (iii) the trumpet curve acceptability per **IEC 60601-2-24**. If the same pump is set to **500 mL/h** and delivers 1020 mL in 2 hours, calculate the % error for the high-rate test. (7 Marks)

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Performance Parameters (7 Marks)

1. **Flow Rate Accuracy (2 Marks):** Tested gravimetrically using calibrated balance or volumetric cylinder. Set rates tested: **1, 5, 25, 100, 200, 500, 1000 mL/h**. Acceptance: **± 5%** per IEC 60601-2-24.

2. **Occlusion Alarm Pressure & Time (1.5 Marks):** Test the time-to-alarm and the pressure at which the alarm triggers. Typically 3 levels: low, medium, high. Acceptance: alarm must trigger within 5 min at low rate and within 30 s at high rate.

3. **Air-in-Line Detection (1 Mark):** Inject a calibrated air bubble; the alarm must trigger for bubbles > 0.1 mL (varies by model).

4. **KVO (Keep Vein Open) Rate (1 Mark):** After completion, pump must switch to a low KVO rate (1–3 mL/h) to maintain IV patency.

5. **Free-Flow Protection (1 Mark):** When the pump door is opened, the IV set must mechanically clamp to prevent gravity free-flow.

6. **Battery Backup Test (0.5 Mark):** Pump must run for ≥ 4 hours on full battery at 125 mL/h.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given for Test 1:** Set rate = 125 mL/h, mass delivered = 242.5 g, t = 2 h

**(i) Actual flow rate:**

$$
Q_{actual} = \frac{242.5\ \text{mL}}{2\ \text{h}} = 121.25\ \text{mL/h}
$$

(1.5 Marks)

**(ii) % Error:**

$$
\%\text{Error} = \frac{121.25 - 125}{125} \times 100 = \frac{-3.75}{125} \times 100 = -3\%
$$

(1.5 Marks)

**(iii) Acceptance:** |−3%| < 5% → **PASSED** (1 Mark)

**Test 2 — High Rate:**

**Given:** Set = 500 mL/h, V_delivered = 1020 mL, t = 2 h

$$
Q_{actual} = \frac{1020}{2} = 510\ \text{mL/h}
$$

(1 Mark)

$$
\%\text{Error} = \frac{510 - 500}{500} \times 100 = \frac{10}{500} \times 100 = +2\%
$$

(1.5 Marks)

**Acceptance:** |+2%| < 5% → **PASSED** (0.5 Mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Water density 1.0 g/mL must be stated before converting mass → volume. Many students write 242.5 g = 242.5 mL without justification. → loses 0.5 mark
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the % error when actual < set. KTU wants the signed value. → loses 0.5 mark
> 3. **Trumpet curve observation window** not mentioned. The ±5% applies to the **longest observation window** (typically 1200 s = 20 min). At shorter windows, allowed error widens. → loses 0.5 mark
> 4. **Mass vs volume confusion** in gravimetric test — must explicitly show the conversion step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Performance Testing of Medical Equipment**

### A. Standards (Always Quote in Exam)
- **IEC 60601-1** → General safety for all electromedical equipment
- **IEC 60601-1-1 / -1-2** → Collateral: General requirements / EMC
- **IEC 60601-2-4** → Defibrillator-specific
- **IEC 60601-2-27, -2-30, -2-34** → ECG / SpO₂ / NIBP modules
- **ISO 80601-2-12** → Ventilator-specific (replaced IEC 60601-2-12)
- **IEC 60601-2-24** → Infusion pump-specific

### B. Universal Test Equipment
- **Defibrillator Analyzer** + **50 Ω non-inductive load**
- **Patient Simulator** (ECG + SpO₂ + IBP)
- **NIBP Simulator** (cuff + manometer)
- **Test Lung** (compliance + resistance simulator)
- **Oxygen Analyzer**
- **Graduated Cylinder / Calibrated Balance** (for infusion)

### C. Acceptance Limits (Bullet Memorization)
- Defib: **±15% or ±3 J, whichever greater**
- SpO₂: **±2%**
- NIBP: **±3 mmHg**
- HR: **±1 BPM or ±1%**
- Ventilator Vt: **±10% or ±10 mL**
- Infusion: **±5%** (trumpet curve)

### D. Electrical Safety Limits (Vital for 14-Mark Questions)
- **Earth resistance < 0.1 Ω**
- **Earth leakage < 500 µA (NC, Class I)**
- **Patient leakage < 10 µA (NC, Type CF)**
- **Dielectric strength: 1500 V AC for 1 min**

### E. Frequency of Preventive Maintenance
- **Defibrillator:** 3-monthly or after each use
- **Patient Monitor:** 6-monthly
- **Ventilator:** 6-monthly (or 1000 hours of use)
- **Infusion Pump:** 6-monthly (or per manufacturer)

### F. KTU Mnemonics for Quick Recall
- **"DC-PVI"** for the four devices: **D**efib, **C**ardiac monitor (Patient monitor), **V**entilator, **I**nfusion pump
- **"FAT"** for performance test cycle: **F**unctional → **A**ccuracy → **T**olerance verification
- **"VIPS"** for documentation: **V**isual inspection, **I**nstrument used, **P**arameters tested, **S**ign-off

### G. Common Viva Questions
1. Why is the defibrillator test load 50 Ω and not the actual patient impedance (~30 Ω)?
2. What is the Trumpet curve and why does it widen at short observation windows?
3. Why does the ventilator use a test lung, not a real patient?
4. Differentiate between Patient Leakage Current and Earth Leakage Current.
5. What is the difference between "Performance Test" and "Acceptance Test"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** Always present answers in the **Definition → Standard → Test Equipment → Procedure → Acceptance Criteria → Numerical Verification** flow. This is the KTU-recommended answer structure and ensures full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acquisition and Analysis of PCG Signal: Segmentation of Systolic and Diastolic Phases, Detection of Abnormalities (e.g., Murmur, Stenosis)
topics.content:
# Acquisition and Analysis of PCG Signal ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Phonocardiogram (PCG)** is the graphical/electrical representation of the acoustic vibrations produced by the mechanical activity of the heart, captured at the chest wall using a sensitive transducer (electronic stethoscope or piezoelectric microphone). The PCG signal provides a **non-invasive diagnostic modality** that complements the Electrocardiogram (ECG) by directly revealing hemodynamic events (valvular closures, blood turbulence, regurgitant jets) rather than purely electrical activity. A standard PCG cycle consists of four principal acoustic events: | Heart Sound | Physiological Origin | Timing | Dominant Frequency | |-------------|--------------------|--------|---------------------| | **S1 (First Heart Sound)** | Closure of Mitral (M1) and Tricuspid (T1) valves | Onset of Systole | 20 – 150 Hz | | **S2 (Second Heart Sound)** | Closure of Aortic (A2) and Pulmonic (P2) valves | Onset of Diastole | 50 – 200 Hz | | **Systolic Phase** | Interval between S1 and S2 (ventricular ejection) | After S1, Before S2 | 20 – 200 Hz (clean) | | **Diastolic Phase** | Interval between S2 and next S1 (ventricular filling) | After S2, Before next S1 | 20 – 100 Hz (clean) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Segmentation of the PCG into Systolic and Diastolic phases is the cornerstone pre-processing step before any pathological classification (murmur, stenosis, regurgitation) can be performed. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine standing near a busy mechanical pump with your ear pressed against its casing. You hear: - A clear **"LUBB"** when two flaps snap shut to start the piston (Systole begins) → this is **S1**. - A quieter **"DUBB"** when the exit valves slam to stop backflow (Diastole begins) → this is **S2**. - The rhythmic pumping cycles between LUBB–DUBB–LUBB–DUBB… If the pump has a **leaky valve**, you will hear an extra **"whoosh"** between the two main beats — that is a **murmur**. If the pipe is too narrow (stenosis), you will hear a **high-pitched turbulent whistle**. The PCG simply *electrifies* this listening process so a computer can measure, segment, and diagnose the sounds quantitatively. > [!IMPORTANT] > The PCG is essentially a **vibration signal with frequencies up to ~1000 Hz**, dominated by transient peaks at S1 and S2. Any energy lasting **more than ~100 ms** between these peaks is suspected pathology (murmur). --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Speed of sound in tissue ≈ 1540 m/s** - **Speed of sound in air ≈ 343 m/s** (relevant for electronic stethoscope coupling) - **Nyquist Sampling Rate for PCG ≥ 2000 Hz** (practically **4000 – 8000 Hz** to preserve murmurs up to 2 kHz) - **Resolution of A/D Converter ≥ 12 bits** (16-bit preferred for clinical use) - **Heart Rate Range: 30 – 200 bpm** (cardiac cycle: 0.3 s – 2.0 s) - **Pathological murmurs extend up to 600 – 2000 Hz** (vs. clean S1/S2 below 200 Hz) --- # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 PCG Acquisition Chain — Operational Breakdown The PCG signal is acquired through a structured instrument chain.
