Each stage must be understood because artefacts introduced here corrupt all downstream segmentation. ### Stage 1: Sensing Transducer - **Piezoelectric microphone** or **capacitive electret microphone** converts chest-wall vibrations into voltage. - Typical sensitivity: **−46 dBV/Pa to −36 dBV/Pa**. - Frequency response: must be flat from **20 Hz to 2000 Hz**. ### Stage 2: Pre-amplification - Low-noise instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128, AD620). - Gain of **100× to 1000×** (40 – 60 dB) to lift the small acoustic signal (typically **1–10 mV**) to ADC range. ### Stage 3: Analogue Filtering - **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** at **20 Hz** to remove DC offset and baseline wander. - **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** at **2000 Hz** (anti-aliasing). ### Stage 4: Analogue-to-Digital Conversion - Sampling rate $f_s \geq 4000$ Hz. - Resolution ≥ **12 bits**. ### Stage 5: Digital Signal Processing (Segmentation & Analysis) - Implemented on PC using MATLAB / Python / LabVIEW. --- ## 2.2 Segmentation of Systolic and Diastolic Phases Segmentation is the process of locating the **S1 and S2 peaks** within each cardiac cycle and demarcating the Systolic (S1→S2) and Diastolic (S2→next S1) intervals. ### Method 1: Shannon Energy Envelope (Most Popular for KTU) The **Shannon Energy** transform amplifies the medium-amplitude peaks (S1, S2) and suppresses low-amplitude noise: $$E_{shannon}(t) = -x^2(t) \cdot \log\bigl(x^2(t)\bigr)$$ The envelope is then extracted via: $$E_{norm}(t) = \frac{E_{shannon}(t) - \min(E_{shannon})}{\max(E_{shannon}) - \min(E_{shannon})}$$ Peak detection on the smoothed envelope yields the locations of **S1 and S2**. ### Method 2: Hilbert Transform Envelope The Hilbert transform $H[x(t)]$ constructs the **analytic signal**: $$z(t) = x(t) + j \cdot H[x(t)]$$ The instantaneous envelope is: $$A(t) = \sqrt{x^2(t) + H[x(t)]^2}$$ ### Method 3: Wavelet Decomposition Daubechies wavelet (db4, db6) decomposes the PCG into multiple frequency sub-bands. S1 and S2 appear as **maxima in the detail coefficients at scale 3–4** (corresponding to 125–500 Hz). ### Method 4: Autocorrelation-based Period Detection For repetitive heart-sound signals, autocorrelation finds the dominant period $T$: $$R_{xx}(\tau) = \sum_{t=0}^{N-1} x(t)\,x(t+\tau)$$ The first strong peak after $\tau = 0$ gives the cardiac period. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units / Range | |--------|--------------------|--------------| | $f_s$ | Sampling rate (≥ 2 × highest frequency) | Hz, typically 4000 | | $E_{shannon}$ | $-x^2(t)\log(x^2(t))$ | Normalized 0 – 1 | | $A(t)$ | $\sqrt{x^2(t) + H[x(t)]^2}$ | Pa or V | | $R_{xx}(\tau)$ | $\sum x(t)\,x(t+\tau)$ | — | | $P_x(f)$ | $\frac{1}{N}\vert X(f)\vert^2$ (PSD) | V²/Hz | | Heart Rate (bpm) | $\frac{60}{T_{cycle}}$ | 30 – 200 bpm | | Murmur freq band | Dominant in PSD between S1–S2 | 100 – 600 Hz | | Systolic duration | $\approx 0.30\,T_{cycle}$ | s | | Diastolic duration | $\approx 0.70\,T_{cycle}$ (rest) | s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vertical bar safety:** All absolute-value bars inside math tables have been written as `\vert` to keep Markdown table syntax intact. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Telemedicine / Remote Cardiac Auscultation:** Portable digital stethoscopes (e.g., 3M Littmann CORE, Eko CORE) stream PCG to cloud servers for AI-based diagnosis. - **Wearable Cardiac Monitors:** Smart watches with acoustic sensors detect atrial fibrillation via PCG. - **Phonocardiography in Neonatology:** PCG detects congenital heart defects (VSD, ASD) in newborns where ECG is less informative. - **Valve-replacement follow-up:** PCG detects prosthetic valve malfunction (regurgitation, stenosis of bioprosthetic valve). --- # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Shannon Energy Transform **Goal:** Convert a raw PCG signal $x(t)$ into an envelope $E(t)$ that highlights S1 and S2 peaks while suppressing background noise. ### Step 1 — Define the signal Let the sampled PCG be $x[n]$, $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$, sampled at $f_s \geq 4000$ Hz. ### Step 2 — Normalize the signal To prevent $\log(\cdot)$ from diverging, normalize so that $\vert x[n]\vert \leq 1$: $$x_{norm}[n] = \frac{x[n]}{\max_{n}\vert x[n]\vert}$$ This guarantees $x_{norm}^2[n] \in [0,1]$ and $-\log(x^2) \geq 0$. ### Step 3 — Apply Shannon Energy at every sample For each discrete sample: $$E[n] = -x_{norm}^2[n] \cdot \log\bigl(x_{norm}^2[n]\bigr)$$ **Behaviour analysis:** - When $\vert x_{norm}\vert \to 0$ (silence): $E \approx 0$. - When $\vert x_{norm}\vert$ is medium (≈ 0.5–0.8, typical of S1/S2): $E$ attains a **maximum**. - When $\vert x_{norm}\vert \to 1$ (saturation/clipping): $E \to 0$ again (self-limiting). This non-monotonic response is precisely why Shannon energy outperforms the simple squared envelope $|x|^2$ in PCG analysis. ### Step 4 — Smooth the envelope Apply a moving-average filter of length $L = 0.04 \cdot f_s$ (≈ 40 ms window) to remove spikes: $$E_{smooth}[n] = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{k=n-L+1}^{n} E[k]$$ ### Step 5 — Peak detection Find local maxima in $E_{smooth}$ that exceed an adaptive threshold $\tau$: $$\tau = 0.3 \cdot \max(E_{smooth})$$ Each detected peak corresponds to either **S1** (taller) or **S2** (shorter). Sorting them by temporal order and using the physiological fact that **S1–S2 interval < S2–next-S1 interval** allows classification into Systolic vs. Diastolic segments. ### Step 6 — Compute Systolic and Diastolic Durations For the $i$-th cardiac cycle: $$T_{systole}^{(i)} = t_{S2}^{(i)} - t_{S1}^{(i)}$$ $$T_{diastole}^{(i)} = t_{S1}^{(i+1)} - t_{S2}^{(i)}$$ $$T_{cycle}^{(i)} = T_{systole}^{(i)} + T_{diastole}^{(i)}$$ **Heart Rate:** $$HR^{(i)} = \frac{60}{T_{cycle}^{(i)}} \quad \text{[bpm]}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Power Spectral Density for Murmur Detection A **murmur** is a sustained vibration caused by turbulent blood flow. It has **broader bandwidth** and **longer duration** than clean S1/S2. The PSD is obtained via the Welch method: $$\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{K \cdot L} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \vert X_k(f)\vert^2$$ where $K$ is the number of overlapping segments, $L$ is the segment length, and $X_k(f)$ is the FFT of the $k$-th windowed segment. A **clean** PCG has PSD concentrated below 200 Hz. A **murmur** PCG has PSD spread up to **400 – 600 Hz** (innocent murmurs) or **600 – 1000 Hz** (pathological murmurs, e.g., aortic stenosis). ### Spectral Centroid (for murmur quantification) $$f_{centroid} = \frac{\sum_{f} f \cdot P_{xx}(f)}{\sum_{f} P_{xx}(f)}$$ A shift in $f_{centroid}$ above **200 Hz** in the systolic interval is a strong indicator of a systolic murmur (e.g., aortic stenosis). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Normal vs. Pathological PCG | Feature | Normal PCG | Systolic Murmur (Stenosis) | Diastolic Murmur (Regurgitation) | |---------|-----------|----------------------------|----------------------------------| | S1 amplitude | High | Often masked by murmur | Preserved | | S2 amplitude | High | May split (A2–P2 gap) | Preserved | | Systolic interval | Quiet (clean) | **Whoosh present** | Quiet | | Diastolic interval | Quiet | Quiet | **Whoosh present** | | Frequency range | 20 – 200 Hz | 100 – 800 Hz | 50 – 500 Hz | | Duration of murmur | — | > 100 ms (longer = more severe) | > 100 ms | | Spectral centroid | < 150 Hz | > 200 Hz (stenosis > 400 Hz) | 150 – 400 Hz | | PCG timing relation | Strict S1–S2 sequence | Vibration S1 → S2 | Vibration S2 → S1 | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Heart Rate from PCG **Given:** S1 peaks detected at $t = 0.85$ s, $1.70$ s, $2.55$ s, $3.40$ s. S2 peaks at $t = 1.10$ s, $1.95$ s, $2.80$ s, $3.65$ s. **Solution:** Step 1 — Compute $T_{cycle}$ between consecutive S1 peaks: $$T_{cycle} = 1.70 - 0.85 = 0.85 \text{ s}$$ Step 2 — Compute Heart Rate: $$HR = \frac{60}{0.85} = 70.59 \approx 71 \text{ bpm}$$ Step 3 — Compute Systolic duration (S1→S2): $$T_{systole} = 1.10 - 0.85 = 0.25 \text{ s}$$ Step 4 — Compute Diastolic duration (S2→next S1): $$T_{diastole} = 1.70 - 1.10 = 0.60 \text{ s}$$ Step 5 — Verify: $$T_{systole} + T_{diastole} = 0.25 + 0.60 = 0.85 \text{ s} = T_{cycle} \checkmark$$ --- # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PCG Acquisition System — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Heart Sounds
Mechanical Vibrations] --> B[Piezoelectric
Microphone
20 Hz to 2 kHz] B --> C[Instrumentation
Amplifier
Gain 1000x] C --> D[Analogue HPF
Cutoff 20 Hz] D --> E[Analogue LPF
Cutoff 2 kHz] E --> F[ADC
fs 4000 Hz
12 to 16 bit] F --> G[Digital PC
DSP Module] G --> H[Shannon Energy
Envelope] H --> I[Peak Detection
S1 and S2] I --> J[Segmentation
Systole and Diastole] J --> K[PSD Analysis
Welch Method] K --> L{Decision
Block} L -- Spectral Centroid below 200 Hz --> M[Normal PCG] L -- Spectral Centroid above 200 Hz in Systole --> N[Systolic Murmur
Possible Stenosis] L -- Spectral Centroid above 200 Hz in Diastole --> O[Diastolic Murmur
Possible Regurgitation] ``` --- ## 4.2 Shannon Energy Segmentation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Raw PCG Signal x of t] --> Q[Normalize Signal
x norm range 0 to 1] Q --> R[Apply Shannon Energy
E equals minus x squared times log of x squared] R --> S[Moving Average Smooth
Window 40 ms] S --> T[Adaptive Threshold
0.3 times max E] T --> U{Local Maximum
Above Threshold} U -- Yes --> V[Mark Peak Candidate] U -- No --> W[Skip Sample] V --> X[Sort Peaks by Amplitude
Alternate S1 S2 S1 S2] X --> Y[Classify Intervals
Systole and Diastole] Y --> Z[Compute Heart Rate
HR equals 60 over T cycle] ``` --- ## 4.3 Normal vs. Pathological PCG Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Normal[Normal PCG Cycle] N1[S1 Lubb
30 to 100 ms] --> N2[Systole
Quiet 20 to 150 Hz] N2 --> N3[S2 Dubb
20 to 80 ms] N3 --> N4[Diastole
Quiet 20 to 100 Hz] N4 --> N1 end subgraph Stenosis[Aortic Stenosis PCG] S1[S1] --> S2[Crescendo Decrescendo
Systolic Murmur
100 to 800 Hz] S2 --> S3[S2] S3 --> S4[Diastole Quiet] S4 --> S1 end subgraph Regurg[Mitral Regurgitation] R1[S1 Soft] --> R2[Systole
Holosystolic Murmur
100 to 600 Hz] R2 --> R3[S2] R3 --> R4[Diastolic Murmur
50 to 400 Hz] R4 --> R1 end ``` --- ## 4.4 Frequency-Band Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Segment PCG into Systole and Diastole] --> B[Compute PSD using Welch] B --> C{Frequency Analysis} C -- Spectral peak below 200 Hz --> D[Normal Heart Sound] C -- Spectral energy 200 to 600 Hz in Systole --> E[Innocent Systolic Murmur] C -- Spectral energy above 600 Hz in Systole --> F[Aortic Stenosis Suspected] C -- Spectral energy 200 to 500 Hz in Diastole --> G[Diastolic Murmur Mitral Stenosis] C -- Broad band 100 to 1000 Hz holosystolic --> H[Mitral or Tricuspid Regurgitation] C -- Continuous murmur both phases --> I[Patent Ductus Arteriosus] ``` --- # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Phonocardiogram. State the standard sampling rate and bandwidth required for PCG acquisition.** **Model Answer:** The Phonocardiogram (PCG) is the time-domain graphical recording of heart sounds produced by the mechanical action of heart valves and blood flow. The standard sampling rate is $f_s \geq 4000$ Hz and the bandwidth is **20 Hz to 2000 Hz**. A 12-bit (minimum) ADC is used. [3 marks] --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] List the four main components of a normal PCG signal with their dominant frequency ranges.** **Model Answer:** - **S1** (closure of mitral and tricuspid valves): **20 – 150 Hz** - **Systolic phase** (interval S1–S2): **20 – 200 Hz** - **S2** (closure of aortic and pulmonic valves): **50 – 200 Hz** - **Diastolic phase** (interval S2–next S1): **20 – 100 Hz** [3 marks] --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions ### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the block diagram of a PCG acquisition system. State the role of each block and justify the choice of sampling rate. **(b) [7 marks]** With the help of the Shannon Energy envelope technique, describe the step-by-step algorithm to segment the PCG into Systolic and Diastolic intervals. State the formula and justify why Shannon Energy is preferred over simple squared envelope. #### Model Solution to (a) A PCG acquisition system has the following stages: 1. **Transducer (Piezoelectric microphone):** Converts chest-wall vibrations to voltage; sensitivity ≈ −46 dBV/Pa, flat response 20 Hz – 2 kHz. 2. **Pre-amplifier:** Low-noise INA128 instrumentation amplifier with gain 1000× (60 dB) to lift 1–10 mV signals. 3. **Analogue HPF (20 Hz):** Removes DC offset and baseline wander. 4. **Analogue LPF (2 kHz):** Anti-aliasing filter to satisfy Nyquist criterion. 5. **ADC:** Sampling at $f_s \geq 4000$ Hz, 12-bit resolution. **Justification of sampling rate:** Highest frequency in PCG ≈ 2000 Hz (murmurs may extend higher). By Nyquist: $$f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 2000 = 4000 \text{ Hz}$$ Practically $f_s = 4000$ to $8000$ Hz is chosen. [7 marks] #### Model Solution to (b) **Algorithm Steps:** 1. Normalize the PCG signal $x[n]$ to the range $[-1, 1]$. 2. Compute Shannon Energy at every sample: $$E[n] = -x_{norm}^2[n] \cdot \log\bigl(x_{norm}^2[n]\bigr)$$ 3. Apply moving-average smoothing with window length $L = 0.04 \times f_s$ (40 ms). 4. Compute adaptive threshold: $$\tau = 0.3 \times \max(E_{smooth})$$ 5. Find local maxima above $\tau$ — these are S1/S2 candidates. 6. Classify peaks alternately as S1 (taller) and S2 (shorter). 7. Compute $T_{systole} = t_{S2} - t_{S1}$ and $T_{diastole} = t_{S1_{next}} - t_{S2}$. **Why Shannon Energy over $|x|^2$:** The squared envelope amplifies **loud spikes** (e.g., knocks, clicks) more than S1/S2. Shannon Energy is **non-monotonic** — it suppresses both very small and very large amplitudes, maximizing at medium amplitudes (typical of S1 and S2 peaks). This gives a more uniform peak amplitude and robust detection. [7 marks] --- ### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a) [7 marks]** Describe how the Power Spectral Density (PSD) of a PCG signal is used to distinguish a normal heart sound from a systolic murmur (aortic stenosis). Include the Welch formula. **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the pathophysiology of mitral stenosis and mitral regurgitation with reference to PCG signatures. Tabulate differences in timing, frequency, and duration of the murmurs. #### Model Solution to (a) The PSD is estimated using the **Welch method**: $$\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{K \cdot L} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \vert X_k(f)\vert^2$$ where the signal is divided into $K$ overlapping segments of length $L$ each, windowed (Hann/Hamming), and FFT-computed. **Normal PCG:** PSD concentrated below **200 Hz**, with sharp peaks corresponding to S1 and S2. **Aortic stenosis PCG:** PSD has **additional energy between 200 Hz and 800 Hz** in the systolic interval (S1–S2 window), corresponding to the high-velocity turbulent jet through the narrowed aortic valve. The spectral centroid shifts above **400 Hz** in systole. **Quantitative indicator — Spectral Centroid:** $$f_{centroid} = \frac{\sum_f f \cdot P_{xx}(f)}{\sum_f P_{xx}(f)}$$ If $f_{centroid} > 200$ Hz **in the systolic segment only**, systolic murmur is confirmed. [7 marks] #### Model Solution to (b) **Mitral Stenosis:** - Pathophysiology: Narrowed mitral valve impedes blood flow from left atrium to left ventricle during **diastole**. - PCG signature: **Diastolic murmur** (rumbling, low-pitched, 50–500 Hz) immediately after S2, often with an opening snap. - Duration: Throughout diastole in severe cases. **Mitral Regurgitation:** - Pathophysiology: Incomplete closure of mitral valve allows backflow from left ventricle to left atrium during **systole**. - PCG signature: **Holosystolic (pansystolic) murmur**, high-pitched, 100–600 Hz, masking S1 and ending at S2. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Mitral Stenosis | Mitral Regurgitation | |---------|----------------|----------------------| | Timing | Diastolic (after S2) | Systolic (S1 to S2) | | Quality | Rumbling, low-pitched | Blowing, high-pitched | | Frequency | 50 – 500 Hz | 100 – 600 Hz | | Duration | Throughout diastole | Throughout systole | | Associated sound | Opening snap after S2 | Soft S1, widely split S2 | | Spectral centroid | 100 – 300 Hz | 200 – 500 Hz | [7 marks] --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Students frequently **confuse S1 and S2 timing** — remember **"S1 = LUBB" (start of systole, mitral/tricuspid closure)** and **"S2 = DUBB" (start of diastole, aortic/pulmonic closure)**. > - Common mark-losing error: forgetting to normalize before computing Shannon Energy, causing $\log(0)$ or numerical overflow. > - Many students write **"sampling rate = 100 Hz"** — this is the ECG rate, NOT PCG. PCG needs $f_s \geq 4000$ Hz. > - When asked about murmurs, always specify **timing (systolic/diastolic) AND frequency AND duration** for full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - **PCG = Phonocardiogram**; recording of heart sound vibrations using piezoelectric microphone. - **Sampling rate ≥ 4000 Hz**; ADC ≥ 12-bit; bandwidth 20 Hz – 2 kHz. - **S1 (LUBB):** mitral + tricuspid closure, **20 – 150 Hz**, marks start of systole. - **S2 (DUBB):** aortic + pulmonic closure, **50 – 200 Hz**, marks start of diastole. - **Systole** = S1 to S2 interval; **Diastole** = S2 to next S1 interval. - **Shannon Energy:** $E = -x^2 \log(x^2)$ — non-monotonic, robust peak detector. - **Hilbert envelope:** $A(t) = \sqrt{x^2(t) + H[x(t)]^2}$ — analytic-signal amplitude. - **Welch PSD:** averaged periodogram over $K$ overlapping segments. - **Normal PCG PSD < 200 Hz**; **Murmur PSD extends to 400 – 1000 Hz**. - **Spectral centroid > 200 Hz in systole ⇒ Systolic murmur (e.g., aortic stenosis)**. - **Spectral centroid > 200 Hz in diastole ⇒ Diastolic murmur (e.g., mitral stenosis)**. - **Aortic stenosis:** crescendo–decrescendo systolic murmur, 100 – 800 Hz, ejection type. - **Mitral regurgitation:** holosystolic murmur masking S1, 100 – 600 Hz. - **Mitral stenosis:** opening snap + diastolic rumble, 50 – 500 Hz. - **Patent Ductus Arteriosus:** continuous machinery murmur, both phases, 100 – 1000 Hz. - **Heart Rate (bpm):** $\frac{60}{T_{cycle}}$ where $T_{cycle} = T_{systole} + T_{diastole}$. - **Pre-processing:** HPF 20 Hz, LPF 2 kHz, notch 50 Hz (powerline), DC removal. - **Wavelet decomposition** (db4/db6) at scale 3–4 highlights S1/S2. - **Autocorrelation** $R_{xx}(\tau)$ finds fundamental cardiac period for HR. - **Murmur detection triad:** (1) Timing relative to S1/S2, (2) Frequency content, (3) Duration > 100 ms. - **Clinical applications:** Telemedicine stethoscopes, neonatal CHD screening, prosthetic valve monitoring, wearable cardiac patches. ---

topics.title: Acquisition and Analysis of IR Images: Segmentation and Detection of Anomalies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL607 - BIO INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Acquisition and Analysis of IR Images: Segmentation and Detection of Anomalies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Infrared (IR) Thermography** in the biomedical context is defined as a *non-contact, non-ionizing, passive imaging modality* that captures the spatial distribution of infrared radiation (wavelength band **8–14 μm**, Long-Wave Infrared, LWIR) emitted from the surface of the human body. The intensity of the emitted radiation is converted by a focal-plane-array (FPA) detector into a two-dimensional *thermogram* — a pseudo-color or grayscale map in which pixel intensity is directly proportional to the surface temperature of the underlying tissue.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus):** "Acquisition of IR images refers to the capture of thermal emission patterns from the body surface using a calibrated infrared camera, while segmentation and detection of anomalies involve isolation of regions of abnormal thermal signature (hyperthermia or hypothermia) relative to a healthy baseline using image-processing algorithms."

**Segmentation** is the partitioning of a thermogram into disjoint, semantically meaningful regions corresponding to anatomical structures or pathological hotspots. **Anomaly Detection** is the subsequent classification of these segmented regions as *normal physiological* or *abnormal pathological* based on statistical, morphological, or machine-learning criteria.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Snake's Tongue:** Snakes detect prey in complete darkness using heat-sensing pit organs. They do not "see" the prey — they "feel" its temperature as infrared energy. An IR camera in a hospital does precisely the same job: it converts the invisible heat fingerprint of a tissue (e.g., an inflamed joint, a malignant tumor with elevated metabolic rate) into a visible image the doctor can interpret. Just as a snake brain highlights a warm mouse against a cool forest floor, image-processing algorithms in MATLAB/Python automatically highlight the abnormal "hot" zone against the healthy "cool" background.

**Plain-English interpretation for a first-time student:**
1. The human body constantly emits heat. You cannot see this heat with your eyes, but a special camera (thermal/IR camera) can.
2. The camera assigns a *color* to each *temperature*. Hot regions appear red/white; cold regions appear blue/black.
3. We use **segmentation** to draw a digital boundary around the hot (or cold) abnormal region.
4. We then use **anomaly detection** logic to decide if that segmented region is medically concerning (e.g., inflammation, tumor, vascular blockage).

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following values are *must memorize* for KTU board exams:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Numerical Value | Units | Context of Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ | W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ | Total radiated power per unit area |
| Wien's displacement constant | $b$ | $2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | m·K | Peak emission wavelength |
| Speed of light | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ | m·s⁻¹ | Planck's law derivation |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s | Quantum of radiation |
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | J·K⁻¹ | Thermal energy scale |
| Human skin emissivity | $\varepsilon_{skin}$ | **0.98** | dimensionless | Critical calibration parameter |
| Normal core body temp | $T_{core}$ | **310.15 K (37 °C)** | K | Baseline reference |
| Mean skin temperature | $T_{skin}$ | **305.15–308.15 K (32–35 °C)** | K | Typical thermogram range |
| Medical IR window (LWIR) | $\lambda$ | **8–14 μm** | μm | Atmospheric transmission |
| Thermal resolution (clinical) | $\Delta T_{min}$ | **≤ 0.05 K (50 mK)** | K | Modern FPA detectors |
| Spatial resolution (clinical) | IFOV | **≤ 1.0 mrad** | mrad | Lens-detector combo |
| Clinical detection threshold | $\Delta T_{lesion}$ | **≥ 1.0–2.0 °C** | °C | Minimum asymmetry flag |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important number in the entire experiment is the **emissivity of human skin ≈ 0.98**. If the student forgets to set this value in the camera (defaults to 0.95 for matte surfaces), every temperature reading will be wrong by 1–2 °C — enough to cause a **false-negative** in anomaly detection.

---

### 1.4 System-Level Block View of a Biomedical IR Workstation

A complete IR-imaging experiment in the KTU lab comprises three cascaded stages: **(i) Acquisition**, **(ii) Pre-processing**, **(iii) Analysis & Interpretation**.

$$\text{Thermogram} \xrightarrow{\text{Acquire}} \text{Raw IR Frame} \xrightarrow{\text{Calibrate}} \text{Temperature Map} \xrightarrow{\text{Segment}} \text{ROI Mask} \xrightarrow{\text{Classify}} \text{Normal / Anomaly}$$

This pipeline will be expanded into a full Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics Foundation — Why the Body Emits IR

Every material with temperature $T > 0$ K contains charged particles in random thermal motion. Accelerating charges emit electromagnetic radiation. The spectral radiance $L_\lambda$ emitted by a *black body* at absolute temperature $T$ and wavelength $\lambda$ is given by **Planck's Law**:

$$L_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{2 h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h c \,/\, \lambda k_B T} - 1}$$

For a *real (grey) body* such as human skin, this is multiplied by the **emissivity** $\varepsilon$:

$$L_\lambda^{real}(\lambda, T) = \varepsilon \cdot L_\lambda(\lambda, T)$$

**Why this matters clinically:** At $T = 310$ K, Wien's law ($\lambda_{max} = b/T$) places the peak emission at $\approx 9.34$ μm — squarely inside the atmospheric 8–14 μm "transparency window" where the camera lens is designed to operate.

---

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Imaging Pipeline

The full experiment flows through six discrete sub-steps. Each step is described below in its engineering logic:

**Step 1 — Patient / Subject Preparation**
- Acclimatization: subject sits in a temperature-controlled room (typically **20–24 °C, ±1 °C**) for **15 minutes** so the skin reaches steady-state thermal equilibrium with the environment.
- Removal of clothing/jewellery over the Region of Interest (ROI) to avoid artifacts.
- Documentation of medical history, medications (vasodilators skew readings).

**Step 2 — Camera Calibration**
- Set **emissivity = 0.98** (skin).
- Set **reflected temperature** = ambient room temperature (measured with a separate thermocouple).
- Set **atmospheric temperature** and **relative humidity**.
- Set **distance** to subject (1–3 m typical for clinical protocols).
- Perform a **Non-Uniformity Correction (NUC)** — the camera's internal shutter snaps and the FPA re-balances.

**Step 3 — Image Acquisition**
- Trigger capture; FPA (typically uncooled microbolometer with **640 × 480** pixels) records IR photons.
- Frame is digitized at 14-bit resolution.
- Stored as a radiometric file (e.g., *.csv*, *.fff* — FLIR format) where each pixel holds an actual temperature, not just a grayscale value.

**Step 4 — Pre-processing (Image Enhancement)**
- **Dead-pixel correction:** replace non-responsive detector pixels by interpolating neighbours.
- **Spatial filtering:** Gaussian low-pass filter to suppress sensor noise (cutoff chosen at the Nyquist limit of the optics).
- **Histogram equalization:** stretches the dynamic range of the temperature histogram.
- **Registration:** if multiple frames are averaged, align them to remove patient micro-movements.

**Step 5 — Segmentation (Isolating the Anomalous Region)**

The four principal algorithms tested in the KTU lab are:

| # | Algorithm | Underlying Principle | When Used in IR Imaging |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Otsu's Thresholding** | Maximizes inter-class variance of the bimodal temperature histogram | Clear hot/cold bimodality (e.g., breast thermogram with one obvious hot lesion) |
| 2 | **Region Growing** | Seeds a pixel and grows a region while pixel temperature stays within a $\Delta T$ tolerance | Diffuse boundaries (e.g., inflammatory edema) |
| 3 | **Watershed Transform** | Treats the temperature gradient as a topographic surface; floods from minima | Multiple touching lesions (cluster of hotspots) |
| 4 | **Active Contour (Snake)** | Energy-minimizing curve fitting a temperature iso-line | Irregular, smooth boundaries (e.g., tumor margin) |
| 5 | **K-Means / Fuzzy C-Means Clustering** | Unsupervised grouping of pixels into $k$ thermal classes | Multi-zone thermal patterns (vascular map) |

**Step 6 — Anomaly Detection (Classification)**
Three clinical decision rules are applied to the segmented ROI:

- **Rule A — Absolute Threshold:** If $T_{ROI,max} > 37.5$ °C and patient is afebrile, flag as *hyperthermic anomaly*.
- **Rule B — Asymmetry Rule ( $\Delta T$-asymmetry):** Compute the contralateral (mirror-image) ROI. If $\vert T_{left} - T_{right} \vert > 0.7$ °C, flag as *asymmetric anomaly*. (Used in breast thermography.)
- **Rule C — Gradient Rule:** Compute the spatial temperature gradient $\nabla T$ around the ROI boundary. A gradient $> 1.0$ °C/cm is suspicious for an *active inflammatory or neoplastic process*.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are *high-yield* — they are the minimum set required to solve any numerical question on this experiment in the KTU End-Semester Examination.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stefan–Boltzmann (Total Power) | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | $E$ = emissive power, $T$ = absolute temperature | W·m⁻² | Total heat radiated per m² of skin |
| 2 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{max} = b \,/\, T$ | $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | m | Pick the optimal detector band |
| 3 | Planck's Spectral Radiance | $L_\lambda = \frac{2hc^{2}}{\lambda^{5}(e^{hc/\lambda k_B T}-1)}$ | $h, c, k_B$ fundamental constants | W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹·m⁻¹ | Spectral design of camera optics |
| 4 | Net Radiative Heat Loss | $Q_{net} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_{skin}^{4} - T_{amb}^{4})$ | $T_{amb}$ = ambient temp | W·m⁻² | Physiological heat balance |
| 5 | Radiometric Calibration | $T_{rad} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{S}{\varepsilon \sigma}}$ | $S$ = detector signal | K | Convert pixel value → temperature |
| 6 | Thermal Contrast (Anomaly) | $\Delta T = T_{lesion} - T_{normal}$ | $T_{lesion}$, $T_{normal}$ from ROI stats | K or °C | Primary detection metric |
| 7 | Asymmetry Index | $AI = \frac{\vert T_L - T_R \vert}{\max(T_L, T_R)} \times 100\%$ | $L, R$ = left/right ROI | % | Breast thermography flag |
| 8 | Spatial Gradient (Edge) | $\nabla T = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right)^{2}}$ | Discrete pixel differences | K/cm | Tumor margin sharpness |
| 9 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_{signal}}{\Delta T_{noise}}\right)$ | $T_{noise}$ = NETD of camera | dB | Detector quality |
| 10 | Noise-Equivalent Temperature Difference | $NETD = \frac{N_{rms}}{R}$ | $N_{rms}$ = RMS noise, $R$ = responsivity | mK | Smallest detectable $\Delta T$ |
| 11 | Modulation Transfer Function | $MTF(f) = e^{-(f/f_c)^{2}}$ | $f$ = spatial frequency, $f_c$ = cutoff | lp/mm | Spatial resolution limit |
| 12 | Instantaneous Field of View | $IFOV = \frac{p}{f_{lens}}$ | $p$ = pixel pitch, $f_{lens}$ = focal length | mrad | Pixel footprint on target |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical-bar rule:** In the markdown table above, every absolute value `|x|` has been written as `\vert x \vert` or replaced with `vert( x )` to avoid breaking the pipe-delimited table syntax. The same rule is applied throughout this document.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

The IR-imaging + segmentation + anomaly-detection pipeline is used in:

1. **Oncology** — Breast thermography, melanoma screening (surface temperature elevation due to angiogenesis).
2. **Rheumatology** — Detecting active synovitis in rheumatoid arthritis (joint hyperthermia).
3. **Vascular Medicine** — Raynaud's phenomenon, deep vein thrombosis (asymmetric limb temperature).
4. **Diabetic Foot Clinics** — Detecting pre-ulcerative inflammation (the "Charcot foot").
5. **Burn Units** — Mapping burn depth (1st vs 2nd degree via temperature deficit).
6. **Sports Medicine** — Muscle injury, tendinopathy assessment.
7. **Public Health** — Mass fever screening (airports during pandemics such as COVID-19, H1N1).
8. **Forensic Medicine** — Time-of-death estimation using body cooling curves.
9. **Pharmaceutical R&D** — Drug-induced vasodilation assessment in clinical trials.
10. **Space Medicine (ISRO / NASA)** — Thermoregulation studies in microgravity.

---

### 2.5 Clinical Validation & Imaging Protocol Standards

Two international standards are used in this experiment:

- **ASTM E1965 — Standard Practice for Thermography of the Human Body.**
- **Gautherie Protocol (1980, 1995) — Gold-standard breast thermography protocol:** 15-min acclimatization at 20 °C, $\pm 0.1$ °C stability, subject standing, hands on head, 5 standard views (frontal, left lateral, right lateral, left oblique, right oblique).

The KTU lab rubric awards 1 mark for citing the relevant standard in the viva.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Skip Mandate:** No step is omitted. Every algebraic transition is followed by an explanatory text line in plain English. Multi-line derivations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` blocks as required.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak Emission Wavelength for Human Skin

**Given:** Human skin surface temperature $T_{skin} = 305.15$ K (= 32 °C).
**Find:** Peak wavelength $\lambda_{max}$ of the emitted IR spectrum.

Wien's displacement law is derived by differentiating Planck's law with respect to $\lambda$ and setting the derivative to zero. The result is the transcendental equation:

$$5(1 - e^{-x}) = x$$

where $x = h c \,/\, (\lambda_{max} k_B T)$. The numerical solution is $x \approx 4.965$, leading to:

$$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

Solving for $\lambda_{max}$ at the skin temperature:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{max} &= \frac{b}{T_{skin}} \\
&= \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}{305.15 \text{ K}} \\
&= 9.497 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
&= 9.50 \; \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of every transition:**
- *Line 1* — Apply Wien's law in its rearranged form. The product $b = \lambda_{max} T$ is a constant of nature.
- *Line 2* — Substitute numerical values. Both numerator and denominator carry units of m·K, so units cancel correctly to meters.
- *Line 3* — Perform the division. The answer is in meters because we used SI units throughout.
- *Line 4* — Convert to micrometers (1 μm = 10⁻⁶ m) to match the standard LWIR band.

**Conclusion:** Human skin peaks at **9.5 μm**, which lies inside the 8–14 μm atmospheric window. This is *exactly why* clinical IR cameras are built with optics and microbolometer detectors sensitive to LWIR — they are physically matched to the human body's natural emission peak.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Radiative Power from a Human Forearm

**Given:** A human forearm has area $A = 0.06$ m², skin temperature $T_{skin} = 305$ K, ambient temperature $T_{amb} = 296$ K (= 23 °C), and skin emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.98$.
**Find:** (a) Total power radiated by the skin; (b) Net power lost by the skin to the environment (Stefan–Boltzmann net exchange).

**(a) Total power emitted by the skin:**

The Stefan–Boltzmann law gives the power per unit area:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{emit} &= \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T_{skin}^{4} \\
&= 0.98 \times (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) \times (305)^{4} \\
&= 0.98 \times (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) \times 8.653 \times 10^{9} \\
&= 0.98 \times 490.6 \\
&= 480.8 \text{ W·m}^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

Total power over the forearm area:

$$P_{emit} = E_{emit} \times A = 480.8 \times 0.06 = 28.85 \text{ W}$$

**Explanation of transitions:**
- *Line 1* — Stefan–Boltzmann for a grey body multiplies the black-body expression by $\varepsilon$.
- *Line 2* — Substitute $\sigma$, $T_{skin}$ (in Kelvin, NOT Celsius).
- *Line 3* — Compute $305^{4} = 305 \times 305 \times 305 \times 305 \approx 8.653 \times 10^{9}$.
- *Line 4* — Multiply $\sigma \times T^{4} = 490.6$ W·m⁻².
- *Line 5* — Multiply by emissivity 0.98 → 480.8 W·m⁻² (about 2% lower than a perfect black body).
- *Final* — Multiply by the forearm area 0.06 m² → **28.85 W**.

**(b) Net power lost to the environment:**

The skin also *receives* radiation from the room walls, so the net loss is the difference:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{net} &= \varepsilon \, \sigma \, \left( T_{skin}^{4} - T_{amb}^{4} \right) \\
&= 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times \left[ (305)^{4} - (296)^{4} \right] \\
&= 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times \left[ 8.653 \times 10^{9} - 7.687 \times 10^{9} \right] \\
&= 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.966 \times 10^{9} \\
&= 53.7 \text{ W·m}^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

For the whole forearm: $Q_{net,total} = 53.7 \times 0.06 = 3.22$ W.

**Conclusion:** The forearm loses only **3.22 W** by radiation, which is reasonable — the body uses blood-flow regulation (vasoconstriction) to keep radiative loss manageable. This derivation shows why a stable ambient temperature (±1 °C) is essential in the lab: a 1 K shift in $T_{amb}$ changes $Q_{net}$ by about 10%.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Radiometric Calibration Equation (from Detector Signal to Temperature)

**Given:** A microbolometer pixel produces a voltage $S$ that is proportional to incident IR power. With the responsivity $R = 1.2 \times 10^{4}$ V·W⁻¹, and an emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.98$, find the temperature that corresponds to $S = 0.42$ V.

The detector equation is:

$$S = R \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T_{rad}^{4}$$

Solving for $T_{rad}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{rad}^{4} &= \frac{S}{R \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma} \\
&= \frac{0.42}{(1.2 \times 10^{4}) \times 0.98 \times (5.670 \times 10^{-8})} \\
&= \frac{0.42}{6.668 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 630.0
\end{aligned}$$

Taking the fourth root:

$$T_{rad} = (630.0)^{1/4} = 5.012 \text{ (in } \sqrt[4]{\text{K}^{4}} \text{)}$$

But that is wrong dimensionally — let us re-evaluate. The numerics $S / (R \varepsilon \sigma)$ give $T^{4}$ in K⁴. So:

$$T_{rad} = (630.0)^{1/4} \approx 5.012 \; \text{K}$$

This is far below body temperature, so the signal value used (0.42 V) is too low for a clinical scenario. For $T = 310$ K, the required signal would be:

$$S = (1.2 \times 10^{4}) \times 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times (310)^{4} = 1.96 \text{ V}$$

**Conclusion:** The microbolometer output is of the order of a few volts, well within the 0–5 V range of the on-board ADC. This explains why the 14-bit ADC (range 0–10 V, resolution ≈ 0.6 mV) easily resolves sub-degree temperature differences.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Anomaly Detection Threshold (Asymmetry Rule)

**Given:** A breast thermogram yields the following statistics from the segmented left and right breast ROIs:
- Left ROI: $\overline{T_L} = 34.2$ °C, $T_{L,max} = 35.8$ °C.
- Right ROI: $\overline{T_R} = 33.1$ °C, $T_{R,max} = 33.9$ °C.
**Find:** Decide whether an anomaly is flagged, using both the absolute rule and the asymmetry rule.

**Absolute Rule (Rule A):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{max,L} &= T_{L,max} - T_{core} = 35.8 - 37.0 = -1.2 \; \text{°C (no fever, below core)} \\
\Delta T_{max,R} &= T_{R,max} - T_{core} = 33.9 - 37.0 = -3.1 \; \text{°C (no fever)}
\end{aligned}$$

Neither ROI exceeds 37.5 °C → **Rule A does not flag** (in the absence of systemic fever).

**Asymmetry Rule (Rule B):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{asym} &= \overline{T_L} - \overline{T_R} = 34.2 - 33.1 = 1.1 \; \text{°C}
\end{aligned}$$

The clinical threshold (Gautherie) is $\vert \Delta T_{asym} \vert > 0.7$ °C. Here $\vert 1.1 \vert > 0.7$ → **Rule B FLAGS an anomaly in the left breast.**

**Asymmetry Index (AI):**

$$AI = \frac{\vert 34.2 - 33.1 \vert}{\max(34.2, 33.1)} \times 100\% = \frac{1.1}{34.2} \times 100\% = 3.22\%$$

Any AI > 2% is considered suspicious; here **AI = 3.22%** strongly suggests an asymmetric, possibly neoplastic, process in the left breast.

**Conclusion:** The patient is referred for further investigation (mammography / ultrasound). The asymmetry-based thermography does not diagnose cancer — it triages patients into higher-risk pools.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Complete KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem:** A thermogram of a patient's knee is segmented using Otsu's method. The resulting hot ROI has a mean temperature $\overline{T}_{hot} = 36.8$ °C and the surrounding normal tissue ROI has $\overline{T}_{normal} = 34.5$ °C. The camera NETD is 50 mK and the lesion area is 4.2 cm². Compute: (a) Thermal contrast $\Delta T$, (b) SNR, (c) Whether the anomaly is detectable (use a 3σ detection criterion), (d) Stefan–Boltzmann power density difference.

**(a) Thermal contrast:**

$$\Delta T = \overline{T}_{hot} - \overline{T}_{normal} = 36.8 - 34.5 = 2.3 \; \text{°C}$$

**(b) Signal-to-Noise Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SNR &= \frac{\Delta T_{signal}}{\Delta T_{noise}} = \frac{2.3}{0.050} = 46 \\
SNR_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}(46) = 20 \times 1.663 = 33.25 \; \text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**(c) Detection criterion (3σ):**

The minimum detectable signal is $3 \times NETD = 3 \times 0.050 = 0.15$ °C.
We have $\Delta T = 2.3$ °C, and $2.3 \gg 0.15$ → **The anomaly is robustly detectable** (15.3× above the noise floor).

**(d) Power density difference:**

Using $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ with $\varepsilon = 0.98$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times \left[ (309.95)^{4} - (307.65)^{4} \right] \\
&= 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times \left[ 9.234 \times 10^{9} - 8.962 \times 10^{9} \right] \\
&= 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.272 \times 10^{9} \\
&= 15.1 \; \text{W·m}^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

Total power from the lesion area:

$$P_{lesion} = 15.1 \times (4.2 \times 10^{-4}) = 6.34 \; \text{mW}$$

**Conclusion:** The lesion radiates about 6.3 mW more than the equivalent area of normal tissue — a measurable, clinically meaningful thermal anomaly consistent with active synovitis (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis flare).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Using °C instead of K in Stefan–Boltzmann** — the formula $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ REQUIRES $T$ in **Kelvin**. Adding 273.15 is mandatory; forgetting it is the single biggest source of wrong numerical answers in board exams.
> 2. **Forgetting to set emissivity = 0.98** — camera defaults to 0.95. Students writing "I set the correct emissivity" without stating the number lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing segmentation with detection** — segmentation gives the *shape*; detection gives the *class label*. Examiners deduct 2 marks for conflating the two.
> 4. **Stating only one asymmetry threshold** — Gautherie uses **0.7 °C**, but many published papers use 0.5 °C or 1.0 °C. Always cite the protocol name *and* the numerical threshold.
> 5. **Skipping the acclimatization step** — the standard is 15 minutes, and the room must be $\pm 1$ °C stable. Writing "subject was asked to rest" without a number loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Plotting histograms without bin counts** — a histogram with 5 bins and no axis labels gets 0; with labels and counts it gets full marks.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**  
> *Define the term "thermal contrast" as used in infrared thermography of the human body. How is it used as a primary metric in anomaly detection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Thermal contrast is defined as the difference in surface temperature between a region of interest (ROI) suspected of pathology and an adjacent, anatomically symmetric, normal reference ROI:
$$\Delta T = T_{lesion} - T_{normal}$$
It is the *primary detection metric* because: (i) it is dimensionless with respect to the camera's absolute calibration (only *differences* matter, common-mode errors cancel); (ii) it is independent of the patient's core temperature, allowing inter-patient comparison; and (iii) clinical thresholds (e.g., $\Delta T > 2.0$ °C for inflammation, $> 1.0$ °C for breast asymmetry) are well-documented in the Gautherie protocol. **Mark split: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark clinical use.**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**  
> *State Stefan–Boltzmann law. Why is the emissivity of human skin (0.98) critical when calibrating an IR camera?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Stefan–Boltzmann law states that the total radiative power emitted per unit area of a black body is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature:
$$E = \sigma T^{4}, \quad \sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$$
For a real (grey) body, $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$. The emissivity of human skin is **0.98**, which means it behaves almost as a perfect black body in the LWIR band. If the camera is calibrated with the default $\varepsilon = 0.95$, the apparent temperature reading is *underestimated* by approximately 1 °C — sufficient to mask an inflammatory or neoplastic hotspot and produce a false-negative diagnosis. Hence $\varepsilon = 0.98$ must be set explicitly before every clinical acquisition. **Mark split: 1 mark statement + 1 mark formula + 1 mark clinical significance.**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe, with a labelled block diagram, the complete pipeline for *acquisition and analysis of infrared images* of the human body. Clearly state the purpose of each block.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The pipeline consists of **seven** sequential blocks:

1. **Subject preparation (1 mark):** Acclimatization for 15 minutes in a climate-controlled room at 20–24 °C ($\pm$ 1 °C), removal of clothing over the ROI, abstention from vasodilators (caffeine, nicotine) for 2 hours prior. The objective is to drive the skin into thermal steady-state so that measured gradients reflect *pathology*, not *environmental stress*.

2. **Camera calibration (1 mark):** Set $\varepsilon = 0.98$, $T_{reflected} = T_{amb}$ (measured with a separate thermocouple), relative humidity, and subject distance. Run the internal Non-Uniformity Correction (NUC) so that all pixels have identical dark-response offset.

3. **Image acquisition (1 mark):** Trigger the FPA. The microbolometer integrates IR photons over a 14-bit radiometric frame. Save the *raw* radiometric file (not just a screenshot) so post-hoc temperature recalibration is possible.

4. **Pre-processing (1 mark):** (i) Dead-pixel replacement; (ii) Gaussian low-pass filtering ($\sigma_{kernel} \approx 3$ pixels) to attenuate sensor noise; (iii) histogram equalization to stretch the temperature dynamic range; (iv) spatial registration if averaging multiple frames.

5. **Segmentation (1 mark):** Apply Otsu's method (for bimodal histograms), region growing (for diffuse boundaries), or watershed (for multiple touching lesions) to extract the binary ROI mask.

6. **Feature extraction (1 mark):** Compute ROI statistics — $T_{mean}$, $T_{max}$, $T_{min}$, standard deviation, and the spatial gradient $\nabla T$ at the ROI boundary.

7. **Anomaly classification (1 mark):** Apply the three clinical rules (absolute threshold 37.5 °C, asymmetry $\Delta T > 0.7$ °C, gradient $> 1.0$ °C/cm). The output class is one of {normal, hyperthermic, asymmetric, edge-sharp}, with a recommendation for follow-up imaging.

A properly drawn block diagram (as in Section 4.1 above) carries 1 of the 7 marks — examiners specifically allocate a mark for the *visual schematic*.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A patient's thermogram is segmented into a hot ROI and a normal ROI. The hot ROI has $\overline{T}_{hot} = 36.9$ °C, $T_{hot,max} = 37.8$ °C; the normal ROI has $\overline{T}_{normal} = 34.4$ °C, $T_{normal,max} = 35.1$ °C. The camera NETD is 60 mK. Using the *asymmetry-equivalent rule* (which compares $\Delta T$ of the two ROIs), determine: (i) the thermal contrast, (ii) the SNR in dB, (iii) whether the anomaly passes the 3σ detection criterion, (iv) the net radiative heat-flux density difference using Stefan–Boltzmann law with $\varepsilon = 0.98$. Comment on the clinical significance.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Thermal contrast [1 mark]:**
$$\Delta T = \overline{T}_{hot} - \overline{T}_{normal} = 36.9 - 34.4 = 2.5 \; \text{°C}$$

**(ii) SNR [2 marks]:**
$$\begin{aligned}
SNR &= \frac{\Delta T_{signal}}{NETD} = \frac{2.5}{0.060} = 41.67 \\
SNR_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}(41.67) = 20 \times 1.620 = 32.4 \; \text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) 3σ detection criterion [2 marks]:**
$$T_{min,detectable} = 3 \times NETD = 3 \times 0.060 = 0.18 \; \text{°C}$$
We have $\Delta T = 2.5$ °C, and $2.5 / 0.18 = 13.9$. The anomaly is detectable at **13.9σ**, i.e., a confidence level of essentially 100% (probability of false alarm $< 10^{-43}$).

**(iv) Net heat-flux density difference [2 marks]:**
With $T_{hot} = 310.05$ K and $T_{normal} = 307.55$ K:
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times \left[ (310.05)^{4} - (307.55)^{4} \right] \\
&= 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times \left[ 9.244 \times 10^{9} - 8.937 \times 10^{9} \right] \\
&= 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.307 \times 10^{9} \\
&= 17.06 \; \text{W·m}^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Clinical comment [implicit in the four numerical results above]:** A 2.5 °C thermal contrast with a 13.9σ detection confidence is a *strong* positive finding — consistent with active inflammation, infection, or a hyper-vascular neoplastic process. The patient should be referred for confirmatory imaging (mammography / ultrasound / MRI depending on the ROI). Total: 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 7 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Otsu's thresholding algorithm in the context of IR-thermogram segmentation. Why is it particularly well-suited to bimodal temperature histograms? Compare it with region-growing segmentation in terms of (i) computational cost, (ii) sensitivity to seed point, (iii) ability to handle overlapping lesions.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

Otsu's method is a *non-parametric, unsupervised* global thresholding technique. It exhaustively searches for the intensity (temperature) value $t$ that **maximizes the inter-class variance** $\sigma_B^{2}(t)$ between the two pixel populations ("cold background" and "hot foreground") [1 mark].

Mathematically, for a histogram with $N$ bins and probability $p_i$ for bin $i$, Otsu chooses:

$$t^{*} = \arg\max_{t} \; \sigma_B^{2}(t) = \arg\max_{t} \; \omega_0(t) \, \omega_1(t) \, \left[ \mu_0(t) - \mu_1(t) \right]^{2}$$

where $\omega_0, \omega_1$ are the class probabilities and $\mu_0, \mu_1$ are the class mean intensities.

**Why it is suited to bimodal histograms [2 marks]:** In a thermogram with one clear hot lesion against a uniform background, the temperature histogram is sharply bimodal — one peak at the skin baseline (≈ 33 °C) and another at the lesion (≈ 36 °C). Otsu's objective function peaks sharply between the two modes, and the global optimum is unique. The method is automatic — no manual threshold is needed.

**Comparison table [4 marks]:**

| Criterion | Otsu | Region Growing |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Computational cost | **Low** — single histogram pass, $O(N)$ | Higher — iterative neighbour checks, $O(N \cdot k)$ where $k$ = avg. region size |
| (ii) Seed sensitivity | **None** — fully automatic | **High** — wrong seed grows the wrong region; multiple seeds needed for multiple lesions |
| (iii) Overlapping lesions | **Poor** — single global threshold merges touching lesions into one blob | **Better** — different seeds for each lesion, but seeds must be manually picked |
| (iv) Bimodal histogram | **Optimal** | Sub-optimal — wastes effort on an already-easy problem |
| (v) Diffuse boundary | Sub-optimal (binary output) | **Optimal** — grows until the $\Delta T$ tolerance is exceeded |

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the *peak emission wavelength* and the *total emissive power* for a human palm at $T = 306$ K with area $A = 0.018$ m² and $\varepsilon = 0.98$. State one clinical observation derived from these numbers.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Peak emission wavelength [3 marks]:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{max} &= \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{306} \\
&= 9.471 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 9.47 \; \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$
This lies in the LWIR atmospheric window, confirming that the microbolometer detector is correctly spectrally matched [embedded 1 mark of explanation].

**Total emissive power [3 marks]:**
$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \varepsilon \sigma T^{4} = 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times (306)^{4} \\
&= 0.98 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.762 \times 10^{9} \\
&= 487.0 \; \text{W·m}^{-2} \\
P_{total} &= E \times A = 487.0 \times 0.018 = 8.77 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Clinical observation [1 mark]:** A palm radiates about **8.77 W**, which is comparable to a small LED light bulb. This is why a steady-state IR camera can detect palm thermal signatures even in a normally lit room — the signal is far above the camera's NETD (~ 50 mK) and well within the dynamic range. The clinical observation is that the palm is a *convenient reference ROI* for thermography protocols because of its high, easily-measured thermal emissive power and its symmetric counterpart (left palm vs right palm) on the contralateral side.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this in the last 10 minutes before the exam):**

- [ ] The human body at 37 °C (310 K) emits IR with peak wavelength ≈ **9.5 μm** (LWIR band).
- [ ] The LWIR camera band is **8–14 μm**, matched to the atmospheric transparency window.
- [ ] Emissivity of human skin = **0.98** — set this on the camera BEFORE acquisition.
- [ ] Acclimatization protocol: **15 minutes at 20–24 °C**, $\pm$ 1 °C.
- [ ] Two international standards to cite: **ASTM E1965** and **Gautherie Protocol**.
- [ ] Stefan–Boltzmann law: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ with $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ and $T$ **in Kelvin**.
- [ ] Wien's displacement law: $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K.
- [ ] Three clinical anomaly rules: **Absolute** (37.5 °C), **Asymmetry** (0.7 °C), **Gradient** (1.0 °C/cm).
- [ ] Otsu is best for **bimodal** histograms; region-growing for diffuse boundaries; watershed for multiple touching lesions.
- [ ] Thermal contrast $\Delta T$ is the *primary detection metric*, not absolute temperature.
- [ ] The microbolometer FPA is **uncooled** (cheaper, but NETD $\approx$ 50 mK) vs. cooled photon detectors (NETD $< 20$ mK, expensive).
- [ ] A radiometric file stores **temperature per pixel**, not just grayscale — always save the radiometric output, never just a screenshot.
- [ ] 3σ detection criterion: $T_{min} = 3 \times NETD$. Anything above this is statistically significant.
- [ ] Asymmetry Index $AI > 2\%$ flags a suspicious finding in breast thermography.
- [ ] The three pitfalls to AVOID: (i) using °C in Stefan–Boltzmann, (ii) forgetting the 0.98 emissivity, (iii) conflating segmentation with detection.
- [ ] Practical applications: oncology, rheumatology, vascular medicine, diabetic foot, burn assessment, fever screening, forensic pathology, sports medicine, space medicine.
- [ ] Image pipeline: Acquire → Calibrate → Pre-process → Segment → Extract features → Classify → Report.

**End of KTU Module Experiment 2 — IR Image Acquisition, Segmentation and Anomaly Detection Notes.**
